EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding slowly dwindled during early August 2019 with dry weather to start the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A continuation of flooding from July, the Big Sioux River near Bruce (gage site BRCS2) started the month at 0.53 feet above flood stage with flooding of agricultural land.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding slowly dwindled during early August 2019 with dry weather to start the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A continuation of flooding from July, the Big Sioux River at Sioux Falls I-90 (gage site SFLS2) crested at 0.28 feet above flood stage on August 3. Significant amounts of agricultural land were flooded north of Sioux Falls.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed along a weak cold front which pushed across southeast South Dakota during the evening of August 15.  With moderate instability of 1000-2000 J/kg and deep layer shear from 45 to 55 knots, a few stronger storms produced hail of nickel to walnut size.

A weak disturbance moving through during the overnight hours produced isolated elevated thunderstorms near the Missouri River.  With limited instability and effective shear, the storms were fairly brief and updrafts limited to marginally severe hail production.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail was from nickel to quarter size.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding slowly dwindled during early August 2019 with dry weather to start the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A continuation of flooding from July, the Big Sioux River near Brookings (gage site BRKS2) began the month at 1.49 feet above flood stage. Significant amounts of agricultural land remained flooded. A few rural roads near the gage site were also flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a weak disturbance acted to focus persistent lower to mid-level warm advection, persistent showers with only a rogue flash of lightning or two blossomed in the high moisture content air mass. Rainfall from 3 to 6 inches was common across a small area of southern Charles Mix and southwest Bon Homme counties, especially between 0500-0900CST.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy early morning rainfall resulted in ponding of 1 to 2 feet of water over parking lots in many areas on the west side of Wagner, including across the road from the fire station.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very narrow and persistent band of showers and isolated thunderstorms developed in the early morning hours. Rainfall rates of 1 to 3 inches per hour were common, with storm total rainfall through the lower James River valley anywhere between 2 and 6 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall resulted in flooded fields and ditches, as well as water across U.S. Highway 18.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusually strong and cold area of high pressure surged into the northern Plains in early March.  Winds were mainly a gusty 20 to 35 mph, and when coupled with temperatures from 10 below to 20 below zero, produced a long duration of wind chills colder than 20 below zero.  Coldest wind chills were recorded between 0400-0900 on March 3, reaching -51 near Oak Lake, -48 at Volga, -47 at Flandreau and White Lake, -46 at Colton and 5 miles south of Hendricks, -45 at Windom, Worthington and near Sioux Falls, and -44 at Huron, Brookings and Madison.  March 3 was one of the coldest March days on record for the area, with the coldest or second coldest March high temperatures on record at Huron, Sioux Falls, Mitchell and Sioux City.
Runoff from late winter and early spring precipitation maintained flooding along sections of the Missouri River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A continuation of flooding from March, higher dam releases and local runoff from occasional precipitation in late March and April maintained minor flooding of recreational lands, closing several boat ramps and flooding marinas around Chamberlain. The Missouri River at Chamberlain (gage site CBLS2) crested at 2.7 feet above flood stage on April 3.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snowmelt and rainfall flooding from late March and April along with above normal May rainfall resulted in continued flooding on the Big Sioux River near Castlewood. The Big Sioux River did fall below flood stage for a short period of time from May 15th to the 18th. The flooding then continued into June. The river reached its highest stage in late March at 12.6 feet where flood stage is 9 feet.

Earlier snowmelt and heavy rain flooding continued into early May on the Big Sioux River near Watertown. The river went below flood stage on May 5th and with heavy rainfall in late May, went above flood stage again for a short period of time from May 24th to May 25th.  The highest the river reached near Watertown was near 11 feet in late March where the flood stage is 8 feet.
Periodic heavy rainfall during June and routing of water downstream resulted in continued flooding of the James River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A continuation of flooding from May, the James River near Forestburg (gage site FORS2) crested 3.71 feet above flood stage on June 1. Many rural roads near the river were inundated, with continued flooding of significant amounts of agricultural land. Minor flooding continued at the end of the month.

A surface low pressure area moving across the central plains brought widespread snow to central and northeast South Dakota from the early morning hours of the 18th to the early morning hours of the 19th. Several locations received heavy snow of 6 to 10 inches. Travel was affected across the region. Some snowfall amounts include, 6 inches at Aberdeen, Miller, and Reliance; 7 inches at Watertown, Castlewood, and Claremont; 8 inches at Murdo and LaBolt; and 10 inches at Clark.

Following in behind a high wind/blizzard event, bitter cold arctic air along with northwest winds brought extreme wind chills to north central and northeast South Dakota.  The extreme wind chills began during the morning hours of the 29th and continued through the morning hours of the 31st.  Many record lows and record low maximums were set mainly on the 30th. Highs were in the teens below zero on the 30th across the east. Some of the record low temperatures were, 31 degrees below zero at Sisseton and Webster; 33 degrees below zero at Watertown; 37 degrees below zero at Eureka and Aberdeen; 38 degrees below zero at Summit; and 40 degrees below zero at Britton.||Most schools along with college campuses and businesses across the region had late starts or cancelled classes for two days. Mail service was also cancelled. Extreme wind chills from 35 degrees below to near 60 degrees below zero occurred. Some of the lowest wind chills include, 42 degrees below zero east of Danforth; 45 degrees below zero at Wallace; 47 degrees below zero at Aberdeen; 54 degrees below zero at Watertown; and 59 degrees below zero at Sisseton.

An intense clipper system followed by a powerful arctic front brought a trace to 3 inches of snow along with high winds to central and north central South Dakota. Northwest winds gusting to 60 to over 70 mph occurred during the late evening of the 27th and ended in the early morning hours of the 28th. These high winds brought the visibility down to less than a quarter mile at times in blowing snow mainly east of the Missouri River. Some of the highest wind gusts include, 61 mph at Lantry; 63 mph at Mobridge; 66 mph 10 miles south of Bullhead; 70 mph at Blunt; 71 mph at Pierre; and 72 mph 12 miles north of Vivian.

Following in behind a high wind/blizzard event, bitter cold arctic air along with northwest winds brought extreme wind chills to north central and northeast South Dakota.  The extreme wind chills began during the morning hours of the 29th and continued through the morning hours of the 31st.  Many record lows and record low maximums were set mainly on the 30th. Highs were in the teens below zero on the 30th across the east. Some of the record low temperatures were, 31 degrees below zero at Sisseton and Webster; 33 degrees below zero at Watertown; 37 degrees below zero at Eureka and Aberdeen; 38 degrees below zero at Summit; and 40 degrees below zero at Britton.||Most schools along with college campuses and businesses across the region had late starts or cancelled classes for two days. Mail service was also cancelled. Extreme wind chills from 35 degrees below to near 60 degrees below zero occurred. Some of the lowest wind chills include, 42 degrees below zero east of Danforth; 45 degrees below zero at Wallace; 47 degrees below zero at Aberdeen; 54 degrees below zero at Watertown; and 59 degrees below zero at Sisseton.

A winter storm brought snow to much of western South Dakota. The heaviest snowfall was across the northern Black Hills and the Spearfish area, where four to eight inches of accumulation was reported. Gusty winds produced some blowing and drifting snow.

A series of weak disturbances brought periods of light snow to the northern Black Hills over a two day period. Several inches of snow fell in many areas, with more than six inches across favored upslope areas.

An intense clipper system followed by a powerful arctic boundary moved across the region from northwest to southeast from the morning of the 27th to the morning of the 28th. Fresh snowfall of 1 to 5 inches on the 27th was followed by high winds gusting from 55 to over 70 mph which brought ground blizzard conditions from the evening into the early morning hours. Visibility was frequently down to zero in ground blizzard conditions. The winds and low visibility hit quickly catching many people off guard. Many people became stranded or went into the ditch and had to be rescued. There was a no travel advisory for the area.||The snowfall began between 10 am and noon and ended from 6 pm to 8 pm in the evening of the 27th before the high winds/blizzard conditions came in. Some of the highest wind gusts include, 68 mph at Aberdeen; 69 mph 8 miles west of Aberdeen; and 72 mph 13 miles east of Eureka. The ground blizzard was then followed by extreme wind chills from the 29th through the 31st.

Fresh snowfall of 1 to 5 inches on the 27th was followed by high winds gusting from 55 to over 70 mph which brought ground blizzard conditions from the evening into the early morning hours. Visibility was frequently down to zero in ground blizzard conditions. The winds and low visibility hit quickly catching many people off guard. Many people became stranded or went into the ditch and had to be rescued. There was a no travel advisory for the area.||The snowfall began between 10 am and noon and ended from 6 pm to 8 pm in the evening of the 27th before the high winds/blizzard conditions came in. Some of the highest wind gusts include, 68 mph at Aberdeen; 69 mph 8 miles west of Aberdeen; and 72 mph 13 miles east of Eureka. The ground blizzard was then followed by extreme wind chills from the 29th through the 31st.

A strong Canadian cold front moved through the Northern Plains during the afternoon and evening. Strong winds developed behind the front and lingered early the next morning. The strongest winds were across the northwest and west central South Dakota plains, where wind gusts around 70 mph were recorded.

A shallow northerly push of colder air strengthened heading through February 3. As a weak disturbance lifted northeast from the Rockies during the overnight hours, drying aloft on top of the shallow cold and moist layer along with weak lift resulted in widespread freezing drizzle. A glaze of ice accumulation ranging from a few hundredths to a tenth of an inch impeded travel during the evening and overnight hours.

Spurred by a period of excessive precipitation that saw from 5 to 10 inches of rainfall between September 10-12, smaller tributaries saw surges to major to record flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Firesteel Creek near Mount Vernon (gage site MTVS2) responded sharply to 5 to 9 inches of rainfall between September 10-12 (6.49 inches at White Lake, 5.68 inches near Stickney and 7.83 inches 5 miles west southwest of Mitchell) to reach the sixth highest crest on record at 7.17 feet above flood stage on September 13. Innumerable county and township roads were inundated and damaged, along with extensive agricultural flooding.

Extremely cold arctic air dominated the weather across central and northeast South Dakota from the late evening of the 2nd through the morning of the 3rd. Record lows in the teens below and 20s below zero were set across the region. Most of the record lows shattered the previous record lows by 5 to 10 degrees. In fact, Britton in northeast South Dakota fell to 29 degrees below zero breaking the old record of 19 degrees below zero.||The arctic cold along with north winds brought dangerously cold wind chills ranging from 35 below zero to near 55 below zero across the region. Some of the extreme wind chills included, 40 degrees below zero at Aberdeen; 42 degrees below zero at Pierre; 45 degrees below zero at Holabird; 46 degrees below zero at Barnard; 47 degrees below zero at Gettysburg; 49 degrees below zero at Britton and Eureka; and 53 degrees below zero south of Bullhead.

Much above normal winter snowfall and melt water/ice jams along with heavy rains in the middle of March brought flooding across parts of central and northeast South Dakota for late March. Rivers and creeks flooded across much of the area along with many fields and roads. The flooding damaged many of the roads and culverts across the region. Some structures were also flooded. Lake levels also rose threatening homes mainly in the east at Lake Kampeska near Watertown. Many counties issued emergency declarations for the flooding to include the mid-March snowstorm. South Dakota's governor also declared a state of emergency. Much of this flooding continued into early April as the snowmelt continued with the high water delaying planting.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding of several township and county roads occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong winter storm moved across the region, bringing heavy snow and blizzard conditions to much of western and south central South Dakota. Snow developed during the morning hours, with rain and snow changing to snow southeast of the Black Hills by the early afternoon. Northwest winds increased during the late morning and afternoon, with blizzard conditions developing across much of the plains. Snow ended the next morning, with winds gradually tapering off by late afternoon. Snowfall amounts were widely variable across the area, ranging from an inch or less over parts of Harding County to one to two feet in some portions of Haakon, Jackson, and Bennett Counties and portions of the northern Black Hills. Drifts of six feet and higher were reported where the heaviest snow fell on the plains. Much of Interstate 90 across western South Dakota were closed for more than 36 hours.

A historic late-season, multi-day winter storm developed rapidly across the central Plains on Wednesday, April 10. This system spiraled several periods of wintry precipitation through the region.  The leading precipitation on April 10 was a mix of rain, freezing rain and sleet from far eastern South Dakota through the Interstate 90 areas of southwest Minnesota and northwest Iowa, changing to snow to the north and west.  There were even a few elevated thunderstorms which occurred overnight on April 10-11 and produced some near severe hail, which actually occurred after some initial wintry precipitation.  Significant icing was centered around 30 miles either side of the Interstate 90 corridor, with as much as a third to three-quarters inch ice accumulation.||Heavy snowfall developed from the early morning of April 11 and continued into the early morning of April 12 across much of southeast South Dakota into northern portions of southwest Minnesota, which accumulated to 1 to 2 feet during the storm.  The most persistent snowfall occurred for areas west to north of Sioux Falls, but even there, a brief period of freezing rain or sleet occurred at some point ahead of the main upper trough passage.||The other aspect of this storm was the intense winds, which gusted at the peak from 40 to 60 mph, producing widespread blizzard conditions to southeast South Dakota and southwest Minnesota.  Considerable power outages occurred as transmission lines succumbed to the ice accumulation and wind.  At the peak, electric companies and cooperatives estimated as many as 25,000 customers were without power.  Many areas were without power for a day or two due to the intensity of the storm.  The strong winds lofted dust from the Texas/Oklahoma panhandles which caused some of the snowfall toward the latter portions of the event to fall with a brownish color.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blizzard conditions led to a shutdown of government offices and schools, and travel was not recommended due to the widespread whiteout conditions. Storm-total snowfall reached 10 to 15 inches, with 13.4 inches at Howard. With winds gusting at times to around 50 mph, snow drifts to several feet made many roads impassable. Ice accumulated to one to two tenths of an inch, with winds resulting in spotty power outages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The continuation of snowmelt from a much above normal snowfall winter combined with a historic heavy snow/blizzard in mid-April resulted in widespread flooding across central and northeast South Dakota. Countless roads along with thousands of acres of cropland were flooded throughout April. Impacts include damaged roads, culverts, and bridges, and livestock, homes, and businesses were affected. Delayed planting resulted across all of the region as well. Cattle and calves were stressed by the cold and wet pattern, as the mud and cold caused some sickness with the livestock. Flooded roads made it difficult for many farmers or ranchers to get to their fields or livestock. There was a water rescue near Frederick on April 7th as someone drove into a washed out road. The wet pattern along with the flooding continued into May, further delaying planting across the region.||All counties declared emergencies/disasters in March and April due to the widespread flooding and March blizzard.  South Dakota's governor declared a disaster for the state in March. This declaration was followed by a disaster declaration by the President of the United States. As a result, 24 of the 26 counties across central and northeast South Dakota were able to have access to public property damage assistance. Overall, damage estimates from the blizzards and floods for the state were at 43 million dollars.

Property damage costs include damage to public infrastructure and utilities.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Extremely cold arctic air dominated the weather across central and northeast South Dakota from the late evening of the 2nd through the morning of the 3rd. Mud Creek and its south branch along with Willow Creek in eastern Codington county had snow melt flooding at the end of March. The creeks rose to 1 to 2.5 feet above flood stage where the flood stages ranged from 4 to 10 feet on both creeks.

Much above normal winter snowfall and melt water/ice jams along with heavy rains in the middle of March brought flooding across parts of central and northeast South Dakota for late March. Rivers and creeks flooded across much of the area along with many fields and roads. The flooding damaged many of the roads and culverts across the region. Some structures were also flooded. Lake levels also rose threatening homes mainly in the east at Lake Kampeska near Watertown. Many counties issued emergency declarations for the flooding to include the mid-March snowstorm. South Dakota's governor also declared a state of emergency. Much of this flooding continued into early April as the snowmelt continued with the high water delaying planting.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowmelt brought flooded roads with many of them damaged along with culverts throughout the county. A travel advisory was issued for all county roads. There was flooding of homes near/in the cities of Miller and Saint Lawrence along with other rural homes throughout the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong winter storm moved across the region, bringing heavy snow and blizzard conditions to much of western and south central South Dakota. Snow developed during the morning hours, with rain and snow changing to snow southeast of the Black Hills by the early afternoon. Northwest winds increased during the late morning and afternoon, with blizzard conditions developing across much of the plains. Snow ended the next morning, with winds gradually tapering off by late afternoon. Snowfall amounts were widely variable across the area, ranging from an inch or less over parts of Harding County to one to two feet in some portions of Haakon, Jackson, and Bennett Counties and portions of the northern Black Hills. Drifts of six feet and higher were reported where the heaviest snow fell on the plains. Much of Interstate 90 across western South Dakota were closed for more than 36 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Much above normal snow pack melt water and ice jamming brought flooding on the the Grand, Moreau, and Bad rivers across Corson, Dewey, Jones, and Stanley counties at the end of March. The Grand River at Little Eagle rose to moderate flood stage, nearly 4 and half feet above the flood stage of 15 feet. The Moreau River near Whitehorse rose to major flood stage, almost 28 feet or 7 feet above flood stage. The Bad River near Fort Pierre rose just above the flood stage of 21 feet to 21.2 feet. The Bad River road was closed for a short time. Agricultural land along with several roads were flooded.

Drifts of six feet and higher were reported where the heaviest snow fell on the plains. Much of Interstate 90 across western South Dakota were closed for more than 36 hours.

A historic late-season, multi-day winter storm developed rapidly across the central Plains on Wednesday, April 10. This system spiraled several periods of wintry precipitation through the region.  The leading precipitation on April 10 was a mix of rain, freezing rain and sleet from far eastern South Dakota through the Interstate 90 areas of southwest Minnesota and northwest Iowa, changing to snow to the north and west.  There were even a few elevated thunderstorms which occurred overnight on April 10-11 and produced some near severe hail, which actually occurred after some initial wintry precipitation.  Significant icing was centered around 30 miles either side of the Interstate 90 corridor, with as much as a third to three-quarters inch ice accumulation.||Heavy snowfall developed from the early morning of April 11 and continued into the early morning of April 12 across much of southeast South Dakota into northern portions of southwest Minnesota, which accumulated to 1 to 2 feet during the storm.  The most persistent snowfall occurred for areas west to north of Sioux Falls, but even there, a brief period of freezing rain or sleet occurred at some point ahead of the main upper trough passage.||The other aspect of this storm was the intense winds, which gusted at the peak from 40 to 60 mph, producing widespread blizzard conditions to southeast South Dakota and southwest Minnesota.  Considerable power outages occurred as transmission lines succumbed to the ice accumulation and wind.  At the peak, electric companies and cooperatives estimated as many as 25,000 customers were without power.  Many areas were without power for a day or two due to the intensity of the storm.  The strong winds lofted dust from the Texas/Oklahoma panhandles which caused some of the snowfall toward the latter portions of the event to fall with a brownish color.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blizzard conditions led to a shutdown of government offices and schools, and travel was not recommended due to the widespread whiteout conditions. Interstate 90 was closed from late on April 10 through noon on April 12. Ice accumulation of one to two tenths of an inch occurred prior to snowfall. Storm-total snowfall from 10 to 17 inches occurred, with 10.8 inches at Chamberlain, 12.0 inches at Pukwana and 16.3 inches 8 miles south southwest of Chamberlain. With winds gusting at times to around 50 mph, many areas had snow drifts of several feet, making roads impassable. Spotty power outages occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The continuation of snowmelt from a much above normal snowfall winter combined with a historic heavy snow/blizzard in mid-April resulted in widespread flooding across central and northeast South Dakota. Countless roads along with thousands of acres of cropland were flooded throughout April. Impacts include damaged roads, culverts, and bridges, and livestock, homes, and businesses were affected. Delayed planting resulted across all of the region as well. Cattle and calves were stressed by the cold and wet pattern, as the mud and cold caused some sickness with the livestock. Flooded roads made it difficult for many farmers or ranchers to get to their fields or livestock. There was a water rescue near Frederick on April 7th as someone drove into a washed out road. The wet pattern along with the flooding continued into May, further delaying planting across the region.||All counties declared emergencies/disasters in March and April due to the widespread flooding and March blizzard.  South Dakota's governor declared a disaster for the state in March. This declaration was followed by a disaster declaration by the President of the United States. As a result, 24 of the 26 counties across central and northeast South Dakota were able to have access to public property damage assistance. Overall, damage estimates from the blizzards and floods for the state were at 43 million dollars.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Much above normal snow pack melt water and ice jamming brought flooding on the the Grand, Moreau, and Bad rivers across Corson, Dewey, Jones, and Stanley counties at the end of March. The Grand River at Little Eagle rose to moderate flood stage, nearly 4 and half feet above the flood stage of 15 feet. The Moreau River near Whitehorse rose to major flood stage, almost 28 feet or 7 feet above flood stage. The Bad River near Fort Pierre rose just above the flood stage of 21 feet to 21.2 feet. The Bad River road was closed for a short time. Agricultural land along with several roads were flooded.

Much above normal snow melt water and ice jamming brought flooding on Turtle Creek and the James River near Redfield at the end of March. The James River rose to nearly 2 feet above the flood stage of 20 feet while Turtle Creek at Redfield rose to major flood stage of 15.3 feet or 8.3 feet above flood stage.  Agricultural land along with some roads were flooded. Some farms in the southwest part of the county were now islands from flooding on the Turtle Creek. The flooding continued into April.

Much above normal winter snowfall and melt water/ice jams along with heavy rains in the middle of March brought flooding across parts of central and northeast South Dakota for late March. Rivers and creeks flooded across much of the area along with many fields and roads. The flooding damaged many of the roads and culverts across the region. Some structures were also flooded. Lake levels also rose threatening homes mainly in the east at Lake Kampeska near Watertown. Many counties issued emergency declarations for the flooding to include the mid-March snowstorm. South Dakota's governor also declared a state of emergency. Much of this flooding continued into early April as the snowmelt continued with the high water delaying planting.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowmelt flooded several roads throughout Hamlin county especially along the Big Sioux River.

Minor flooding occurred along the Cheyenne River from upstream of Wasta to Lake Oahe due to releases from Angostura Dam, snowmelt, and ice jams. On March 15, releases began from Angostura Dam through the radial gates. With ice still in the river, the water began cutting away at the ice, causing large chunks of ice to make their way downstream. A large ice jam formed just downstream from the gage near Wasta. As water backed up behind the jam, minor flooding was observed. The ice jams continued to form and release all the way to Lake Oahe until the ice was completely cleared from the river.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The peak gage height at the Cheyenne River at Wasta reached a preliminary height of 13.98 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Major flooding occurred along the White River west of Oacoma.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large ice jam formed along the White River around the SD Highway 47 bridge. Water backed up behind the ice, causing flooding of hundreds of acres of agricultural land. The high water inundated several outbuildings and approached a home along Highway 47. The river crested just below 25 feet on March 19. With continued snowmelt, additional rises were recorded, resulting in a second crest of 20.5 feet and a flow around 37,900 cfs on March 25, which was the third highest flow of record at this location.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Flooding occurred along the White River and its tributaries from the Nebraska state line to Mellette County. The worst flooding was along the river over Oglala Lakota County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ice jams, combined with runoff from melted snow, caused minor to moderate flooding along the White River and other small creeks in Jackson County. Minor flooding occurred near Interior from March 22 through March 25. Further downstream, south of Kadoka, moderate flooding occurred with widespread flooding of agricultural land and outbuildings. On March 25, the high flows eroded away the river bank where the stream gage south of Kadoka was located. Based on high water marks, the USGS determined the river crested at 15.34 feet at the old location and 16.72 feet at the new upstream location. This would be the fourth highest river level at this location and the highest river level since 1982. Additional flooding occurred around the Long Valley and Wanblee areas, including along Fish Creek Road, where a county bridge was destroyed by flood waters.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The continuation of snowmelt from a much above normal snowfall winter combined with a historic heavy snow/blizzard in mid-April resulted in widespread flooding across central and northeast South Dakota. Countless roads along with thousands of acres of cropland were flooded throughout April. Impacts include damaged roads, culverts, and bridges, and livestock, homes, and businesses were affected. Delayed planting resulted across all of the region as well. Cattle and calves were stressed by the cold and wet pattern, as the mud and cold caused some sickness with the livestock. Flooded roads made it difficult for many farmers or ranchers to get to their fields or livestock. There was a water rescue near Frederick on April 7th as someone drove into a washed out road. The wet pattern along with the flooding continued into May, further delaying planting across the region.||All counties declared emergencies/disasters in March and April due to the widespread flooding and March blizzard.  South Dakota's governor declared a disaster for the state in March. This declaration was followed by a disaster declaration by the President of the United States. As a result, 24 of the 26 counties across central and northeast South Dakota were able to have access to public property damage assistance. Overall, damage estimates from the blizzards and floods for the state were at 43 million dollars.

A historic late-season, multi-day winter storm developed rapidly across the central Plains on Wednesday, April 10. This system spiraled several periods of wintry precipitation through the region.  The leading precipitation on April 10 was a mix of rain, freezing rain and sleet from far eastern South Dakota through the Interstate 90 areas of southwest Minnesota and northwest Iowa, changing to snow to the north and west.  There were even a few elevated thunderstorms which occurred overnight on April 10-11 and produced some near severe hail, which actually occurred after some initial wintry precipitation.  Significant icing was centered around 30 miles either side of the Interstate 90 corridor, with as much as a third to three-quarters inch ice accumulation.||Heavy snowfall developed from the early morning of April 11 and continued into the early morning of April 12 across much of southeast South Dakota into northern portions of southwest Minnesota, which accumulated to 1 to 2 feet during the storm.  The most persistent snowfall occurred for areas west to north of Sioux Falls, but even there, a brief period of freezing rain or sleet occurred at some point ahead of the main upper trough passage.||The other aspect of this storm was the intense winds, which gusted at the peak from 40 to 60 mph, producing widespread blizzard conditions to southeast South Dakota and southwest Minnesota.  Considerable power outages occurred as transmission lines succumbed to the ice accumulation and wind.  At the peak, electric companies and cooperatives estimated as many as 25,000 customers were without power.  Many areas were without power for a day or two due to the intensity of the storm.  The strong winds lofted dust from the Texas/Oklahoma panhandles which caused some of the snowfall toward the latter portions of the event to fall with a brownish color.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blizzard conditions led to a shutdown of government offices and schools, and travel was not recommended due to the widespread whiteout conditions.  Almost unprecedented amounts of storm-total snowfall occurred, with amounts of 18.0 inches at Huron, 25.0 inches 2 miles southeast of Wessington (second greatest 3-day snowfall total on record) and 29.8 inches near Hitchcock.  With winds gusting up to 51 mph at Huron, many areas had snow drifts of 5 to 10 feet, making roads impassable. Spotty power outages occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Ice jams, combined with runoff from snow melt, caused flooding along the White River throughout southern Lyman county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large ice jam formed along the White River around the Highway 47 Bridge. Water backed up behind the ice, causing hundreds of acres of agricultural land to be flooded. The high water inundated several outbuildings and neared a home along Highway 47.  The river crested just shy of 25 feet on March 19th. With the continued snow melt, additional rises were recorded, resulting in a second crest of 20.5 feet and a flow of 37,900 cfs on March 25th. This was the 3rd highest flow on record at this location.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Ice jams, combined with runoff from snow melt, caused flooding along the White River across southern Jones county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ice jams, combined with runoff from snow melt, caused moderate flooding along the White River. On March 22nd, the river forced the closure of Highway 83. About a 3/4 mile stretch of Highway 83 was inundated, due to water backing up behind and ice jam downstream. On March 23rd, a record flow of 21,700 cfs was recorded at the site. Records for this location go back 20 years.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong surface low pressure area moving across the central plains brought widespread heavy snow to north central and northeast South Dakota. Snowfall amounts across the region ranged from 6 to 12 inches which significantly disrupted travel. Some snowfall amounts included, 6 inches at Turton, Bowdle, Gettysburg, Faulkton, Ipswich, and Watertown; 7 inches at Ipswich and Redfield; 8 inches at Summit, Clear Lake, 2 miles northeast of Mina, and 4 miles west of Sisseton; 9 inches at Britton, Mobridge, and Aberdeen; 10 inches at Herreid, Milbank, 6 miles east of Timber Lake, Eureka, and Pollock; 11 inches 8 miles north of Promise, and 7 miles northeast of Leola; and 12 inches 6 miles northeast of Mahto.

A strong storm system moved across the region, bringing areas of heavy snow and gusty winds. The highest snowfall totals were across far northwestern South Dakota and parts of southwestern South Dakota where heavier bands of snow developed. Gusty northwest winds developed behind the system, producing considerable blowing and drifting snow across the plains. Numerous accidents forced the closure of Interstate 90 east of Rapid City for a time late in the day until winds decreased in the evening.

Flooding occurred along the White River and its tributaries from the Nebraska state line to Mellette County. The worst flooding was along the river over Oglala Lakota County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ice jams, combined with runoff from melting snow, caused moderate flooding along the White River. On March 22, the rising river forced the closure of US Highway 83. About a three-quarter mile stretch of Highway 83 was inundated when water backed up behind an ice jam downstream. On March 23, a record flow of 21,700 cfs was recorded at the site. This location has a period of record about 20 years.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Flooding occurred along the White River and its tributaries from the Nebraska state line to Mellette County. The worst flooding was along the river over Oglala Lakota County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate to major flooding occurred along the White River in Oglala Lakota County. Runoff from melting snow into the White River caused river ice to break up and jam. Additional flooding also occurred along White Clay Creek upstream and downstream from Oglala Dam due to the snowmelt.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding occurred along the Little Missouri River due to snowmelt and ice jams.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ice jams, combined with runoff from melting snow, caused minor flooding of agricultural land along the Little Missouri River. The river crested about 4.5 feet above flood stage at the SD Highway 20 bridge near Camp Crook on March 25. The resulting flow of 8500 cfs was the sixth highest discharge measurement at this site in 65 years.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moderate flooding occurred along parts of the Moreau River due to snowmelt and ice jams.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ice jams in the Moreau River, combined with runoff from melting snow, caused moderate flooding along the Moreau River. The river crested about four feet above flood stage at the SD Highway 73 bridge, with a resulting flow of 19,400 cfs. Besides flooding agricultural land along the river, flooded outbuildings and corrals were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Runoff from snowmelt and ice jams caused flooding along many creeks and rivers across south central South Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from melting snow caused major flooding, which washed out numerous roads and flooded a few structures. The Little White River compromised roads in four locations. One section of road was washed completely away for a quarter of a mile, while another spot was washed out nine feet deep and 15 feet wide. The Little White River near Martin recorded its second highest flow, while near Vetal it tied its record crest at 12.53 feet and 3540 cfs.

Runoff from snowmelt and ice jams caused flooding along many creeks and rivers across south central South Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Melting snow, combined with frozen ground, caused minor to moderate overland flooding. Water from the failed Hidden Timber Dam along Antelope Creek flooded several roads along Antelope Creek and the Keya Paha River. A rise of around one to two feet was noticed in the gage readings at Keyapaha. The runoff easily allowed for record levels on the Keya Paha River near Keyapaha, with flows further downstream near Wewela being the second highest on record.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Runoff from snowmelt and ice jams caused flooding along many creeks and rivers across south central South Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: During the early morning of March 23, Hidden Timber Dam along Antelope Creek failed, most likely from being overtopped by the excessive runoff from melted snow on frozen ground. As the water made its way downstream, it flooded several roads along Antelope Creek and the Keya Paha River.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Runoff from snowmelt and ice jams caused flooding along many creeks and rivers across south central South Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from melting snow, combined with frozen culverts, caused flooding which covered numerous roads. Several areas of high water were reported by the Rosebud Sioux Tribe, including 285th Street east of Prairie Hills and BIA 7 near Parmalee. Flooding along the Little White River and its tributaries covered several sections of BIA 5.

The continuation of snowmelt from a much above normal snowfall winter combined with a historic heavy snow/blizzard in mid-April resulted in widespread flooding across central and northeast South Dakota. Countless roads along with thousands of acres of cropland were flooded throughout April. Impacts include damaged roads, culverts, and bridges, and livestock, homes, and businesses were affected. Delayed planting resulted across all of the region as well. Cattle and calves were stressed by the cold and wet pattern, as the mud and cold caused some sickness with the livestock. Flooded roads made it difficult for many farmers or ranchers to get to their fields or livestock. There was a water rescue near Frederick on April 7th as someone drove into a washed out road. The wet pattern along with the flooding continued into May, further delaying planting across the region.||All counties declared emergencies/disasters in March and April due to the widespread flooding and March blizzard.  South Dakota's governor declared a disaster for the state in March. This declaration was followed by a disaster declaration by the President of the United States. As a result, 24 of the 26 counties across central and northeast South Dakota were able to have access to public property damage assistance. Overall, damage estimates from the blizzards and floods for the state were at 43 million dollars.

A historic late-season, multi-day winter storm developed rapidly across the central Plains on Wednesday, April 10. This system spiraled several periods of wintry precipitation through the region.  The leading precipitation on April 10 was a mix of rain, freezing rain and sleet from far eastern South Dakota through the Interstate 90 areas of southwest Minnesota and northwest Iowa, changing to snow to the north and west.  There were even a few elevated thunderstorms which occurred overnight on April 10-11 and produced some near severe hail, which actually occurred after some initial wintry precipitation.  Significant icing was centered around 30 miles either side of the Interstate 90 corridor, with as much as a third to three-quarters inch ice accumulation.||Heavy snowfall developed from the early morning of April 11 and continued into the early morning of April 12 across much of southeast South Dakota into northern portions of southwest Minnesota, which accumulated to 1 to 2 feet during the storm.  The most persistent snowfall occurred for areas west to north of Sioux Falls, but even there, a brief period of freezing rain or sleet occurred at some point ahead of the main upper trough passage.||The other aspect of this storm was the intense winds, which gusted at the peak from 40 to 60 mph, producing widespread blizzard conditions to southeast South Dakota and southwest Minnesota.  Considerable power outages occurred as transmission lines succumbed to the ice accumulation and wind.  At the peak, electric companies and cooperatives estimated as many as 25,000 customers were without power.  Many areas were without power for a day or two due to the intensity of the storm.  The strong winds lofted dust from the Texas/Oklahoma panhandles which caused some of the snowfall toward the latter portions of the event to fall with a brownish color.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blizzard conditions led to a shutdown of government offices and schools, and travel was not recommended due to the widespread whiteout conditions. Interstate 29 was closed from the evening of April 10 through noon on April 12. Storm-total snowfall from 4 to 8 inches occurred, with 8.0 inches 11 miles north of Colman and 5.4 inches at Flandreau. With winds gusting at times from 40 to 50 mph, many areas had snow drifts of several feet, making roads impassable. Ice accumulation of one-quarter to one-half inch occurred, resulting in scattered power outages as wind increased. Property damage costs include damage to utilities.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Extremely cold arctic air dominated the weather across central and northeast South Dakota from the late evening of the 2nd through the morning of the 3rd. Record lows in the teens below and 20s below zero were set across the region. Most of the record lows shattered the previous record lows by 5 to 10 degrees. In fact, Britton in northeast South Dakota fell to 29 degrees below zero breaking the old record of 19 degrees below zero.||The arctic cold along with north winds brought dangerously cold wind chills ranging from 35 below zero to near 55 below zero across the region. Some of the extreme wind chills included, 40 degrees below zero at Aberdeen; 42 degrees below zero at Pierre; 45 degrees below zero at Holabird; 46 degrees below zero at Barnard; 47 degrees below zero at Gettysburg; 49 degrees below zero at Britton and Eureka; and 53 degrees below zero south of Bullhead.

A strong surface low pressure area moving across the central plains brought widespread heavy snow to north central and northeast South Dakota. Snowfall amounts across the region ranged from 6 to 12 inches which significantly disrupted travel. Some snowfall amounts included, 6 inches at Turton, Bowdle, Gettysburg, Faulkton, Ipswich, and Watertown; 7 inches at Ipswich and Redfield; 8 inches at Summit, Clear Lake, 2 miles northeast of Mina, and 4 miles west of Sisseton; 9 inches at Britton, Mobridge, and Aberdeen; 10 inches at Herreid, Milbank, 6 miles east of Timber Lake, Eureka, and Pollock; 11 inches 8 miles north of Promise, and 7 miles northeast of Leola; and 12 inches 6 miles northeast of Mahto.

A very strong winter storm moved across the region, bringing heavy snow and blizzard conditions to much of western and south central South Dakota. Snow developed during the morning hours, with rain and snow changing to snow southeast of the Black Hills by the early afternoon. Northwest winds increased during the late morning and afternoon, with blizzard conditions developing across much of the plains. Snow ended the next morning, with winds gradually tapering off by late afternoon. Snowfall amounts were widely variable across the area, ranging from an inch or less over parts of Harding County to one to two feet in some portions of Haakon, Jackson, and Bennett Counties and portions of the northern Black Hills. Drifts of six feet and higher were reported where the heaviest snow fell on the plains. Much of Interstate 90 across western South Dakota were closed for more than 36 hours.

In fact, Britton in northeast South Dakota fell to 29 degrees below zero breaking the old record of 19 degrees below zero.||The arctic cold along with north winds brought dangerously cold wind chills ranging from 35 below zero to near 55 below zero across the region. Some of the extreme wind chills included, 40 degrees below zero at Aberdeen; 42 degrees below zero at Pierre; 45 degrees below zero at Holabird; 46 degrees below zero at Barnard; 47 degrees below zero at Gettysburg; 49 degrees below zero at Britton and Eureka; and 53 degrees below zero south of Bullhead.

A strong surface low pressure area moving across the central plains brought widespread heavy snow to north central and northeast South Dakota. Snowfall amounts across the region ranged from 6 to 12 inches which significantly disrupted travel. Some snowfall amounts included, 6 inches at Turton, Bowdle, Gettysburg, Faulkton, Ipswich, and Watertown; 7 inches at Ipswich and Redfield; 8 inches at Summit, Clear Lake, 2 miles northeast of Mina, and 4 miles west of Sisseton; 9 inches at Britton, Mobridge, and Aberdeen; 10 inches at Herreid, Milbank, 6 miles east of Timber Lake, Eureka, and Pollock; 11 inches 8 miles north of Promise, and 7 miles northeast of Leola; and 12 inches 6 miles northeast of Mahto.

A record breaking surface low pressure area moved across the central plains and brought rain, freezing rain, sleet, heavy snow and blizzard conditions to most all of central and northeast South Dakota. Heavy rain of 1 to over 2 inches occurred across the east and southeast part of the region in the Webster, Clark, Milbank, Clear Lake, and Watertown areas before the precipitation changed over to snow. In between the rain and snow, a band of freezing rain and sleet occurred. Ice accumulation up to quarter to a half inch combined with high winds brought down some power lines and poles bringing many power outages along with bringing treacherous travel.  Nearly 1,500 people were without power for a few days mainly for Hand, Spink, Brown, Day, Marshall, and Roberts counties. ||The heavy snow and strong winds also brought some cattle losses across the region along with some damage to buildings.  Interstate 90 was closed along with many other roads across the region. Nearly all schools were closed. The State B Basketball Tournament in Aberdeen was also affected by the storm with a delay in the start time. Emergency declarations were issued for many counties for the hazardous travel conditions and impassable roads along with livestock losses and structure damage. The declarations included subsequent flooding at the end of March. Agricultural producers were eligible for loans from the USDA who incurred losses from the blizzard.||Some of the snowfall amounts include, 6 inches at Faulkton; 7 inches at Miller and Mclaughlin; 8 inches at Pollock and Roscoe; 10 inches at Ipswich, Pierre, Selby, Artas, Eureka, and 5 miles northwest of Mission Ridge. Locations with a foot of more include, 12 inches 19 miles north of Highmore; 13 inches at Bowdle, 13 miles west of Leola, and 9 miles south of Long Lake; 14 inches at Murdo and 4 miles northwest of Elm Lake; 15 inches at Ree Heights; and 16 inches 4 miles northwest of Onida and Draper. ||The heavy rainfall amounts east and southeast in the region include, 1.50 inches at Webster; 1.54 inches at Milbank and Clark, 1.91 inches at Watertown, and 2.43 inches at Clear Lake.  This rain added water to an already deep and high water content snow pack across the region which would turn into flooding by the end of March.||The highest wind gusts include, 49 mph at Watertown; 58 mph at Mobridge; 60 mph at Pierre and Aberdeen; 69 mph 10 miles west of Long Lake; and 70 mph 13 miles north of Vivian. The high winds and heavy snow created 5 to 10 foot drifts.

A very strong winter storm moved across the region, bringing heavy snow and blizzard conditions to much of western and south central South Dakota. Snow developed during the morning hours, with rain and snow changing to snow southeast of the Black Hills by the early afternoon. Northwest winds increased during the late morning and afternoon, with blizzard conditions developing across much of the plains. Snow ended the next morning, with winds gradually tapering off by late afternoon. Snowfall amounts were widely variable across the area, ranging from an inch or less over parts of Harding County to one to two feet in some portions of Haakon, Jackson, and Bennett Counties and portions of the northern Black Hills. Drifts of six feet and higher were reported where the heaviest snow fell on the plains. Much of Interstate 90 across western South Dakota were closed for more than 36 hours.

A historic late-season, multi-day winter storm developed rapidly across the central Plains on Wednesday, April 10. This system spiraled several periods of wintry precipitation through the region.  The leading precipitation on April 10 was a mix of rain, freezing rain and sleet from far eastern South Dakota through the Interstate 90 areas of southwest Minnesota and northwest Iowa, changing to snow to the north and west.  There were even a few elevated thunderstorms which occurred overnight on April 10-11 and produced some near severe hail, which actually occurred after some initial wintry precipitation.  Significant icing was centered around 30 miles either side of the Interstate 90 corridor, with as much as a third to three-quarters inch ice accumulation.||Heavy snowfall developed from the early morning of April 11 and continued into the early morning of April 12 across much of southeast South Dakota into northern portions of southwest Minnesota, which accumulated to 1 to 2 feet during the storm.  The most persistent snowfall occurred for areas west to north of Sioux Falls, but even there, a brief period of freezing rain or sleet occurred at some point ahead of the main upper trough passage.||The other aspect of this storm was the intense winds, which gusted at the peak from 40 to 60 mph, producing widespread blizzard conditions to southeast South Dakota and southwest Minnesota.  Considerable power outages occurred as transmission lines succumbed to the ice accumulation and wind.  At the peak, electric companies and cooperatives estimated as many as 25,000 customers were without power.  Many areas were without power for a day or two due to the intensity of the storm.  The strong winds lofted dust from the Texas/Oklahoma panhandles which caused some of the snowfall toward the latter portions of the event to fall with a brownish color.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blizzard conditions led to a shutdown of government offices and schools, and travel was not recommended due to the widespread whiteout conditions. Storm-total snowfall from 8 to 16 inches occurred, with 14.0 inches at Parkston and 10.0 inches at Menno and 11 miles south of Emery. With winds gusting at times over 50 mph, many areas had snow drifts of several feet, making roads impassable. Ice accumulation of one to two tenths of an inch occurred, resulting in spotty power outages as wind increased. Power was lost for numerous hours for the communities and areas surrounding Freeman and Menno.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The continuation of snowmelt from a much above normal snowfall winter combined with a historic heavy snow/blizzard in mid-April resulted in widespread flooding across central and northeast South Dakota. Countless roads along with thousands of acres of cropland were flooded throughout April. Impacts include damaged roads, culverts, and bridges, and livestock, homes, and businesses were affected. Delayed planting resulted across all of the region as well. Cattle and calves were stressed by the cold and wet pattern, as the mud and cold caused some sickness with the livestock. Flooded roads made it difficult for many farmers or ranchers to get to their fields or livestock. There was a water rescue near Frederick on April 7th as someone drove into a washed out road. The wet pattern along with the flooding continued into May, further delaying planting across the region.||All counties declared emergencies/disasters in March and April due to the widespread flooding and March blizzard.  South Dakota's governor declared a disaster for the state in March. This declaration was followed by a disaster declaration by the President of the United States. As a result, 24 of the 26 counties across central and northeast South Dakota were able to have access to public property damage assistance. Overall, damage estimates from the blizzards and floods for the state were at 43 million dollars.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blizzard conditions led to a shutdown of government offices and schools, and travel was not recommended due to the widespread whiteout conditions. Interstate 90 was closed from late on April 10 through noon on April 12. Storm-total snowfall from 7 to 14 inches occurred, with 14.0 inches at Bridgewater, the highest 3-day April snowfall on record. Winds gusted at times from 50 to 60 mph, and many areas had snow drifts of several feet, making roads impassable. Ice accumulation of one-quarter to one-half inch occurred prior to snowfall, and numerous power outages occurred as wind increased.  Power was lost for the communities of Bridgewater, Canistota, Montrose and Salem, as well as surrounding areas, with some customers without power for more than two days. Property damage costs include damage to utilities.

Record lows in the teens below and 20s below zero were set across the region. Most of the record lows shattered the previous record lows by 5 to 10 degrees. In fact, Britton in northeast South Dakota fell to 29 degrees below zero breaking the old record of 19 degrees below zero.||The arctic cold along with north winds brought dangerously cold wind chills ranging from 35 below zero to near 55 below zero across the region. Some of the extreme wind chills included, 40 degrees below zero at Aberdeen; 42 degrees below zero at Pierre; 45 degrees below zero at Holabird; 46 degrees below zero at Barnard; 47 degrees below zero at Gettysburg; 49 degrees below zero at Britton and Eureka; and 53 degrees below zero south of Bullhead.

A record breaking surface low pressure area moved across the central plains and brought rain, freezing rain, sleet, heavy snow and blizzard conditions to most all of central and northeast South Dakota. Heavy rain of 1 to over 2 inches occurred across the east and southeast part of the region in the Webster, Clark, Milbank, Clear Lake, and Watertown areas before the precipitation changed over to snow. In between the rain and snow, a band of freezing rain and sleet occurred. Ice accumulation up to quarter to a half inch combined with high winds brought down some power lines and poles bringing many power outages along with bringing treacherous travel.  Nearly 1,500 people were without power for a few days mainly for Hand, Spink, Brown, Day, Marshall, and Roberts counties. ||The heavy snow and strong winds also brought some cattle losses across the region along with some damage to buildings.  Interstate 90 was closed along with many other roads across the region. Nearly all schools were closed. The State B Basketball Tournament in Aberdeen was also affected by the storm with a delay in the start time. Emergency declarations were issued for many counties for the hazardous travel conditions and impassable roads along with livestock losses and structure damage. The declarations included subsequent flooding at the end of March. Agricultural producers were eligible for loans from the USDA who incurred losses from the blizzard.||Some of the snowfall amounts include, 6 inches at Faulkton; 7 inches at Miller and Mclaughlin; 8 inches at Pollock and Roscoe; 10 inches at Ipswich, Pierre, Selby, Artas, Eureka, and 5 miles northwest of Mission Ridge. Locations with a foot of more include, 12 inches 19 miles north of Highmore; 13 inches at Bowdle, 13 miles west of Leola, and 9 miles south of Long Lake; 14 inches at Murdo and 4 miles northwest of Elm Lake; 15 inches at Ree Heights; and 16 inches 4 miles northwest of Onida and Draper. ||The heavy rainfall amounts east and southeast in the region include, 1.50 inches at Webster; 1.54 inches at Milbank and Clark, 1.91 inches at Watertown, and 2.43 inches at Clear Lake.  This rain added water to an already deep and high water content snow pack across the region which would turn into flooding by the end of March.||The highest wind gusts include, 49 mph at Watertown; 58 mph at Mobridge; 60 mph at Pierre and Aberdeen; 69 mph 10 miles west of Long Lake; and 70 mph 13 miles north of Vivian. The high winds and heavy snow created 5 to 10 foot drifts.

In between the rain and snow, a band of freezing rain and sleet occurred. Ice accumulation up to quarter to a half inch combined with high winds brought down some power lines and poles bringing many power outages along with bringing treacherous travel.  Nearly 1,500 people were without power for a few days mainly for Hand, Spink, Brown, Day, Marshall, and Roberts counties. ||The heavy snow and strong winds also brought some cattle losses across the region along with some damage to buildings.  Interstate 90 was closed along with many other roads across the region. Nearly all schools were closed. The State B Basketball Tournament in Aberdeen was also affected by the storm with a delay in the start time. Emergency declarations were issued for many counties for the hazardous travel conditions and impassable roads along with livestock losses and structure damage. The declarations included subsequent flooding at the end of March. Agricultural producers were eligible for loans from the USDA who incurred losses from the blizzard.||Some of the snowfall amounts include, 6 inches at Faulkton; 7 inches at Miller and Mclaughlin; 8 inches at Pollock and Roscoe; 10 inches at Ipswich, Pierre, Selby, Artas, Eureka, and 5 miles northwest of Mission Ridge. Locations with a foot of more include, 12 inches 19 miles north of Highmore; 13 inches at Bowdle, 13 miles west of Leola, and 9 miles south of Long Lake; 14 inches at Murdo and 4 miles northwest of Elm Lake; 15 inches at Ree Heights; and 16 inches 4 miles northwest of Onida and Draper. ||The heavy rainfall amounts east and southeast in the region include, 1.50 inches at Webster; 1.54 inches at Milbank and Clark, 1.91 inches at Watertown, and 2.43 inches at Clear Lake.  This rain added water to an already deep and high water content snow pack across the region which would turn into flooding by the end of March.||The highest wind gusts include, 49 mph at Watertown; 58 mph at Mobridge; 60 mph at Pierre and Aberdeen; 69 mph 10 miles west of Long Lake; and 70 mph 13 miles north of Vivian. The high winds and heavy snow created 5 to 10 foot drifts.

Much above normal winter snowfall and melt water/ice jams along with heavy rains in the middle of March brought flooding across parts of central and northeast South Dakota for late March. Rivers and creeks flooded across much of the area along with many fields and roads. The flooding damaged many of the roads and culverts across the region. Some structures were also flooded. Lake levels also rose threatening homes mainly in the east at Lake Kampeska near Watertown. Many counties issued emergency declarations for the flooding to include the mid-March snowstorm. South Dakota's governor also declared a state of emergency. Much of this flooding continued into early April as the snowmelt continued with the high water delaying planting.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Many roads were flooded in the county and on the Standing Rock Indian Reservation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong winter storm moved across the region, bringing heavy snow and blizzard conditions to much of western and south central South Dakota. Snow developed during the morning hours, with rain and snow changing to snow southeast of the Black Hills by the early afternoon. Northwest winds increased during the late morning and afternoon, with blizzard conditions developing across much of the plains. Snow ended the next morning, with winds gradually tapering off by late afternoon. Snowfall amounts were widely variable across the area, ranging from an inch or less over parts of Harding County to one to two feet in some portions of Haakon, Jackson, and Bennett Counties and portions of the northern Black Hills. Drifts of six feet and higher were reported where the heaviest snow fell on the plains. Much of Interstate 90 across western South Dakota were closed for more than 36 hours.

A historic late-season, multi-day winter storm developed rapidly across the central Plains on Wednesday, April 10. This system spiraled several periods of wintry precipitation through the region.  The leading precipitation on April 10 was a mix of rain, freezing rain and sleet from far eastern South Dakota through the Interstate 90 areas of southwest Minnesota and northwest Iowa, changing to snow to the north and west.  There were even a few elevated thunderstorms which occurred overnight on April 10-11 and produced some near severe hail, which actually occurred after some initial wintry precipitation.  Significant icing was centered around 30 miles either side of the Interstate 90 corridor, with as much as a third to three-quarters inch ice accumulation.||Heavy snowfall developed from the early morning of April 11 and continued into the early morning of April 12 across much of southeast South Dakota into northern portions of southwest Minnesota, which accumulated to 1 to 2 feet during the storm.  The most persistent snowfall occurred for areas west to north of Sioux Falls, but even there, a brief period of freezing rain or sleet occurred at some point ahead of the main upper trough passage.||The other aspect of this storm was the intense winds, which gusted at the peak from 40 to 60 mph, producing widespread blizzard conditions to southeast South Dakota and southwest Minnesota.  Considerable power outages occurred as transmission lines succumbed to the ice accumulation and wind.  At the peak, electric companies and cooperatives estimated as many as 25,000 customers were without power.  Many areas were without power for a day or two due to the intensity of the storm.  The strong winds lofted dust from the Texas/Oklahoma panhandles which caused some of the snowfall toward the latter portions of the event to fall with a brownish color.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blizzard conditions led to a shutdown of government offices and schools, and travel was not recommended due to the widespread whiteout conditions. Almost unprecedented amounts of storm-total snowfall occurred, with the 3-day snowfall of 20.0 inches at Wessington Springs the second greatest 3-day snowfall total on record.  With winds gusting to around 50 mph, many areas had snow drifts of 5 to 10 feet, making many roads impassable. Spotty power outages occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The continuation of snowmelt from a much above normal snowfall winter combined with a historic heavy snow/blizzard in mid-April resulted in widespread flooding across central and northeast South Dakota. Countless roads along with thousands of acres of cropland were flooded throughout April. Impacts include damaged roads, culverts, and bridges, and livestock, homes, and businesses were affected. Delayed planting resulted across all of the region as well. Cattle and calves were stressed by the cold and wet pattern, as the mud and cold caused some sickness with the livestock. Flooded roads made it difficult for many farmers or ranchers to get to their fields or livestock. There was a water rescue near Frederick on April 7th as someone drove into a washed out road. The wet pattern along with the flooding continued into May, further delaying planting across the region.||All counties declared emergencies/disasters in March and April due to the widespread flooding and March blizzard.  South Dakota's governor declared a disaster for the state in March. This declaration was followed by a disaster declaration by the President of the United States. As a result, 24 of the 26 counties across central and northeast South Dakota were able to have access to public property damage assistance. Overall, damage estimates from the blizzards and floods for the state were at 43 million dollars.

Extremely cold arctic air dominated the weather across central and northeast South Dakota from the late evening of the 2nd through the morning of the 3rd. Record lows in the teens below and 20s below zero were set across the region. Most of the record lows shattered the previous record lows by 5 to 10 degrees. In fact, Britton in northeast South Dakota fell to 29 degrees below zero breaking the old record of 19 degrees below zero.||The arctic cold along with north winds brought dangerously cold wind chills ranging from 35 below zero to near 55 below zero across the region. Some of the extreme wind chills included, 40 degrees below zero at Aberdeen; 42 degrees below zero at Pierre; 45 degrees below zero at Holabird; 46 degrees below zero at Barnard; 47 degrees below zero at Gettysburg; 49 degrees below zero at Britton and Eureka; and 53 degrees below zero south of Bullhead.

An intense spring storm system brought rain, snow, and gusty winds to the area. Rain developed during the late afternoon and night of April 9. Rain gradually switched over to snow across northwestern South Dakota and the Black Hills area during the overnight hours. Thundersnow was also noted over the Black Hills area during the early morning of April 10. Snow was heavy at times during the late night and morning across parts of northwestern South Dakota. Snow expanded across the rest of the western and south central South Dakota plains during the day. Winds increased from the north late Wednesday and persisted into Thursday. Blowing and drifting snow developed, especially east of the Black Hills, where blizzard conditions occurred in some areas late Wednesday night and Thursday. Snowfall totals ranged from a few inches over far southwestern South Dakota to over two feet across portions of west central and south central South Dakota.

A storm system brought rain and snow to much of the area. A band of heavier precipitation produced more significant snowfall across the central and southern Black Hills during the morning. Snowfall across these areas and the adjacent foothills ranged from two to six inches on grass and other cold surfaces, with some higher amounts to eight inches.

Runoff from a widespread May 19-21 rainfall of 1 to 2 inches across the Vermillion River basin returned much of the river to moderate to major flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Vermillion River near Wakonda (gage site WKAS2) reached a peak of 3.10 feet above flood stage on May 31. 25000 acres of agricultural land were flooded between Centerville and Vermillion.  Several county and state roads were inundated, including Highway 19 in several locations south of Centerville. Major flooding continued at the end of the month.

A surface low pressure trough moved across the region and brought heavy snow to central and north central South Dakota. Snowfall amounts ranged from 6 to 10 inches across this region. Travel was disrupted or halted. Some snowfall amounts included, 6 inches at Pierre, Mobridge, and Highmore; 7 inches at Presho, Hayes, and 7 miles west of Sully Buttes; 8 inches at Murdo, Gettysburg and 14 miles northeast of Isabelle; 9 inches at Mission Ridge; and 10 inches at Isabelle.

An upper level low pressure trough brought 2 to 5 inches of snowfall to northeast South Dakota followed by arctic air and very strong north winds. North winds gusting to 40 to  50 mph brought blizzard conditions to much of northeast South Dakota from the morning to the evening of the 7th. All roads in northeast South Dakota were no travel advised or closed. Interstate 29 was closed from Sioux Falls to the North Dakota border through the night into the next morning. Activities and schools were cancelled or closed for the day.   A rural Watertown man died in the blizzard conditions and bitter cold when he left his stranded vehicle and tried to walk for help.

A strong winter storm brought snow to portions of western and south central South Dakota. A band of heavy snow developed over northwestern South Dakota and lingered across the area for a prolonged period. Snowfall totals of eight to 15 inches were reported over much of northwestern South Dakota, with locally higher amounts over Harding and Perkins Counties. Areas south and east into south central South Dakota received two to six inches of snow. Wind gusts of 25 to 35 mph and very cold air behind the system produced blowing and drifting snow across the northwestern and west central South Dakota plains, as well as wind chill values to 25 below zero.

Wind chills ranged from 35 degrees below zero to near 50 degrees below zero. Many activities were cancelled and schools started late or closed for the day. Some of the lowest wind chills included, 45 degrees below zero at Aberdeen, Webster, Gettysburg, and Mobridge; 47 degrees below zero at Eureka; and 48 degrees below zero south of Bullhead in Corson county.

A southwest to northeast band of heavy snow developed over north central South Dakota during the early morning hours, ending in the afternoon. Heavy snowfall of 6 to 16 inches occurred in about an 30 mile wide band. Travel was significantly disrupted or halted. Some snowfall amounts included, 6 inches at Pollock; 7 inches at Selby, 8 miles northeast of Promise, 5 miles northwest of Mission Ridge, and 6 miles southwest of Akaska; 9 inches at Mobridge; 11 inches at Leola and Bowdle; 12 inches 4 miles north of Eureka and Long Lake; and 16 inches 11 miles southwest of Eureka.

Travel was disrupted or halted. Some snowfall amounts included, 6 inches at Pierre, Mobridge, and Highmore; 7 inches at Presho, Hayes, and 7 miles west of Sully Buttes; 8 inches at Murdo, Gettysburg and 14 miles northeast of Isabelle; 9 inches at Mission Ridge; and 10 inches at Isabelle.

An upper level low pressure trough brought 2 to 5 inches of snowfall to northeast South Dakota followed by arctic air and very strong north winds. North winds gusting to 40 to  50 mph brought blizzard conditions to much of northeast South Dakota from the morning to the evening of the 7th. All roads in northeast South Dakota were no travel advised or closed. Interstate 29 was closed from Sioux Falls to the North Dakota border through the night into the next morning. Activities and schools were cancelled or closed for the day.   A rural Watertown man died in the blizzard conditions and bitter cold when he left his stranded vehicle and tried to walk for help.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Spring snowmelt and heavy rain flooding from March, April, and May continued into June. This combined with above normal June rainfall resulted in hundreds of thousands of acres of crops damaged or unplanted across central and northeast South Dakota. For the entire state of South Dakota, nearly 4 million acres of crops were left unplanted as a result of the flooding. Total damaged or unplanted crop loss estimates for central and northeast South Dakota were near 307 million dollars.

The largest hail fell from southwest Rapid City to Hermosa and to west of Fairburn.

As a weak mid-level wave moved across the area, a few storms fired along a frontal boundary. While the environment featured 1500-2000 J/kg MLCAPE, the deep layer shear was 20 knots or less, and this resulted in most storms weakening fairly rapidly after development. Isolated storms ended up with strong enough updrafts to produce hail.

Areas near and south of Interstate 90 experienced the first dangerous heat of the year. Afternoon and early evening temperatures from 90 to 95, along with dew point readings from 72 to 77 resulted in maximum heat index values of 100 to 105 degrees.  Some higher heat index readings included 106 degrees at Yankton, 103 degrees 5 miles south southeast of Centerville and Le Mars, 102 degrees at Parkston, White Lake and Sioux City, and 101 degrees at Gregory.  Several roads buckled due to the heat near Sioux City.

Rainfall of one to three inches on frozen ground and into a snow pack with between 2 and 5 inches of liquid water equivalent resulted in considerable overland flooding.||One of the hardest hit areas was around Yankton, where a No Travel Advisory was issued for the city on March 13-14.  Businesses along north Highway 81 toward Yankton Mall had considerable ponding of water.  Water rescues were necessary as cars stalled in high water in low spots around Yankton.  Two people were also rescued from their inundated vehicle on Jim River Road east of Yankton. Water also got into power substations and caused spotty power outages and necessitated temporary evacuation of residents.||State government offices across the area were closed on March 14 due to the flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: South Dakota Highway 46 was closed both east and west of Wagner due to water flowing over the roadway. Other flooding resulted in damage to public infrastructure including county and township roads and culverts.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Interstate 90 was closed due to water flowing over the road between exits 364 and 368 southeast of Salem. Other flooding resulted in extensive damage to public infrastructure including numerous county and township roads, culverts, and some bridges.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A historic flooding event unfolded as heavy rainfall drenched the area on March 13-14, 2019.  With frozen and impervious ground, full and rapid runoff was maximized. In addition to the rainfall, between 1 and 3 inches of snow water equivalent remained in the snow pack south of Interstate 90, with 3 to 6 inches of snow water equivalent north of Interstate 90. Fifteen river gage sites measured record stages around the area.  Winds gusted to 50 to 70 mph at times, and colder air filtering into the system at the end of the storm produced a light  accumulation of ice west of the James River and from 1 to 4 inches of snowfall in parts of southeast South Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow melt and heavy rainfall (measured 2.74 inches at Hartford, 2.44 inches near Renner, 2.25 inches at Colton, and 2.49 inches at Sioux Falls) resulted in extensive flooding of agricultural land and recreational areas near Brandon. The Big Sioux River near Brandon (gage site BBSS2) reached the highest crest on record at 6.85 feet above flood stage on March 15. The river fell back to minor flood stage on March 15, but would rise back to major flood stage and crest 6.58 feet above flood stage on March 26  due to water routed from upstream melting. The river remained in major flood stage at the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A historic flooding event unfolded as heavy rainfall drenched the area on March 13-14, 2019.  With frozen and impervious ground, full and rapid runoff was maximized. In addition to the rainfall, between 1 and 3 inches of snow water equivalent remained in the snow pack south of Interstate 90, with 3 to 6 inches of snow water equivalent north of Interstate 90. Fifteen river gage sites measured record stages around the area.  Winds gusted to 50 to 70 mph at times, and colder air filtering into the system at the end of the storm produced a light  accumulation of ice west of the James River and from 1 to 4 inches of snowfall in parts of southeast South Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow melt and heavy rainfall (measured 3.07 inches 14 miles north northeast of Sioux Falls and 2.11 inches near Garretson) resulted in significant flooding of agricultural lands. State Highway 11 was barricaded due to water over the road near the confluence with Beaver Creek. Split Rock Creek at Corson (gage site COSS2) reached the tenth highest stage on record cresting 4.42 feet above flood stage on March 14.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A historic flooding event unfolded as heavy rainfall drenched the area on March 13-14, 2019.  With frozen and impervious ground, full and rapid runoff was maximized. In addition to the rainfall, between 1 and 3 inches of snow water equivalent remained in the snow pack south of Interstate 90, with 3 to 6 inches of snow water equivalent north of Interstate 90. Fifteen river gage sites measured record stages around the area.  Winds gusted to 50 to 70 mph at times, and colder air filtering into the system at the end of the storm produced a light  accumulation of ice west of the James River and from 1 to 4 inches of snowfall in parts of southeast South Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow melt and heavy rainfall (measured 2.74 inches near Hartford and 2.57 inches 3 miles west southwest of Sioux Falls) resulted in extensive flooding of agricultural land in the flood plain. Extensive damage to city parks on the west side of Sioux Falls occurred, including destruction of a pedestrian bridge at Dunham Park.  West 12th Street was covered by several feet of water from the creek westward to near Sertoma Avenue.  Numerous water rescues were required in residential areas near the gaging site, with as many as 11 residents evacuated from flooded areas. Skunk Creek at Sioux Falls (gage site SIFS2) reached the highest crest on record at 6.57 feet above flood stage on March 14. Property damage costs are rough estimates.
