An intense mid-level wave developed rapidly on October 10-11 across the Northern Plains.  While initial conditions were quite mild with widespread rain and a few thunderstorms, the system managed to draw in some extremely cold air which allowed a changeover during October 10 and 11 as the post-frontal precipitation band developed back through areas from the James River valley westward.  The cyclone wrapped up and backed westward across northern Minnesota heading into October 12, wrapping the snow band across parts of southeast South Dakota as winds increased to a gusty 30 to 45 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A light wintry mix changed to snow during the morning of October 11, and light to occasionally moderate snow over the following two days accumulated to 3 to 6 inches. Visibility dropped to 1 mile or less at times as snowfall totaled 5.8 inches at Howard.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense mid-level wave developed rapidly on October 10-11 across the Northern Plains.  While initial conditions were quite mild with widespread rain and a few thunderstorms, the system managed to draw in some extremely cold air which allowed a changeover during October 10 and 11 as the post-frontal precipitation band developed back through areas from the James River valley westward.  The cyclone wrapped up and backed westward across northern Minnesota heading into October 12, wrapping the snow band across parts of southeast South Dakota as winds increased to a gusty 30 to 45 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A light wintry mix changed to snow during the morning of October 11, and light to occasionally moderate snow over the following two days accumulated to 3 to 7 inches. Visibility dropped to 1 mile or less at times as snowfall totaled 4.0 inches at De Smet and 7.0 inches at Iroquois.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A rare anticyclonic tornado touched down northeast of Estelline during the evening and lasted less than a minute. The tornado moved east and knocked down seven trees and tossed tree branches to the south and west at a farmstead. One of the tree branches hit the corner of a shed and bent part of the metal overhang on the shed.

Snowmelt runoff brought the Little Minnesota River near Peever to above the flood stage of 17 feet on April 18th to just below 19 feet on April 19th. The river then quickly fell below flood stage on April 21st.

Late March snowmelt flooding on the Elm River near Frederick continued into early April. The river rose above the flood stage of 13 feet to 15.7 feet on March 31st and then slowly fell to below flood stage on April 12th. The river then rose slightly above flood stage again from April 18th through the 21st from additional runoff from an mid-April blizzard.

A historic flooding event unfolded as heavy rainfall drenched the area on March 13-14, 2019.  With frozen and impervious ground, full and rapid runoff was maximized. In addition to the rainfall, between 1 and 3 inches of snow water equivalent remained in the snow pack south of Interstate 90, with 3 to 6 inches of snow water equivalent north of Interstate 90. Fifteen river gage sites measured record stages around the area.  Winds gusted to 50 to 70 mph at times, and colder air filtering into the system at the end of the storm produced a light  accumulation of ice west of the James River and from 1 to 4 inches of snowfall in parts of southeast South Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow melt and heavy rainfall (measured 2.01 inches 2 miles north of Chester and 2.74 inches at Hartford) resulted in extensive flooding of agricultural land in the flood plain, and inundated portions of 259th St. near the gage. Willow Creek near Crooks (gage site WCCS2) reached the highest crest on record at 3.4 feet above flood stage on March 14.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Areas near and south of Interstate 90 experienced the first dangerous heat of the year. Afternoon and early evening temperatures from 90 to 95, along with dew point readings from 72 to 77 resulted in maximum heat index values of 100 to 105 degrees.  Some higher heat index readings included 106 degrees at Yankton, 103 degrees 5 miles south southeast of Centerville and Le Mars, 102 degrees at Parkston, White Lake and Sioux City, and 101 degrees at Gregory.  Several roads buckled due to the heat near Sioux City.

In a very warm and humid air mass, featuring up to 3000 J/kg MLCAPE,  isolated surface-based storms fired along a boundary in the late afternoon as a weak mid-level wave moved into the region.  Shear remained fairly weak, concentrated at lower levels near the surface boundary.  Storms moved erratically, nearly stationary over a couple hours.  One high-based tornado developed in Gregory County near Lucas, SD which was visible over a large area.

A combination of higher than normal dam releases and periodic rain episodes maintained flooding on sections of the Missouri River between Big Bend and Gavins Point dams, as well as downstream toward Sioux City.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A continuation of flooding from May, the Missouri River at Chamberlain (gage site CBLS2) started the month with a week of major flooding, as water crested 6.06 feet above flood stage on June 2 , the third highest crest on record.  American Creek Campground and Marina were inundated. Numerous recreational boat ramps were closed due to high water. A gradual decrease in water levels continued through mid-month, with the river falling below flood stage on June 21.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A combination of higher than normal dam releases and periodic rain episodes maintained flooding on sections of the Missouri River between Big Bend and Gavins Point dams, as well as downstream toward Sioux City.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Much of the month (June 3-24) was spent in moderate flood stage. The Missouri River near Greenwood (gage site GRWS2) crested 1.85 feet above flood stage on June 20, the third highest crest on record.  Impacts were mainly limited to inundation of agricultural land, as well as closing of numerous recreational boat ramps. The river remained in flood at the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A combination of higher than normal dam releases and periodic rain episodes maintained flooding on sections of the Missouri River between Big Bend and Gavins Point dams, as well as downstream toward Sioux City.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A continuation of flooding from May, the Missouri River near Verdel (gage site MRVN1) crested 1.07 feet above flood stage on June 20.  High water closed numerous recreational boat ramps. The river remained at minor flood stage at the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A tornadic thunderstorm developed rapidly along a boundary located over western Bennett County. The tornado was unusual because it lasted 40 minutes and only moved within a two mile wide area, mainly over corn fields. The storm dissipated relatively quickly after it moved away from the boundary.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado formed in a field southwest of Allen and spun in a two-mile area, first moving west, then south, and finally north. It damaged a house and barn, blew over a couple of grain bins, snapped large trees and two power poles. It also ripped corn from the fields, killed several deer, and picked up some grain bags and carried them along its track before dropping them as it dissipated.

In addition to the rainfall, between 1 and 3 inches of snow water equivalent remained in the snow pack south of Interstate 90, with 3 to 6 inches of snow water equivalent north of Interstate 90. Fifteen river gage sites measured record stages around the area.  Winds gusted to 50 to 70 mph at times, and colder air filtering into the system at the end of the storm produced a light  accumulation of ice west of the James River and from 1 to 4 inches of snowfall in parts of southeast South Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow melt and heavy rainfall (measured 2.11 inches near Garretson and 1.70 inches at  Pipestone) resulted in significant flooding of agricultural lands. 250th Street along the Minnesota/South Dakota border was flooded for numerous days. An intense mid-level wave developed rapidly on October 10-11 across the Northern Plains.  While initial conditions were quite mild with widespread rain and a few thunderstorms, the system managed to draw in some extremely cold air which allowed a changeover during October 10 and 11 as the post-frontal precipitation band developed back through areas from the James River valley westward.  The cyclone wrapped up and backed westward across northern Minnesota heading into October 12, wrapping the snow band across parts of southeast South Dakota as winds increased to a gusty 30 to 45 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A light wintry mix changed to snow during the early morning of October 11, and light to occasionally moderate snow over the following two days accumulated to 2 to 4 inches. Visibility dropped to 1 mile or less at times as snowfall totaled 2.9 inches at Madison and 3.0 inches 2 miles south of Winfred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rainfall of one to three inches on frozen ground and into a snow pack with between 2 and 5 inches of liquid water equivalent resulted in considerable overland flooding.||One of the hardest hit areas was around Yankton, where a No Travel Advisory was issued for the city on March 13-14.  Businesses along north Highway 81 toward Yankton Mall had considerable ponding of water.  Water rescues were necessary as cars stalled in high water in low spots around Yankton.  Two people were also rescued from their inundated vehicle on Jim River Road east of Yankton. Water also got into power substations and caused spotty power outages and necessitated temporary evacuation of residents.||State government offices across the area were closed on March 14 due to the flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple rural roads under water. Other flooding resulted in extensive damage to public infrastructure including numerous county and township roads, culverts, and some bridges.

A historic flooding event unfolded as heavy rainfall drenched the area on March 13-14, 2019.  With frozen and impervious ground, full and rapid runoff was maximized. In addition to the rainfall, between 1 and 3 inches of snow water equivalent remained in the snow pack south of Interstate 90, with 3 to 6 inches of snow water equivalent north of Interstate 90. Fifteen river gage sites measured record stages around the area.  Winds gusted to 50 to 70 mph at times, and colder air filtering into the system at the end of the storm produced a light  accumulation of ice west of the James River and from 1 to 4 inches of snowfall in parts of southeast South Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow melt and heavy rainfall (measured 2.74 inches at Hartford, 2.25 inches at Colton, and 2.49 inches at Sioux Falls) resulted in extensive flooding of the Sioux Falls park system near the natural channel of the Big Sioux River around the southern side of Sioux Falls. Water, and the broken ice cover from the river system, caused extensive damage to city property with numerous bridges and buildings completely destroyed, equipment damaged, and miles of bike trail washed out. Around 80 homes were inundated by floodwater with evacuation of 17 residents and their pets, as water reached the highest levels since construction of the homes.  Water filled the tunnel on 57th Street under Interstate 229, flowed onto several portions of Interstate 229, and over 26th Street east of Interstate 229 and Cliff Avenue south of I-229. The Big Sioux River at Western Avenue (gage site WAVS2) reached the highest stage on record cresting 9.15 feet above flood stage on March 14. The river remained at minor flood stage at the end of the month. Property damage costs are rough estimates.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm briefly became severe over the northeastern slopes of the Black Hills, producing hail to quarter size in the Nemo area.
A thunderstorm briefly became severe over the northeastern slopes of the Black Hills, producing hail to quarter size in the Nemo area.

A historic flooding event unfolded as heavy rainfall drenched the area on March 13-14, 2019.  With frozen and impervious ground, full and rapid runoff was maximized. In addition to the rainfall, between 1 and 3 inches of snow water equivalent remained in the snow pack south of Interstate 90, with 3 to 6 inches of snow water equivalent north of Interstate 90. Fifteen river gage sites measured record stages around the area.  Winds gusted to 50 to 70 mph at times, and colder air filtering into the system at the end of the storm produced a light  accumulation of ice west of the James River and from 1 to 4 inches of snowfall in parts of southeast South Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow melt and heavy rainfall (measured 2.74 inches at Hartford, 2.25 inches at Colton, and 2.49 inches at Sioux Falls) resulted in extensive flooding of the Sioux Falls park system near the natural channel of the Big Sioux River around the southern side of Sioux Falls. Water, and the broken ice cover from the river system, caused extensive damage to city property with numerous bridges and buildings completely destroyed, equipment damaged, and miles of bike trail washed out. Around 80 homes were inundated by floodwater with evacuation of 17 residents and their pets, as water reached the highest levels since construction of the homes.  Water filled the tunnel on 57th Street under Interstate 229, flowed onto several portions of Interstate 229, and over 26th Street east of Interstate 229 and Cliff Avenue south of I-229. The Big Sioux River at Western Avenue (gage site WAVS2) reached the highest stage on record cresting 9.15 feet above flood stage on March 14. The river remained at minor flood stage at the end of the month. Property damage costs are rough estimates.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: March and April snowmelt and heavy rain flooding along with above normal May precipitation resulted in hundreds of thousands of acres of crops damaged or delayed/unplanted across central and northeast South Dakota. The widespread flooding continued into June with millions of dollars of loss.

Although locally heavy precipitation early in the month allowed for a brief push to flood levels in portions of the Big Sioux Basin, lesser precipitation during the month generally allowed river levels to gradually drop during October 2019.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: After rainfall on October 1 of 1.08 inches in Clear Lake, 0.81 inches in Bruce, and 0.80 inches in Brookings, the Big Sioux River near Brookings (gage site BRKS2) climbed back into flood stage and crested at 0.13 feet above flood stage on October 7.  A few county and township roads were flooded near the gage site, along with a considerable amount of agricultural land. Property damage costs are rough estimates.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Although locally heavy precipitation early in the month allowed for a brief push to flood levels in portions of the Big Sioux Basin, lesser precipitation during the month generally allowed river levels to gradually drop during October 2019.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall around October 1 which averaged 1.00 to 1.50 inches around Sioux Falls and 2.00 to 3.00 inches in the Rock River Basin pushed the Big Sioux River at Akron (gage site AKRI4) into minor flood. The river crested 1.38 feet above flood stage on October 7.  Significant flooding of agricultural land occurred, with a few county and township roads briefly inundated near the river. Property damage costs are rough estimates.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Although locally heavy precipitation early in the month allowed for a brief push to flood levels in portions of the Big Sioux Basin, lesser precipitation during the month generally allowed river levels to gradually drop during October 2019.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall which averaged 1.00 to 1.50 inches around Sioux Falls and from 2.00 to 3.00 inches along the Rock River Basin in northwest Iowa at the start of the month pushed the Big Sioux River above Hawarden (gage site HWDI4) into minor flooding. The river crested 1.52 feet above flood stage on October 6.  Extensive agricultural flooding occurred. Property damage costs are rough estimates.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure intensified moving from Kansas to Minnesota on October 21, producing widespread rainfall, which was locally heavy in upper portions of the Big Sioux River basin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: After rainfall on October 20-21 which totaled 1.48 inches at Clear Lake, 0.64 inches at Castlewood, and 0.57 inches at Bruce, the Big Sioux River near Bruce (gage site BRCS2) crested on October 24 at 0.15 feet above flood stage, resulting in minor flooding of agricultural land. Property damage costs are rough estimates.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a weak mid-level wave moved across the area, a few storms fired along a frontal boundary. While the environment featured 1500-2000 J/kg MLCAPE, the deep layer shear was 20 knots or less, and this resulted in most storms weakening fairly rapidly after development. Isolated storms ended up with strong enough updrafts to produce hail.
A couple of small and brief funnels occurred as storms developed during the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Observed small funnel for a couple of minutes.

A long-lived supercell thunderstorm developed northwest of Rapid City and moved south-southeast along the eastern foothills. The storm produced a long swath of large hail from western and southern portions of Rapid City to near Fairburn. The storm weakened as it moved off the Black Hills east of Buffalo Gap. The largest hail fell from southwest Rapid City to Hermosa and to west of Fairburn.

With frozen ground and most urban drainage plugged by ice and snow, a period of heavy rainfall during the morning in Sioux Falls produced urban flooding. Up to 1000 intersections were inundated with water as deep as 1 to 2 feet due to runoff from morning rain and plugged storm drains.  Numerous vehicles became stranded in standing water. The mayor of Sioux Falls joined in the effort to clear plugged drains.  Even by 1600CST, over 300 intersections remained inundated by standing water.  ||A record precipitation amount for the day occurred at the National Weather Service office in Sioux Falls (0.86 inches).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A combination of increased releases from Gavins Point Dam and the widespread 2 to 4 inches of rainfall between May 21 and May 27 across much of southeast South Dakota, northwest Iowa, and northeast Nebraska led to flooding of the lower Missouri River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Increased releases from upstream Gavins Point Dam along with runoff from heavy rainfall over the preceding week produced minor flooding on the Missouri River at Sioux City (gage site SSCN1). Hamilton Boulevard and a few park areas along the river near Interstate 29 were flooded as the river rose to 0.22 feet above flood stage on May 31. The river remained in minor flood stage at the end of the month.

Agricultural areas near the river were inundated as the river rose to 2.46 feet above flood stage on May 31. The river remained in moderate flood stage at the end of the month.

Very heavy rainfall caused the Oak Creek near Wakpala to rise quickly above and below flood stage from July 9th to the 10th. The creek rose to above the flood stage of 10 feet in the late evening hours of the 9th to 13.51 feet in the morning hours of the 10th. The creek then fell back to below flood stage in the early evening hours of the 10th.
An isolated severe thunderstorm with estimated seventy mph winds brought down and destroyed a calf shelter three miles south of Brandt.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Calf shelter destroyed by estimated seventy mph winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A widespread 2 to 4 inches of rainfall between May 21 and May 27 across much of southeast South Dakota resulted in late month flooding on the upper and lower portions of the Vermillion River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The West Fork of the Vermillion River near Parker (gage site PKRS2) reached a crest of 1.59 feet above flood stage on May 30. Impacts were mainly agricultural, causing further delays to spring planting and preparations throughout the flood plain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After major to record flooding due to widespread heavy precipitation during the previous week, conditions were primed for additional flooding when rain began May 26. As much as four inches of rain was observed near Faith, while one to two inches of rain fell over portions of the White River basin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from heavy rain caused minor flooding of agricultural land along White Clay Creek. Flooding and high water levels along White Clay Creek quickly filled Oglala Dam and White Clay Dam. Due to the large volume of water and saturated soils, both reservoirs had to be drained.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated late afternoon thunderstorm developed along a weak convergence axis across southeast South Dakota. Instability was around 1500-2000 J/kg with weak deep-layer shear, resulting in brief stronger updrafts and small hail.
Wake low winds behind exiting thunderstorms gusted to 58 mph at Pierre.
A widespread 2 to 4 inches of rainfall between May 21 and May 27 across much of southeast South Dakota resulted in late month flooding on the upper and lower portions of the Vermillion River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Vermillion River at Vermillion (gage site VERS2) reached a crest of 1.40 feet above flood stage on May 31. Impacts were mainly agricultural, causing further delays to spring planting and preparations throughout the flood plain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front pushing southward on the evening of May 31 spurred a few thunderstorms with the diurnal heating ahead of the boundary producing up to 1000-1500 J/kg MLCAPE. The environment featured meager (<20 knots) deep-layer shear and an inverted-V profile. As a result, short-lived updrafts produced some sub-severe wind and hail.

A powerful storm system slowly moved from the Four Corners region northeastward through the Plains, leading to a prolonged period of precipitation across western South Dakota May 20-22. This three day period was among the coldest, wettest, and snowiest on record for late May, with several temperature and precipitation records set. Runoff from the rain and melting snow led to flooding of creeks, rivers, low-lying areas, and some roads across western and south central South Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant flooding occurred along the White River south of Kadoka. A record gauge height of 17.57 feet was set (flood stage is 13 feet), with a peak flow of 17,800 cfs, which is the highest flow recorded since measurements began at this location in 1942. Widespread flooding of agricultural land occurred and several outbuildings were inundated. A county road and bridge about 10 miles downstream of the gauge were washed out and SD Highway 44 south of Interior was covered with water in three locations.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A resident measured 5.8 inches of rain in 24 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful storm system slowly moved from the Four Corners region northeastward through the Plains, leading to a prolonged period of precipitation across western South Dakota May 20-22. This three day period was among the coldest, wettest, and snowiest on record for late May, with several temperature and precipitation records set. Runoff from the rain and melting snow led to flooding of creeks, rivers, low-lying areas, and some roads across western and south central South Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from heavy rain caused moderate flooding along the Bad River from near Cottonwood to east of Midland. The peak gauge height on the Bad River at Midland reached a preliminary height of 23.45 feet on May 24, which is the second highest gauge height recorded at this location since 1946.

Minor flooding of agricultural land occurred along the Belle Fourche River, which crested at 19.5 feet, just above flood stage of 19 feet, with a peak flow around 32,200 cfs, at SD Highway 34 east of Sturgis.

The river fell back to minor flood stage on March 15, but would rise back to major flood stage and crest 6.58 feet above flood stage on March 26  due to water routed from upstream melting. The river remained in major flood stage at the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After major to record flooding due to widespread heavy precipitation during the previous week, conditions were primed for additional flooding when rain began May 26. As much as four inches of rain was observed near Faith, while one to two inches of rain fell over portions of the White River basin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three to four inches of rain in an hour and three to five inches of rain in two hours over parts of northeastern Meade County caused rapid rises on small creeks and overland flooding of rural areas. The flooding compromised five culverts and a bridge on county roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After major to record flooding due to widespread heavy precipitation during the previous week, conditions were primed for additional flooding when rain began May 26. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three to five inches of rain fell in about two hours over portions of southern Ziebach County, causing rapid rises on small creeks and overland flooding of rural areas. Several county and BIA roads were washed out, including Dupree Road and BIA 9. US Highway 212 near mile marker 121 was also inundated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several days of heavy rain, along with major to record flooding the previous week, led to more flooding across portions of the western South Dakota plains and foothills of the Black Hills. The period of wet weather culminated with heavy rain and thunderstorms over the Rapid City area during the afternoon and evening of May 31, which led to widespread flooded roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from heavy rain over northwestern Nebraska caused minor to moderate flooding of the White River from the Nebraska state line to the Badlands.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding of agricultural land occurred along the White River around the US Highway 83 bridge north of the town of White River. The river crested just over flood stage (14 feet) at 14.92 feet on May 28, with a peak flow around 18,100 cfs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snowmelt and heavy rain from late March and April along with above normal rainfall in May brought periodic flooding on the Turtle Creek throughout May. The creek fell below flood stage on the 1st of May and then above and below flood stage from the 11th through the 15th and again from the 22nd through the end of May into June. The creek reached its highest stage of 15.50 feet on April 1st where flood stage is 7 feet.

Battle Creek covered several roads west of Hermosa. Spring Creek covered portions of Lower Spring Creek Road east of Hermosa. Cottonwood Creek overtopped Cottonwood Cutoff Road east of Buffalo Gap.

At least three people had to be rescued after driving into flood waters, including one where a culvert washed out on Nike Road just east of Haines Avenue and north of Country Road.

Evacuations occurred on Line Road as water surrounded several mobile homes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong spring storm brought heavy precipitation to western South Dakota. Precipitation type was dependent on elevation, with mainly rain across the plains north and east of the Black Hills, while temperatures were cold enough for snow over the Black Hills and foothills. One to two feet of snow was common across the Black Hills and along the eastern slopes. The higher foothills from Spearfish to south of Hermosa saw snowfall of six inches to over a foot during the latter part of the storm as temperatures dropped. The heavy, wet snow downed trees and power lines across the Black Hills.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from heavy rain caused flooding upstream of Shadehill Reservoir and along the Moreau River. Date Road south of SD Highway 20 was closed, Rabbit Creek covered a couple of county roads, a culvert on Zeona Road was washed out, and county roads C5 and C1 were inundated with water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several days of heavy rain, along with major to record flooding the previous week, led to more flooding across portions of the western South Dakota plains and foothills of the Black Hills. The period of wet weather culminated with heavy rain and thunderstorms over the Rapid City area during the afternoon and evening of May 31, which led to widespread flooded roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding of agricultural land occurred along the White River south of Kadoka. The river crested at 14.25 feet (flood stage of 13 feet) on May 29.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful storm system slowly moved from the Four Corners region northeastward through the Plains, leading to a prolonged period of precipitation across western South Dakota May 20-22. This three day period was among the coldest, wettest, and snowiest on record for late May, with several temperature and precipitation records set. Runoff from the rain and melting snow led to flooding of creeks, rivers, low-lying areas, and some roads across western and south central South Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding of agricultural land occurred along the White River near the SD Highway 47 bridge south of Reliance, the Keya Paha River near Keyapaha and Wewela, and smaller creeks and streams. The White River crested at 18.86 feet (flood stage is 15 feet), with a peak flow around 29,900 cfs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Close on the heels of the last winter storm, an amplified upper-air pattern brought another impulse out of the southwestern U.S. early on Friday, November 29 and pushed a shield of light mixed precipitation northeastward across the region with a light accumulation of ice.  As the main trough and area of low pressure organized on Saturday, November 30 across Nebraska and deepened as it moved into southern Wisconsin by the morning of Sunday, December 1, additional snowfall developed and was accompanied by strong and gusty northwest winds of 30 to 50 mph by later Saturday night into Sunday morning.||Snowfall totals over the period generally ranged from 6 to 16 inches west of the James River,  to less than one inch from northeast Nebraska and far southeast South Dakota toward southern portions of northwest Iowa.  Another mesoscale band of heavy snowfall occurred on Saturday, November 30 near the Minnesota-Iowa border with between 6 and 9 inches accumulation.||This storm continued into December 1, although a majority of accumulating snowfall ended between 0000 and 0900CST.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mixed precipitation, with occasional light freezing rain or freezing drizzle, began late afternoon on November 29, and resulted in up to a tenth of an inch ice accumulation through the early morning of November 30. Snow developed again by early morning, with periods of moderate snowfall into early Sunday. Snowfall totals by early Sunday reached 5.5 inches at Madison and 4.2 inches 2 miles north of Chester.  In addition, winds gusted to 40 mph at Madison with patchy reduction in visibility below 1 mile in blowing snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rainfall of one to three inches on frozen ground and into a snow pack with between 2 and 5 inches of liquid water equivalent resulted in considerable overland flooding.||One of the hardest hit areas was around Yankton, where a No Travel Advisory was issued for the city on March 13-14.  Businesses along north Highway 81 toward Yankton Mall had considerable ponding of water.  Water rescues were necessary as cars stalled in high water in low spots around Yankton.  Two people were also rescued from their inundated vehicle on Jim River Road east of Yankton. Water also got into power substations and caused spotty power outages and necessitated temporary evacuation of residents.||State government offices across the area were closed on March 14 due to the flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water was flowing across SD Highway 34/U.S. 81 west of 447th Avenue, just west of Junius. Other flooding resulted in damage to public infrastructure including county and township roads and culverts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High releases from upstream dams due to runoff from October snow melt continued flooding along the Missouri River below Fort Randall Dam.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A continuation of flooding from October, the Missouri River near Greenwood (gage site GRWS2) remained in major flood stage much of the month, and crested 2.27 feet above flood stage on November 5. Impacts were mainly limited to inundation of agricultural land, as well as the closing of numerous recreational boat ramps. The river remained in minor flood at the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A reinforcing jet streak dropped into a deep trough across the Northern Plains. Increasing warm advection in advance of the wave led to the development of a narrow frontal band, which was reinforced for a time during the late afternoon and evening of November 5 as the main jet streak dug into the region.  The snow band dropped from 2 to 4 inch accumulations from the mid-James River Valley into southern Minnesota. |In a narrow zone on the south side of the band of snowfall, a brief period of freezing rain produced a light glaze of ice.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A period of light freezing rain and mixed precipitation resulted in a glaze of ice accumulation on roadways, resulting in several non-injury accidents.  A period of light snowfall followed with accumulations reaching 4.0 inches 11 miles north of Colman and 3.5 inches at Flandreau.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusually strong and cold area of high pressure surged into the northern Plains in early March.  Winds were mainly a gusty 20 to 35 mph, and when coupled with temperatures from 10 below to 20 below zero, produced a long duration of wind chills colder than 20 below zero.  Coldest wind chills were recorded between 0400-0900 on March 3, reaching -51 near Oak Lake, -48 at Volga, -47 at Flandreau and White Lake, -46 at Colton and 5 miles south of Hendricks, -45 at Windom, Worthington and near Sioux Falls, and -44 at Huron, Brookings and Madison.  March 3 was one of the coldest March days on record for the area, with the coldest or second coldest March high temperatures on record at Huron, Sioux Falls, Mitchell and Sioux City.

Above normal snowmelt runoff from the winter along with snowmelt from a mid-April blizzard caused near historic rises on Lake Poinsett in southern Hamlin county. Lake Poinsett rose to 1,657.1 feet on April 27th which was just below the record of 1657.6 feet. The high lake levels combined with periodic strong winds and early ice breakup brought extensive damage to homes and outbuildings along the lake. The strong winds brought 3 to 4 foot waves crashing into and damaging structures. Some outbuildings were destroyed.  Extensive sandbagging took place to minimize the flooding. Several roads were also flooded and damaged. The high lake levels continued into May with ongoing property damage.

Snowmelt runoff brought the Moccasin Creek at Aberdeen above the flood stage of 6 feet on April 3rd. The creek rose to 7.2 feet on April 6th and then back to below flood stage on April 11th. Some roads were flooded along the creek with a housing development threatened on the north side of Aberdeen.
Snowmelt runoff caused the Big Sioux River at Florence to go slightly above the flood stage of 8 feet to 8.4 feet from April 18th to the 19th.
A sharp cool down along with high river levels resulted in rapid ice development and spotty ice jams, resulting in minor flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement confirmed that ice development on the Big Sioux River piled up on several bridges near the gage site and caused minor lowland flooding.  The Big Sioux River near Estelline (gage site BSES2) reached minor flood stage and crested at 1.20 feet above flood stage on November 13.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A continuation of minor flooding from October, the James River at Huron (gage site HURS2) crested at 2.46 feet above flood stage on November 26. Significant amounts of agricultural land remained flooded. Moderate flooding persisted at the end of the month.

Increasing warm advection in advance of the wave led to the development of a narrow frontal band, which was reinforced for a time during the late afternoon and evening of November 5 as the main jet streak dug into the region.  The snow band dropped from 2 to 4 inch accumulations from the mid-James River Valley into southern Minnesota. |In a narrow zone on the south side of the band of snowfall, a brief period of freezing rain produced a light glaze of ice.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light freezing rain created a glaze of ice on roadways late in the evening of November 5. One fatality (indirect) occurred when a vehicle lost control on an ice-covered bridge on Interstate 229 in Sioux Falls, rolled down an embankment and struck a tree.  A 16-year-old passenger in the vehicle was also severely injured (indirect).  In total, there were 39 accidents around Sioux Falls through the morning of November 6, with several more accidents along nearby sections of Interstates 29 and 90.  At least 4 additional injuries (indirect) occurred with these other accidents. Property damage costs are rough estimates.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Routing of snow melt water from upper portions of the James River Basin in North Dakota and northern South Dakota reached lower portions of the river basin late month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The James River near Scotland (gage site SCOS2) returned to flood stage, and crested on November 30 at 0.30 feet above flood stage. Impacts were generally inundation of agricultural lands near the river.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A historic flooding event unfolded as heavy rainfall drenched the area on March 13-14, 2019.  With frozen and impervious ground, full and rapid runoff was maximized. In addition to the rainfall, between 1 and 3 inches of snow water equivalent remained in the snow pack south of Interstate 90, with 3 to 6 inches of snow water equivalent north of Interstate 90. Fifteen river gage sites measured record stages around the area.  Winds gusted to 50 to 70 mph at times, and colder air filtering into the system at the end of the storm produced a light  accumulation of ice west of the James River and from 1 to 4 inches of snowfall in parts of southeast South Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow melt and heavy rainfall (measured 1.80 inches 5 miles west of Geddes) resulted in significant flooding of agricultural lands, as Platte Creek at Platte (gage site PCPS2) crested at 0.3 feet above flood stage on March 13.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snowmelt flooding, beginning in late March, continued throughout April on the Big Sioux River near Castlewood. A mid-April heavy snow/blizzard event added to the snowmelt runoff and flooding. The river remained well above the flood stage of 9 feet throughout April. The highest stage was moderate stage of 12.6 feet on March 28th. Some roads and cropland were flooded. The flooding continued into May.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A historic blizzard affected all of central and northeast South Dakota from April 11th into the 12th. The storm came in two waves. The first wave brought a band of moderate to heavy snow and thunder as it lifted from south to north across the region during the early morning hours of the 10th. The thunder snow with this first wave brought snowfall rates of 2 inches or more an hour with initial snowfall accumulations of 2 to 10 inches. There were some areas of light freezing rain from Pierre to Watertown in the early morning hours of the 10th.||The second wave of heavy snow and strong north winds were with the main surface low pressure area moving across the central plains.  The heavy snow in combination with winds gusting to 35 to 50 mph brought widespread blizzard conditions along with heavy drifting. At the storm's end, most locations received anywhere from 4 to 15 inches of snowfall with some locations reporting extraordinary snowfall amounts of 16 to 30 inches. ||The blizzard had wide ranging impacts across the region, mainly to cattle producers and roadways. Countless roads were blocked or impassable. Thousands of ranchers were affected. There were stranded herds of cows with countless calves buried in the snow (many lost). There were also some spotty power outages. Interstates 29 and 90 were closed, and most other area roads were designated by the DOT as no travel advised.  Many vehicles became stuck across the region with several rescues taking place. There were also several accidents reported. Schools were closed for two days along with state offices throughout central and northeast South Dakota.  With the ongoing flooding across the region from the expansive snowmelt from the winter, the additional snowmelt water from this blizzard would only exacerbate the widespread flooding across the region. Many counties declared disasters in March with several more counties declaring disasters in April for the flooding and the March blizzard. ||Snowfall amounts include 7 inches at Eureka and McLaughlin; 8 inches at Hayti; 9 inches at Miller; 10 inches at Timber Lake; 11 inches at Mobridge and Doland. A historic blizzard affected all of central and northeast South Dakota from April 11th into the 12th. The storm came in two waves. The first wave brought a band of moderate to heavy snow and thunder as it lifted from south to north across the region during the early morning hours of the 10th. The thunder snow with this first wave brought snowfall rates of 2 inches or more an hour with initial snowfall accumulations of 2 to 10 inches. There were some areas of light freezing rain from Pierre to Watertown in the early morning hours of the 10th.||The second wave of heavy snow and strong north winds were with the main surface low pressure area moving across the central plains.  The heavy snow in combination with winds gusting to 35 to 50 mph brought widespread blizzard conditions along with heavy drifting. At the storm's end, most locations received anywhere from 4 to 15 inches of snowfall with some locations reporting extraordinary snowfall amounts of 16 to 30 inches. ||The blizzard had wide ranging impacts across the region, mainly to cattle producers and roadways. Countless roads were blocked or impassable. Thousands of ranchers were affected. There were stranded herds of cows with countless calves buried in the snow (many lost). There were also some spotty power outages. Interstates 29 and 90 were closed, and most other area roads were designated by the DOT as no travel advised.  Many vehicles became stuck across the region with several rescues taking place. There were also several accidents reported. Schools were closed for two days along with state offices throughout central and northeast South Dakota.  With the ongoing flooding across the region from the expansive snowmelt from the winter, the additional snowmelt water from this blizzard would only exacerbate the widespread flooding across the region. Many counties declared disasters in March with several more counties declaring disasters in April for the flooding and the March blizzard. ||Snowfall amounts include 7 inches at Eureka and McLaughlin; 8 inches at Hayti; 9 inches at Miller; 10 inches at Timber Lake; 11 inches at Mobridge and Doland. Locations with 12 to 18 inches include 12 inches at Andover and Gann Valley; 14 inches at Aberdeen, Presho, Britton and Selby; 15 inches at Murdo; 16 inches at Pierre; 17 inches at Faulkton and Wilmot; and 18 inches at Webster, Kennebec, and Milbank. ||Snowfall amounts of over 18 inches include 19 inches at Sisseton, Reliance, and 7 miles east of Hayes; 21 inches at Orient; 22 inches at Summit, 2 miles northeast of Cresbard, Highmore, Hayti, and Bowdle; 23 inches at Gettysburg and Castlewood; 24 inches at Redfield and 5 miles northwest of Mission Ridge; 25 inches at Roscoe, Ipswich, Watertown, Miller, and 8 miles west of Mansfield; 26 inches at Clear Lake and Hoven; 29 inches at Clark; and 30 inches at Ree Heights.  The 25 inches at Watertown broke their three day record for snowfall set in March 1937.

Winter snowmelt flooding from late March along with snowmelt from a mid-April heavy snow/blizzard kept the Big Sioux River near Watertown flooded throughout April. The highest the river reached was 3 feet above the flood stage of 8 feet to moderate stage of 11 feet in late March.
A line of thunderstorms developed over eastern portions of Meade and Pennington Counties, producing strong wind gusts over portions of the area.

A severe thunderstorm developed over central Jackson County and tracked east-northeast into central South Dakota. The storm produced hail to quarter size along its track.
A thunderstorm briefly became severe and produced quarter sized hail north of Millboro.
A funnel cloud was spotted northeast of Fort Thompson.

A powerful low pressure system slowly moved from the four corners region northward through the Plains leading to prolonged rainfall across the region. This three-day rainfall event resulted in flooding on the White River in south central South Dakota across southern Jones and Lyman counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding of agricultural land occurred along the White River around the Highway 83 bridge south of Murdo. The river crested just over the flood stage of 14 feet to 14.8 feet on May 25th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal zone sagging slowly southward across the region interacted with a zone of slightly unstable air on its southern flank to produce a band of snowfall, which became heavy at times during several hours between 0800-1300CST.  Snowfall was at such a rate (frequent visibility of less than a half mile) that numerous non-injury accidents occurred for many locations near and north of Interstate 90.  Snowfall was generally from 3 to 5 inches over a 6 to 9 hour period.  Greatest snowfall amounts were reported at De Smet (7.5 inches), Iroquois (5.5 inches), Flandreau (5.3 inches), 5 miles northeast of Salem (5.2 inches), Wessington Springs (5.0 inches), 2 miles north of Chester (4.8 inches) and Mitchell (4.6 inches).

An early season winter storm brought snow and gusty winds to much of western and south central South Dakota. The heaviest snow fell across the northern Black Hills and portions of northwestern South Dakota, where eight to 12 inches were reported with some locally higher amounts. Wind gusts of 35 to 45 mph on the back side of the storm caused blowing and drifting snow, with greatly reduced visibility on the plains. Icy roads and gusty winds tipped over several high profile vehicles on Interstate 90 east of Rapid City.

Relentless rainfall during July 2019 brought basin averages from 6 to 10 inches north of Interstate 90 and 4 to 6 inches south of Interstate 90. As a result, flooding along the Big Sioux River either continued, or was renewed, during the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal zone remained locked in place under southwest flow aloft as a series of mid-level waves moved across the region over a three day period.  Widespread heavy rainfall resulted and amounts reached two-day records for several locations including Platte (6.29 inches), White Lake (6.28 inches), Bridgewater (8.05 inches), Montrose (5.63 inches), Alexandria (8.30 inches), Madison (7.63 inches), Howard (7.05 inches), 2 miles south of Winfred (7.01 inches), and 2 miles north of Chester (8.52 inches). Flooding, both river and areal, tallied nearly $17 million dollars in damages across southeast South Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding resulted in crop losses and damage to public infrastructure including county and township roads and culverts. Crop loss estimates provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

A frontal zone remained locked in place under southwest flow aloft as a series of mid-level waves moved across the region over a three day period.  Widespread heavy rainfall resulted and amounts reached two-day records for several locations including Platte (6.29 inches), White Lake (6.28 inches), Bridgewater (8.05 inches), Montrose (5.63 inches), Alexandria (8.30 inches), Madison (7.63 inches), Howard (7.05 inches), 2 miles south of Winfred (7.01 inches), and 2 miles north of Chester (8.52 inches). Flooding, both river and areal, tallied nearly $17 million dollars in damages across southeast South Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain from September 10-12 (as much as 8.81 inches 2 miles north of Chester, 7.36 inches 4 miles west northwest of Rutland, and 7.01 inches 2 miles south of Winfred) led to widespread areal flooding. Flooding was most significant around Madison, where ponding waters led to the evacuation of 30 people by boat and payloader. As many as 200 residences were inundated by water in Madison. Highway 34 was closed both east and west of Madison, Highway 19 was closed south of Madison, and travel was not advised across most of the county with water over most township and county roads. Lake Herman State Park was closed due to high water and remained so for most of the month. No wake zones were established on Lake Madison, Lake Herman, and Brant Lake. Other flooding resulted in the loss of crops and damage to public infrastructure including numerous county and township roads, culverts, and some bridges. Property damage costs are rough estimates. Crop loss estimates provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As the snowmelt continued into April, the flooding in late March on the James River and Turtle creek near Redfield expanded throughout Brown and Spink counties. Gauging stations on the James River from Columbia to Stratford to Ashton all went above flood stage at the beginning of April and continued through the end of April. The Snake Creek near Ashton also went above flood stage in early April and continued through the end of the month. An intense blizzard in mid-April also brought a substantial amount of water to exacerbate the flooding situation. Many roads and cropland were affected.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The James River flooded across Spink county throughout April at both gauging sites of Redfield and Ashton. Flooding on the James River near Redfield in late March continued to rise above the flood stage of 20 feet throughout April to major flood stage, nearly 27 feet, by April 22nd. The James River near Ashton rose above the flood stage of 13 feet on April 1st to major flood stage of 23.20 feet on April 23rd.  Agricultural land along with several roads along the river were flooded.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: This is a continuation of flooding from June 2019. There were several crests through July, but the most significant for the Big Sioux River at Akron (gage site AKRI4) was a crest 2.54 feet above flood stage on July 19. Significant flooding of crop and pasture land persisted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal zone sagging slowly southward across the region interacted with a zone of slightly unstable air on its southern flank to produce a band of snowfall, which became heavy at times during several hours between 0800-1300CST.  Snowfall was at such a rate (frequent visibility of less than a half mile) that numerous non-injury accidents occurred for many locations near and north of Interstate 90.  Snowfall was generally from 3 to 5 inches over a 6 to 9 hour period.  Greatest snowfall amounts were reported at De Smet (7.5 inches), Iroquois (5.5 inches), Flandreau (5.3 inches), 5 miles northeast of Salem (5.2 inches), Wessington Springs (5.0 inches), 2 miles north of Chester (4.8 inches) and Mitchell (4.6 inches).

Scattered thunderstorms developed mid to late afternoon along a surface boundary near the Minnesota-Iowa border which extended back into southeast South Dakota. Deep layer shear of 30-35 knots and up to 1500 J/kg MLCAPE led to rapidly developing storms which quickly became multi-cellular and organized in clusters.  One stronger updraft produced marginally severe hail.

High water levels from April continued throughout May and into June on Lake Poinsett. Above normal rainfall along with periods of strong winds in May kept lake levels high and created large waves which brought more damage to homes and flooded roads along the lake. Lake Poinsett rose to 1,657.1 feet on April 27th which was just below the record of 1657.6 feet.

An early season winter storm brought snow and gusty winds to much of western and south central South Dakota. The heaviest snow fell across the northern Black Hills and portions of northwestern South Dakota, where eight to 12 inches were reported with some locally higher amounts. Wind gusts of 35 to 45 mph on the back side of the storm caused blowing and drifting snow, with greatly reduced visibility on the plains. Icy roads and gusty winds tipped over several high profile vehicles on Interstate 90 east of Rapid City.

Above normal September rainfall brought the Big Sioux River near Watertown to above flood stage on September 11th. The river continued to flood throughout the rest of the month and into October.
Heavy rainfall brought the Turtle Creek near Redfield over the flood stage of 7 feet on September 15th. The creek rose to 8.4 feet on the 18th and then fell back below flood stage on September 23rd.

Heavy rainfall runoff brought the Big Sioux River near Castlewood to above the flood stage of 9 feet on September 13th. The river rose to 10.3 feet on the 14th before falling back to below flood stage on September 16th. Some lowland and agricultural land was flooded as a result.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A historic flooding event unfolded as heavy rainfall drenched the area on March 13-14, 2019.  With frozen and impervious ground, full and rapid runoff was maximized. In addition to the rainfall, between 1 and 3 inches of snow water equivalent remained in the snow pack south of Interstate 90, with 3 to 6 inches of snow water equivalent north of Interstate 90. Fifteen river gage sites measured record stages around the area.  Winds gusted to 50 to 70 mph at times, and colder air filtering into the system at the end of the storm produced a light  accumulation of ice west of the James River and from 1 to 4 inches of snowfall in parts of southeast South Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow melt and heavy rainfall (measured 2.01 inches 2 miles north of Chester) resulted in extensive flooding of agricultural land in the flood plain. Skunk Creek near Chester (gage site SCCS2) reached the second highest crest on record at 0.2 feet above flood stage on March 18.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A historic flooding event unfolded as heavy rainfall drenched the area on March 13-14, 2019.  With frozen and impervious ground, full and rapid runoff was maximized. In addition to the rainfall, between 1 and 3 inches of snow water equivalent remained in the snow pack south of Interstate 90, with 3 to 6 inches of snow water equivalent north of Interstate 90. Fifteen river gage sites measured record stages around the area.  Winds gusted to 50 to 70 mph at times, and colder air filtering into the system at the end of the storm produced a light  accumulation of ice west of the James River and from 1 to 4 inches of snowfall in parts of southeast South Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow melt and heavy rainfall (measured 2.74 inches near Hartford) resulted in extensive flooding of agricultural land in the flood plain. State Highway 38 east of Hartford was submerged several times at varying degrees during the period from March 14 to March 23. Skunk Creek near Hartford (gage site HTFS2) reached the highest crest on record at 3.29 feet above flood stage on March 14.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system moved across the region, bringing rain, snow, and strong northwest winds. The strongest winds were in the Rapid City area and parts of the southern Black Hills. Gusts over 80 mph in the Custer area caused damage, including the partial collapse of a building in downtown Custer.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor damage and power outages occurred in the Custer area. A building  in downtown Custer suffered more significant damage. There were no injuries.

Heavy rainfall runoff caused the Big Sioux River near Florence to go above and below flood stage several times from July 4th through July 10th. Flood stage is 8 feet and the highest the river rose to was 8.44 feet on July 5th.

A severe thunderstorm developed over the central Black Hills and moved east through the Rapid City area and southeastern Pennington County, producing wind gusts near 70 mph. Some minor wind damage was noted in northern Rapid City.

A severe thunderstorm developed over southwestern Custer County and moved east-southeast into north central Fall River County before weakening. The storm produced large hail from Dewey to near Hot Springs.

As the snowmelt continued into April, the flooding in late March on the James River and Turtle creek near Redfield expanded throughout Brown and Spink counties. Gauging stations on the James River from Columbia to Stratford to Ashton all went above flood stage at the beginning of April and continued through the end of April. The Snake Creek near Ashton also went above flood stage in early April and continued through the end of the month. An intense blizzard in mid-April also brought a substantial amount of water to exacerbate the flooding situation. Many roads and cropland were affected.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding on Turtle Creek in late March continued throughout April. The creek rose above the flood stage of 7 feet to major stage of 15.5 feet on April 1st. Snake Creek near Ashton rose above the flood stage of 11.5 feet on April 3rd to major stage of 16.3 feet by April 22nd. Both the Turtle and Snake Creeks continued to flood into May. Agricultural land along with several roads were flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal boundary provided focus for convective development across south central South Dakota during the very late afternoon and early evening as a strong impulse moved in the southwest flow aloft.  Initial discrete cells produced spotty hail and damaging wind, with MLCAPE values around 1500-2000 J/kg and 45-55 knots of deep-layer shear.  As cells spread eastward and developed into a line, the increase in low-level jet during the evening quickly increased the 0-3 km shear to 35-40 knots.  Three QLCS tornadoes occurred within the city limits of Sioux Falls.  In addition to the tornadoes, widespread wind damage to trees and spottier damage to residences were reported across much of the city.  A total of nine people were injured in the storms around Sioux Falls.  Repeated cells resulted in flash flooding by later evening and into the overnight hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down around one quarter mile east of the intersection of Interstates 29 and 229, near the intersection of 69th Street and Tallgrass Avenue.  The tornado tracked east-northeast for a quarter mile before it struck a building under construction, causing extensive damage to concrete and metal framing, and tossed a construction truck 200 yards into a tree.  The tornado then damaged the Avera Heart and Behavioral Hospital Complex, where cars were displaced up to 100 feet and most windows were broken along with roof and facade damage to the hospital buildings. Eight people were injured by flying glass on the hospital complex.  The tornado lifted about 400 yards to the east of the hospital. Average path width was estimated at 40 yards. Property damage costs are rough estimates.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal boundary provided focus for convective development across south central South Dakota during the very late afternoon and early evening as a strong impulse moved in the southwest flow aloft.  Initial discrete cells produced spotty hail and damaging wind, with MLCAPE values around 1500-2000 J/kg and 45-55 knots of deep-layer shear.  As cells spread eastward and developed into a line, the increase in low-level jet during the evening quickly increased the 0-3 km shear to 35-40 knots.  Three QLCS tornadoes occurred within the city limits of Sioux Falls.  In addition to the tornadoes, widespread wind damage to trees and spottier damage to residences were reported across much of the city.  A total of nine people were injured in the storms around Sioux Falls.  Repeated cells resulted in flash flooding by later evening and into the overnight hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Initial damage to a few power poles occurred as the tornado touched down at the intersection of 85th Street and Western Avenue in far southern portions of the Sioux Falls metro area.  The tornado tracked northeast for a quarter mile causing tree damage in a residential area before turning more northerly. The tornadic path was evident by scouring vegetation while moving through a wetland area, tossing a trampoline, and causing tree damage to adjacent residences.  As the tornado entered a residential area on the north end of the wetlands, more significant damage occurred to several single family residences, with loss of significant sections of roofs and walls, as well as numerous uprooted large trees.  At this point, the tornado turned northeast for the last quarter mile of its path, moving along La Quinta Street.  Two homes incurred more substantial damage with loss of exterior walls.  The tornado lifted before reaching Venita Avenue.  Average path width was estimated at 40 yards. Property damage costs are rough estimates.

A historic flooding event unfolded as heavy rainfall drenched the area on March 13-14, 2019.  With frozen and impervious ground, full and rapid runoff was maximized. In addition to the rainfall, between 1 and 3 inches of snow water equivalent remained in the snow pack south of Interstate 90, with 3 to 6 inches of snow water equivalent north of Interstate 90. Fifteen river gage sites measured record stages around the area.  Winds gusted to 50 to 70 mph at times, and colder air filtering into the system at the end of the storm produced a light  accumulation of ice west of the James River and from 1 to 4 inches of snowfall in parts of southeast South Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow melt and heavy rainfall (measured 2.01 inches 2 miles north of Chester) resulted in extensive flooding of agricultural land in the flood plain. Skunk Creek near Chester (gage site SCCS2) reached the second highest crest on record at 0.2 feet above flood stage on March 18.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated thunderstorm brought severe winds of sixty mph to parts of Faulk and Spink counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Estimated sixty mph winds took some shingles off a building and caused some minor corn damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Above normal rainfall in July resulted in the continuation of very high lake levels on Lake Poinsett throughout the month. The very high lake levels along with large waves from stronger winds continued to flood and damage structures around the lake. The high lake levels remained into August.

Elevated instability from 1000-2000 J/kg along with 40-50 kt deep-layer shear sustained organized convection pushing though southeast South Dakota, southwest Minnesota and northwest Iowa.  Winds averaged from 60 to 80 mph along the path of the large scale bow echo, producing significant damage to trees and power lines, and minor damage to buildings along the path.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Measured by RWIS station.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Close on the heels of the last winter storm, an amplified upper-air pattern brought another impulse out of the southwestern U.S. early on Friday, November 29 and pushed a shield of light mixed precipitation northeastward across the region with a light accumulation of ice.  As the main trough and area of low pressure organized on Saturday, November 30 across Nebraska and deepened as it moved into southern Wisconsin by the morning of Sunday, December 1, additional snowfall developed and was accompanied by strong and gusty northwest winds of 30 to 50 mph by later Saturday night into Sunday morning.||Snowfall totals over the period generally ranged from 6 to 16 inches west of the James River,  to less than one inch from northeast Nebraska and far southeast South Dakota toward southern portions of northwest Iowa.  Another mesoscale band of heavy snowfall occurred on Saturday, November 30 near the Minnesota-Iowa border with between 6 and 9 inches accumulation.||This storm continued into December 1, although a majority of accumulating snowfall ended between 0000 and 0900CST.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mixed precipitation, with occasional light freezing rain or freezing drizzle, began late afternoon on November 29, and resulted in up to a tenth of an inch ice accumulation through the early morning of Saturday, November 30. Snow developed again by early morning, with periods of moderate snowfall into early Sunday. Snowfall totals by early Sunday reached 2.2 inches 10 miles south of Emery and 1.0 inches at Alexandria .  In addition, winds gusted to 47 mph at Mitchell and 38 mph near Alexandria, with spotty reduction in visibility below 1 mile in blowing snow.  Travel was not recommended by late evening on Saturday, November 30.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A historic blizzard affected all of central and northeast South Dakota from April 11th into the 12th. The storm came in two waves. The first wave brought a band of moderate to heavy snow and thunder as it lifted from south to north across the region during the early morning hours of the 10th. The thunder snow with this first wave brought snowfall rates of 2 inches or more an hour with initial snowfall accumulations of 2 to 10 inches. There were some areas of light freezing rain from Pierre to Watertown in the early morning hours of the 10th.||The second wave of heavy snow and strong north winds were with the main surface low pressure area moving across the central plains.  The heavy snow in combination with winds gusting to 35 to 50 mph brought widespread blizzard conditions along with heavy drifting. At the storm's end, most locations received anywhere from 4 to 15 inches of snowfall with some locations reporting extraordinary snowfall amounts of 16 to 30 inches. ||The blizzard had wide ranging impacts across the region, mainly to cattle producers and roadways. Countless roads were blocked or impassable. Thousands of ranchers were affected. There were stranded herds of cows with countless calves buried in the snow (many lost). There were also some spotty power outages. Interstates 29 and 90 were closed, and most other area roads were designated by the DOT as no travel advised.  Many vehicles became stuck across the region with several rescues taking place. There were also several accidents reported. Schools were closed for two days along with state offices throughout central and northeast South Dakota.  With the ongoing flooding across the region from the expansive snowmelt from the winter, the additional snowmelt water from this blizzard would only exacerbate the widespread flooding across the region. Many counties declared disasters in March with several more counties declaring disasters in April for the flooding and the March blizzard. ||Snowfall amounts include 7 inches at Eureka and McLaughlin; 8 inches at Hayti; 9 inches at Miller; 10 inches at Timber Lake; 11 inches at Mobridge and Doland. Locations with 12 to 18 inches include 12 inches at Andover and Gann Valley; 14 inches at Aberdeen, Presho, Britton and Selby; 15 inches at Murdo; 16 inches at Pierre; 17 inches at Faulkton and Wilmot; and 18 inches at Webster, Kennebec, and Milbank. ||Snowfall amounts of over 18 inches include 19 inches at Sisseton, Reliance, and 7 miles east of Hayes; 21 inches at Orient; 22 inches at Summit, 2 miles northeast of Cresbard, Highmore, Hayti, and Bowdle; 23 inches at Gettysburg and Castlewood; 24 inches at Redfield and 5 miles northwest of Mission Ridge; 25 inches at Roscoe, Ipswich, Watertown, Miller, and 8 miles west of Mansfield; 26 inches at Clear Lake and Hoven; 29 inches at Clark; and 30 inches at Ree Heights.  The 25 inches at Watertown broke their three day record for snowfall set in March 1937.

The first wave brought a band of moderate to heavy snow and thunder as it lifted from south to north across the region during the early morning hours of the 10th. The thunder snow with this first wave brought snowfall rates of 2 inches or more an hour with initial snowfall accumulations of 2 to 10 inches. There were some areas of light freezing rain from Pierre to Watertown in the early morning hours of the 10th.||The second wave of heavy snow and strong north winds were with the main surface low pressure area moving across the central plains.  The heavy snow in combination with winds gusting to 35 to 50 mph brought widespread blizzard conditions along with heavy drifting. At the storm's end, most locations received anywhere from 4 to 15 inches of snowfall with some locations reporting extraordinary snowfall amounts of 16 to 30 inches. ||The blizzard had wide ranging impacts across the region, mainly to cattle producers and roadways. Countless roads were blocked or impassable. Thousands of ranchers were affected. There were stranded herds of cows with countless calves buried in the snow (many lost). There were also some spotty power outages. Interstates 29 and 90 were closed, and most other area roads were designated by the DOT as no travel advised.  Many vehicles became stuck across the region with several rescues taking place. There were also several accidents reported. Schools were closed for two days along with state offices throughout central and northeast South Dakota.  With the ongoing flooding across the region from the expansive snowmelt from the winter, the additional snowmelt water from this blizzard would only exacerbate the widespread flooding across the region. Many counties declared disasters in March with several more counties declaring disasters in April for the flooding and the March blizzard. ||Snowfall amounts include 7 inches at Eureka and McLaughlin; 8 inches at Hayti; 9 inches at Miller; 10 inches at Timber Lake; 11 inches at Mobridge and Doland. Locations with 12 to 18 inches include 12 inches at Andover and Gann Valley; 14 inches at Aberdeen, Presho, Britton and Selby; 15 inches at Murdo; 16 inches at Pierre; 17 inches at Faulkton and Wilmot; and 18 inches at Webster, Kennebec, and Milbank. ||Snowfall amounts of over 18 inches include 19 inches at Sisseton, Reliance, and 7 miles east of Hayes; 21 inches at Orient; 22 inches at Summit, 2 miles northeast of Cresbard, Highmore, Hayti, and Bowdle; 23 inches at Gettysburg and Castlewood; 24 inches at Redfield and 5 miles northwest of Mission Ridge; 25 inches at Roscoe, Ipswich, Watertown, Miller, and 8 miles west of Mansfield; 26 inches at Clear Lake and Hoven; 29 inches at Clark; and 30 inches at Ree Heights. A frontal boundary provided focus for convective development across south central South Dakota during the very late afternoon and early evening as a strong impulse moved in the southwest flow aloft.  Initial discrete cells produced spotty hail and damaging wind, with MLCAPE values around 1500-2000 J/kg and 45-55 knots of deep-layer shear.  As cells spread eastward and developed into a line, the increase in low-level jet during the evening quickly increased the 0-3 km shear to 35-40 knots.  Three QLCS tornadoes occurred within the city limits of Sioux Falls.  In addition to the tornadoes, widespread wind damage to trees and spottier damage to residences were reported across much of the city.  A total of nine people were injured in the storms around Sioux Falls.  Repeated cells resulted in flash flooding by later evening and into the overnight hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Shortly after touchdown just southeast of O'Gorman High School, the tornado struck a multi-story apartment building and removed a majority of the roof.  Numerous trees were uprooted in residential areas north of 41st Street as the tornado continued in a general southeast direction. The tornado crossed 41st Street into a more commercial district, causing significant structural damage to several businesses, including the removal of the roof from one strip mall and exterior walls from another building.  After another quarter mile of travel to the southeast, the tornado destroyed an apartment building garage.  The tornado lifted before reaching the corner of 49th Street and Western Avenue. Average path width was 50 yards. Property damage costs are rough estimates.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal zone remained locked in place under southwest flow aloft as a series of mid-level waves moved across the region over a three day period.  Widespread heavy rainfall resulted and amounts reached two-day records for several locations including Platte (6.29 inches), White Lake (6.28 inches), Bridgewater (8.05 inches), Montrose (5.63 inches), Alexandria (8.30 inches), Madison (7.63 inches), Howard (7.05 inches), 2 miles south of Winfred (7.01 inches), and 2 miles north of Chester (8.52 inches). Flooding, both river and areal, tallied nearly $17 million dollars in damages across southeast South Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: September 10-12 brought up to 8.30 inches of rainfall at Alexandria, producing widespread areal flooding.  The spillway at Hanson Lake washed out and damaged the roadway below the spillway. Numerous state, county and township roads were impassable due to water, including State Highway 42 east of Ethan. The property damage costs include estimates for public property losses. Flooding also resulted in crop losses across the area. Crop loss estimates provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system moved across the region, bringing rain to much of the plains. Temperatures became cold enough for snow across the northern and central Black Hills and northern foothills. Strong northerly winds helped to produce heavy upslope snow, especially across the higher elevations. Accumulations of one to two feet were reported from the Lead and Deadwood areas to Cheyenne Crossing. Another area of heavy snow developed from the Hill City area to Deerfield, where as much as a foot of snow was reported.

A strong low pressure system moved across the region, bringing rain, snow, and strong northwest winds. The strongest winds were in the Rapid City area and parts of the southern Black Hills. Gusts over 80 mph in the Custer area caused damage, including the partial collapse of a building in downtown Custer.

Close on the heels of the last winter storm, an amplified upper-air pattern brought another impulse out of the southwestern U.S. early on Friday, November 29 and pushed a shield of light mixed precipitation northeastward across the region with a light accumulation of ice.  As the main trough and area of low pressure organized on Saturday, November 30 across Nebraska and deepened as it moved into southern Wisconsin by the morning of Sunday, December 1, additional snowfall developed and was accompanied by strong and gusty northwest winds of 30 to 50 mph by later Saturday night into Sunday morning.||Snowfall totals over the period generally ranged from 6 to 16 inches west of the James River,  to less than one inch from northeast Nebraska and far southeast South Dakota toward southern portions of northwest Iowa.  Another mesoscale band of heavy snowfall occurred on Saturday, November 30 near the Minnesota-Iowa border with between 6 and 9 inches accumulation.||This storm continued into December 1, although a majority of accumulating snowfall ended between 0000 and 0900CST.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mixed precipitation, with occasional light freezing rain or freezing drizzle, began late afternoon on November 29, and resulted in a few hundredths of an inch ice accumulation through the early morning of Saturday, November 30. Snow developed again by early morning, with periods of snowfall into early Sunday. Snowfall totals by early Sunday reached 3.3 inches at Tyndall.  In addition, winds gusted to 42 mph near Tyndall, with spotty reduction in visibility below 1 mile in blowing snow.  Travel was not recommended by late evening on Saturday, November 30.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A historic flooding event unfolded as heavy rainfall drenched the area on March 13-14, 2019.  With frozen and impervious ground, full and rapid runoff was maximized. In addition to the rainfall, between 1 and 3 inches of snow water equivalent remained in the snow pack south of Interstate 90, with 3 to 6 inches of snow water equivalent north of Interstate 90. Fifteen river gage sites measured record stages around the area.  Winds gusted to 50 to 70 mph at times, and colder air filtering into the system at the end of the storm produced a light  accumulation of ice west of the James River and from 1 to 4 inches of snowfall in parts of southeast South Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow melt and heavy rainfall (measured 3.13 inches at Bridgewater and 2.75 inches at Montrose) resulted in extensive flooding of agricultural lands and inundation of numerous county and township roads (including bridges on 455th and 456th Avenue east of Parker). Water briefly flowed across State Highway 19 east of Parker.  The East Fork of the Vermillion River near Parker (gage site VRPS2) reached the highest stage on record cresting 4.09 feet above flood stage on March 14.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snowmelt and heavy rain flooding from late March and April along with above normal rainfall in May kept the James River in flood throughout May across most of Brown and Spink counties. Several roads and agricultural land remained flooded.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The James River at Columbia and Stratford reached their highest stages in late April close to 19 feet. Flood stage at Stratford is 14 feet and 13 feet at Columbia.

An unusually strong and cold area of high pressure surged into the northern Plains in early March.  Winds were mainly a gusty 20 to 35 mph, and when coupled with temperatures from 10 below to 20 below zero, produced a long duration of wind chills colder than 20 below zero.  Coldest wind chills were recorded between 0400-0900 on March 3, reaching -51 near Oak Lake, -48 at Volga, -47 at Flandreau and White Lake, -46 at Colton and 5 miles south of Hendricks, -45 at Windom, Worthington and near Sioux Falls, and -44 at Huron, Brookings and Madison.  March 3 was one of the coldest March days on record for the area, with the coldest or second coldest March high temperatures on record at Huron, Sioux Falls, Mitchell and Sioux City.

The first wave brought a band of moderate to heavy snow and thunder as it lifted from south to north across the region during the early morning hours of the 10th. Many vehicles became stuck across the region with several rescues taking place. There were also several accidents reported. Schools were closed for two days along with state offices throughout central and northeast South Dakota.  With the ongoing flooding across the region from the expansive snowmelt from the winter, the additional snowmelt water from this blizzard would only exacerbate the widespread flooding across the region.
