Close on the heels of the last winter storm, an amplified upper-air pattern brought another impulse out of the southwestern U.S. early on Friday, November 29 and pushed a shield of light mixed precipitation northeastward across the region with a light accumulation of ice.  As the main trough and area of low pressure organized on Saturday, November 30 across Nebraska and deepened as it moved into southern Wisconsin by the morning of Sunday, December 1, additional snowfall developed and was accompanied by strong and gusty northwest winds of 30 to 50 mph by later Saturday night into Sunday morning.||Snowfall totals over the period generally ranged from 6 to 16 inches west of the James River,  to less than one inch from northeast Nebraska and far southeast South Dakota toward southern portions of northwest Iowa.  Another mesoscale band of heavy snowfall occurred on Saturday, November 30 near the Minnesota-Iowa border with between 6 and 9 inches accumulation.||This storm continued into December 1, although a majority of accumulating snowfall ended between 0000 and 0900CST.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A period of light snow developed during the evening of Thursday, November 28 bringing a light accumulation before tapering off. Another surge of mixed-type precipitation developed the following afternoon, with occasional light freezing rain or freezing drizzle before changing back to snow during the evening of November 30. Up to a tenth of an inch ice accumulation occurred through through November 30, followed by snowfall of 2.0 inches at Flandreau by early Sunday. In addition, winds gusted to 40 mph near Ward with a brief reduction in visibility below 1 mile in blowing snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal zone remained locked in place under southwest flow aloft as a series of mid-level waves moved across the region over a three day period.  Widespread heavy rainfall resulted and amounts reached two-day records for several locations including Platte (6.29 inches), White Lake (6.28 inches), Bridgewater (8.05 inches), Montrose (5.63 inches), Alexandria (8.30 inches), Madison (7.63 inches), Howard (7.05 inches), 2 miles south of Winfred (7.01 inches), and 2 miles north of Chester (8.52 inches). Flooding, both river and areal, tallied nearly $17 million dollars in damages across southeast South Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding resulted in crop losses and damage to public infrastructure including county and township roads and culverts. Crop loss estimates provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

An unusually strong and cold area of high pressure surged into the northern Plains in early March.  Winds were mainly a gusty 20 to 35 mph, and when coupled with temperatures from 10 below to 20 below zero, produced a long duration of wind chills colder than 20 below zero.  Coldest wind chills were recorded between 0400-0900 on March 3, reaching -51 near Oak Lake, -48 at Volga, -47 at Flandreau and White Lake, -46 at Colton and 5 miles south of Hendricks, -45 at Windom, Worthington and near Sioux Falls, and -44 at Huron, Brookings and Madison.  March 3 was one of the coldest March days on record for the area, with the coldest or second coldest March high temperatures on record at Huron, Sioux Falls, Mitchell and Sioux City.

Widespread heavy rainfall resulted and amounts reached two-day records for several locations including Platte (6.29 inches), White Lake (6.28 inches), Bridgewater (8.05 inches), Montrose (5.63 inches), Alexandria (8.30 inches), Madison (7.63 inches), Howard (7.05 inches), 2 miles south of Winfred (7.01 inches), and 2 miles north of Chester (8.52 inches). EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread areal flooding resulted after excessive rainfall from September 10-12, which brought 6.40 inches to Platte and 6.31 inches 2 miles northeast of Academy. Flooding resulted in crop losses and damage to public infrastructure including numerous county and township roads, culverts, and some bridges. Access was cut off to several homes with water rising to 6 to 12 inches deep across parts of Lake Andes. Crop loss estimates provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal zone sagging slowly southward across the region interacted with a zone of slightly unstable air on its southern flank to produce a band of snowfall, which became heavy at times during several hours between 0800-1300CST.  Snowfall was at such a rate (frequent visibility of less than a half mile) that numerous non-injury accidents occurred for many locations near and north of Interstate 90.  Snowfall was generally from 3 to 5 inches over a 6 to 9 hour period.  Greatest snowfall amounts were reported at De Smet (7.5 inches), Iroquois (5.5 inches), Flandreau (5.3 inches), 5 miles northeast of Salem (5.2 inches), Wessington Springs (5.0 inches), 2 miles north of Chester (4.8 inches) and Mitchell (4.6 inches).

Winter snowmelt flooding from late March along with snowmelt from a mid-April heavy snow/blizzard kept the Big Sioux River at Watertown and Lake Kampeska flooded into early April. The high lake levels continued to threaten homes, especially when the winds increased.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Late March flooding continued into early April on the Big Sioux River at Watertown, reaching its highest level in late March of 11.8 feet or almost a foot above flood stage. The gauge at this location fell below flood stage on April 5th and then rose again slightly above flood stage from the 19th to the 25th. Lake Kampeska slowly fell to below the flood stage of 21 feet on April 4th. The lake's highest level was in late March at 21.76 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As the snowmelt continued into April, the flooding in late March on the James River and Turtle creek near Redfield expanded throughout Brown and Spink counties. Gauging stations on the James River from Columbia to Stratford to Ashton all went above flood stage at the beginning of April and continued through the end of April. The Snake Creek near Ashton also went above flood stage in early April and continued through the end of the month. An intense blizzard in mid-April also brought a substantial amount of water to exacerbate the flooding situation. Many roads and cropland were affected.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The James River near Columbia rose above the flood stage of 13 feet on April 2nd to major stage of 19 feet by April 21st. The river gauge at Stratford rose above the flood stage of 14 feet on April 3rd to major stage of 18.7 feet by the 19th of April. The James River continued to flood into May. Several roads along with agricultural land were affected.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snowmelt runoff brought the Elm River at Westport and Ordway above flood stage in the first few days of April. The Elm River near Westport went above the flood stage of 14 feet on April 1st, and then rose quickly to major flood stage of nearly 21 feet on April 3rd before falling back to below flood stage on April 12th. The river gauge at Ordway rose above the flood stage of 13 feet on April 2nd, reached 15.7 feet on April 4th, and then fell back below flood stage on April 11th.

A frontal zone remained locked in place under southwest flow aloft as a series of mid-level waves moved across the region over a three day period.  Widespread heavy rainfall resulted and amounts reached two-day records for several locations including Platte (6.29 inches), White Lake (6.28 inches), Bridgewater (8.05 inches), Montrose (5.63 inches), Alexandria (8.30 inches), Madison (7.63 inches), Howard (7.05 inches), 2 miles south of Winfred (7.01 inches), and 2 miles north of Chester (8.52 inches). Flooding, both river and areal, tallied nearly $17 million dollars in damages across southeast South Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Excessive rainfall from September 10-12 (8.27 inches in Bridgewater, 7.14 inches 6 miles northwest of Montrose, 6.08 inches in Montrose) caused extensive areal flooding.  Hardest hit were areas near Montrose, where rising waters in the East Fork of the Vermillion River encroached on eastern portions of the city and prompted a mandatory evacuation of areas along First Avenue from Highway 38 to Main Street on September 12.  Numerous county and township roads were washed out. Interstate 90 was closed from Humboldt to Bridgewater on September 12 and portions remained closed through September 16. State Highway 38 was closed from Montrose to Salem. Significant ponding of water also occurred in Spencer and Bridgewater.  Lake Vermillion Recreational Area was closed due to flooding and remained closed through September 26 while damage was cleaned up. Property damage costs are rough estimates. Flooding also resulted in crop losses across the area. Crop loss estimates provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

Snowmelt and heavy rain flooding from late March and April along with above normal rainfall in May kept the James River in flood throughout May across most of Brown and Spink counties. Several roads and agricultural land remained flooded.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The James River at Ashton flooded throughout May while the James River at Redfield flooded early on in May and again in late May. The highest stages at both locations occurred in late April. The James River at Redfield rose to nearly 27 feet in late April while the James River at Ashton rose to just over 23 feet in late April. The flood stage at Redfield is 20 feet and 13 feet at Ashton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Close on the heels of the last winter storm, an amplified upper-air pattern brought another impulse out of the southwestern U.S. early on Friday, November 29 and pushed a shield of light mixed precipitation northeastward across the region with a light accumulation of ice.  As the main trough and area of low pressure organized on Saturday, November 30 across Nebraska and deepened as it moved into southern Wisconsin by the morning of Sunday, December 1, additional snowfall developed and was accompanied by strong and gusty northwest winds of 30 to 50 mph by later Saturday night into Sunday morning.||Snowfall totals over the period generally ranged from 6 to 16 inches west of the James River,  to less than one inch from northeast Nebraska and far southeast South Dakota toward southern portions of northwest Iowa.  Another mesoscale band of heavy snowfall occurred on Saturday, November 30 near the Minnesota-Iowa border with between 6 and 9 inches accumulation.||This storm continued into December 1, although a majority of accumulating snowfall ended between 0000 and 0900CST.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mixed precipitation, with occasional light freezing rain or freezing drizzle, began late afternoon on November 29, and resulted in a few hundredths of an inch ice accumulation through the early morning of Saturday, November 30. Snow developed again by early morning, with periods of snowfall into early Sunday. Snowfall totals by early Sunday reached 4.5 inches at Menno.  In addition, winds gusted to 45 mph near Tripp, with spotty reduction in visibility below 1 mile in blowing snow.  Travel was not recommended by late evening on Saturday, November 30.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A historic blizzard affected all of central and northeast South Dakota from April 11th into the 12th. The storm came in two waves. The first wave brought a band of moderate to heavy snow and thunder as it lifted from south to north across the region during the early morning hours of the 10th. The thunder snow with this first wave brought snowfall rates of 2 inches or more an hour with initial snowfall accumulations of 2 to 10 inches. There were some areas of light freezing rain from Pierre to Watertown in the early morning hours of the 10th.||The second wave of heavy snow and strong north winds were with the main surface low pressure area moving across the central plains.  The heavy snow in combination with winds gusting to 35 to 50 mph brought widespread blizzard conditions along with heavy drifting. At the storm's end, most locations received anywhere from 4 to 15 inches of snowfall with some locations reporting extraordinary snowfall amounts of 16 to 30 inches. ||The blizzard had wide ranging impacts across the region, mainly to cattle producers and roadways. Countless roads were blocked or impassable. Thousands of ranchers were affected. There were stranded herds of cows with countless calves buried in the snow (many lost). There were also some spotty power outages. Interstates 29 and 90 were closed, and most other area roads were designated by the DOT as no travel advised.  Many vehicles became stuck across the region with several rescues taking place. There were also several accidents reported. Schools were closed for two days along with state offices throughout central and northeast South Dakota.  With the ongoing flooding across the region from the expansive snowmelt from the winter, the additional snowmelt water from this blizzard would only exacerbate the widespread flooding across the region. Many counties declared disasters in March with several more counties declaring disasters in April for the flooding and the March blizzard. ||Snowfall amounts include 7 inches at Eureka and McLaughlin; 8 inches at Hayti; 9 inches at Miller; 10 inches at Timber Lake; 11 inches at Mobridge and Doland. Locations with 12 to 18 inches include 12 inches at Andover and Gann Valley; 14 inches at Aberdeen, Presho, Britton and Selby; 15 inches at Murdo; 16 inches at Pierre; 17 inches at Faulkton and Wilmot; and 18 inches at Webster, Kennebec, and Milbank. ||Snowfall amounts of over 18 inches include 19 inches at Sisseton, Reliance, and 7 miles east of Hayes; 21 inches at Orient; 22 inches at Summit, 2 miles northeast of Cresbard, Highmore, Hayti, and Bowdle; 23 inches at Gettysburg and Castlewood; 24 inches at Redfield and 5 miles northwest of Mission Ridge; 25 inches at Roscoe, Ipswich, Watertown, Miller, and 8 miles west of Mansfield; 26 inches at Clear Lake and Hoven; 29 inches at Clark; and 30 inches at Ree Heights.  The 25 inches at Watertown broke their three day record for snowfall set in March 1937.

Two periods of heavy rainfall totaling 4 to nearly 8 inches occurred across Jones and Lyman counties at the end of May leading to widespread flooding. The first three-day period of heavy rainfall occurred as the result of a powerful low pressure system that slowly moved from the four corners region northward through the Plains.  The second period was the result of a series of disturbances associated with a persistent area of low pressure over the western half of the U.S. that produced several rounds of heavy rain. Both of these rainfalls, coupled with already saturated soils, resulted in widespread flooding across both counties particularly on Medicine Creek.||Numerous roads were flooded and damaged across the region with several culverts washed out. Many farms and homes were also flooded and damaged with many people unable to get out due to washed out roads. A home 10 miles west of Presho had 4 feet of water flow right through it. Presho and Kennebec received the brunt of the flooding on the Medicine Creek with a lot of property damage. Also, some cattle were lost in the flooding. ||Rainfall amounts from the two periods of rain were substantial ranging from 4 to nearly 8 inches. Some of the rainfall amounts include 4.40 inches 6 miles southwest of Oacoma; 5.67 inches 5 miles north of Vivian; 6.10 inches 14 miles northwest of Presho; 6.82 inches at Kennebec; 7.72 inches at Murdo; and 7.96 inches 3 miles northwest of Okaton.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mixed precipitation, with occasional light freezing rain or freezing drizzle, began late afternoon on November 29, and resulted in up to a tenth of an inch ice accumulation through the early morning of November 30. Snow developed again by early morning, becoming heavy at times during the afternoon. Snowfall totals by early Sunday reached 8.0 inches 5 miles west of Armour.  In addition, winds gusted to 48 mph near Plankinton and 39 mph at Wagner with areas of blowing snow with visibility of a half mile or less at times through Sunday morning. Travel was not recommended by late afternoon of November 30.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of disturbances associated with a persistent area of low pressure over the western half of the U.S. produced several rounds of heavy rain. This rainfall, coupled with saturated soils from a wet early spring and flooding over the previous week, led to more flooding on the White River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding of agricultural land occurred along the White River around the Highway 47 bridge south of Reliance. The river crested a few feet over the flood stage of 15 feet to 19 feet on May 29th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall in late May brought the Little Minnesota River near Peever quickly above and below flood stage on May 23rd. The river rose to six tenths of a foot above flood stage to 17.6 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Relentless rainfall during July 2019 brought basin averages from 6 to 10 inches north of Interstate 90 and 4 to 6 inches south of Interstate 90.  As a result, flooding along the Big Sioux River either continued, or was renewed, during the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Big Sioux River near Estelline (gage site BSES2) crested at moderate levels 2.22 feet above flood stage on July 10 and again 1.83 feet above flood stage on July 16, causing significant flooding of crop and pasture land.  Minor flooding continued at the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Relentless rainfall during July 2019 brought basin averages from 6 to 10 inches north of Interstate 90 and 4 to 6 inches south of Interstate 90.  As a result, flooding along the Big Sioux River either continued, or was renewed, during the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Big Sioux River near Bruce (gage site BRCS2) returned quickly to flood stage to start the month. Much of the month was spent at moderate flood stage, with a crest 1.81 feet above flood stage on July 3, followed by the fifth highest crest on record at 2.55 feet above flood stage on July 11. Significant flooding of crop and pasture land occurred throughout the month, and several county roads were inundated by water. Minor flooding continued at the end of the month.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Big Sioux River near Brookings (gage site BRKS2) rapidly returned above flood stage early in the month. A crest of 3.24 feet above flood stage occurred around 1900CST on July 13, with a secondary crest of 1.56 feet above flood stage on July 31.|Significant amounts of agricultural land remained flooded. A few rural roads near the gage site were also flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal zone remained locked in place under southwest flow aloft as a series of mid-level waves moved across the region over a three day period.  Widespread heavy rainfall resulted and amounts reached two-day records for several locations including Platte (6.29 inches), White Lake (6.28 inches), Bridgewater (8.05 inches), Montrose (5.63 inches), Alexandria (8.30 inches), Madison (7.63 inches), Howard (7.05 inches), 2 miles south of Winfred (7.01 inches), and 2 miles north of Chester (8.52 inches). Flooding, both river and areal, tallied nearly $17 million dollars in damages across southeast South Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall from September 10-12 totalling 7 to 8 inches near Mitchell led to widespread areal flooding. Travel was significantly hampered across most of the county, including the closure of Interstate 90. Smaller creeks and ponding resulted in the closure of most township and county roads.  Five miles south of Mitchell, a bridge over Enemy Creek was washed out, requiring a swift water rescue of one person who was overwhelmed by the current. Three residents located one-half mile east of this bridge were also evacuated from waist deep waters.  This bridge was one of nine damaged across the county.  A no-wake order was placed on Lake Mitchell due to extremely high water. Significant street flooding occurred around Mitchell for the better part of three days. Property damage costs are rough estimates. Flooding also resulted in the loss of crops. Crop loss estimates provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

After less than 24 hours below flood stage during July 14-15, a secondary crest of 3.92 feet above flood stage occurred on July 19, resulting in significant flooding of crop and pasture land.

A strong jet dug into the southwestern U.S. on November 25 and ejected into the plains by early November 26, where a surface low pressure area began to organize in the southern High Plains.  The mid-level trough and surface cyclone intensified rapidly lifting toward the western Great Lakes by the morning of November 27. With sharp baroclinicity in place, a band of moderate to heavy snowfall from 4 to 9 inches blanketed far southeast South Dakota, northeast Nebraska, northwest Iowa, and much of southwest Minnesota. The snow was accompanied by northwest winds with gusts as high as 35 to 50 mph near and east of Interstate 29, which resulted in periodic visibility from one-quarter to one-half mile. Some heavier snowfall amounts across southeast South Dakota included 6.5 inches 6 miles southeast of Centerville, 6 inches at Yankton, 5.6 inches 2 miles north of Chester, 5.5 inches 6 miles northwest of Montrose and Menno, 5.3 inches 4 miles northwest of Renner, and 5.0 inches at Hurley.

A surface low pressure area and attached cold front triggered storms across north central South Dakota which became quickly severe. The severe storms became destructive as they moved southeast from the late afternoon through the evening hours. Extensive crop and property damage resulted from winds up to eighty mph along with large hail to baseball size or larger across portions of Corson, Dewey, Walworth, Potter, Sully, Hughes, and Lyman counties. Two rounds of severe storms about an hour apart affected areas roughly from Blunt to Lower Brule to Iona. Siding, windows, and vehicles were damaged along the path of the storms. Some big round hay bales were also rolled for quite a distance by the severe winds. ||The most destructive path of grassland and crop damage occurred from Little Eagle in Corson county southeast to near Onida in Sully county.  Several thousand acres of crops and grassland were destroyed along an estimated 70 mile path which varied from 2 to 6 miles wide. Satellite imagery showed the path of destruction.

A very unstable atmosphere was in place across south central and southeast South Dakota with around 2000 J/kg MLCAPE.  As a strong upper-level wave dropped from Montana into North Dakota, storms initiated after 1400CST along a pre-frontal surface trough across north central South Dakota.  As storms moved into an environment with deep-layer effective shear of 40 to 50 knots, the southernmost cells produced very large hail as well as a brief tornado near Burke later in the evening. Two people were injured when the garage they were moving cars into at the time of the storm was destroyed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado developed and touched down in the heart of town near Washington and 7th streets, causing damage to several trees at the touchdown point.  Shortly after, the tornado severely damaged several structures while tracking south, including leveling several buildings at the lumber yard, destroying the civic center, and causing significant damage to the school.  The start of the school year was delayed by two weeks due to extent of the damage. Two people were (directly) injured by debris when a garage collapsed on them while moving vehicles to safety. Numerous power transmission poles and lines were removed by the tornado.  Around 2 miles south southeast of Burke, the tornado struck a residence causing severe damage to the roof.  Shortly thereafter, the tornado lifted before reaching the intersection of 295th Street and 345th Avenue after traveling a bit less than 4 miles.  The path width reached a maximum of 75 yards within the city of Burke. Up to 3000 trees were damaged by the tornado in and near Burke. Property damage costs are rough estimates.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Close on the heels of the last winter storm, an amplified upper-air pattern brought another impulse out of the southwestern U.S. early on Friday, November 29 and pushed a shield of light mixed precipitation northeastward across the region with a light accumulation of ice.  As the main trough and area of low pressure organized on Saturday, November 30 across Nebraska and deepened as it moved into southern Wisconsin by the morning of Sunday, December 1, additional snowfall developed and was accompanied by strong and gusty northwest winds of 30 to 50 mph by later Saturday night into Sunday morning.||Snowfall totals over the period generally ranged from 6 to 16 inches west of the James River,  to less than one inch from northeast Nebraska and far southeast South Dakota toward southern portions of northwest Iowa.  Another mesoscale band of heavy snowfall occurred on Saturday, November 30 near the Minnesota-Iowa border with between 6 and 9 inches accumulation.||This storm continued into December 1, although a majority of accumulating snowfall ended between 0000 and 0900CST.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mixed precipitation, with occasional light freezing rain or freezing drizzle, began late afternoon on November 29, and resulted in up to several hundredths of an inch ice accumulation through the early evening of November 29. Snow developed again overnight, becoming heavy at times Saturday. Snowfall totals by early Sunday reached 10.2 inches 8 miles south southwest of Chamberlain, 10.0 inches 5 miles south of Chamberlain, and 8 inches 2 miles south of Pukwana.  In addition, winds gusted to 38 mph near Kimball with areas of blowing snow with visibility of a half mile or less through Sunday morning. Travel was not recommended by late afternoon of November 30.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow melt runoff caused the Maple River near Frederick to go above the flood stage of 10 feet in the early morning of April 2nd. The river quickly rose to the major flood stage of 14.3 feet late in the evening of the 2nd before falling slowly to below flood stage during the afternoon of the 10th.
Snowmelt runoff brought Willow and Mud Creek above flood stage for a short period of time from the 18th to the 19th of April. Both locations rose only half a foot above flood stage.

A strong upper level wave lifted northeast from the Texas Panhandle early on March 9, reaching the western Great Lakes as an intense closed low.  This system included a leading surge of warm air, allowing precipitation to become rain or light freezing rain over a large area, with many locations receiving a few hundredths to two tenths of an inch ice accumulation before colder air wrapped in behind and transitioned precipitation to snowfall.  Brief periods of heavier snowfall due to marginally unstable air off the surface led to accumulations of 2 to 6 inches north of Interstate 90 along with travel disruptions, with generally a trace to 2 inches south; the exception being parts of northwest Iowa which received 2 to 4 inches.  As the surface low pressure moved across Iowa by later in the afternoon and evening of March 9, northwest winds increased to a gusty 30 to 45 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: After a brief period of freezing rain from 0400-0700CST which resulted in a light glaze of icing, snowfall developed with accumulations up to 1.5 inches near Platte. Winds increased as the snow tapered off, gusting as high as 45 mph near Wagner.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous thunderstorms moving over the region brought very heavy rainfall across Hand, Day, and Codington counties. This only added to the water problems they were already experiencing with flooded land, high lake levels, along with several roads. The additional rainfall amounts of 2 to as much as 8 inches brought about more flooded roads, land, along with increasing lake levels. Many roads were barricaded. Some of the heaviest rainfall amounts include 5.10 inches 5 miles west of South Shore, 5.50 inches 5 miles east of Bristol, 6 inches 3 miles west of Burdette, and 8.00 inches 8 miles south of Vayland.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: James River flooding which began in early April remained across Brown county throughout August in part to another above normal rainfall month. The James River at Ashton in Spink county had just gone below flood stage on July 30th and then rose right back above flood stage on August 2nd remaining above flood stage through August. The James River at all three of the river gauges hit their highest stages in April. Flooding of agricultural land along with several roads remained.

As a strong upper level wave pushed into the western Dakotas during the evening hours,  isolated supercellular storms developed across central South Dakota and moved through areas west of the James River.  With 2000-2500 J/kg of MLCAPE and deep layer shear of 35 to 45 knots, the isolated storms produced hail up to golf ball size.  Soon, an increasing amount of DCAPE of 1000-1250 J/kg and increase in 0-3 km shear with development of a strong low-level jet aided in the transition to enhanced linear structures, which then developed a series of mesovortices as the line moved into far eastern South Dakota, southwest Minnesota and northwest Iowa.  Several brief tornadoes developed with mesovortices in southwest Minnesota and northwest Iowa, along with areas of enhanced wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several large branches were snapped off trees.

Heavy August rainfall caused the Big Sioux River near and above Watertown to go above flood stage on August 3rd. The gauging site above Watertown just went above the flood stage of 10 feet for a short time period on August 3rd rising to 10.7 feet in the early morning and then back below flood stage in the early evening. The gauging site near Watertown rose above the flood stage of 8 feet on August 3rd to 9.7 feet on August 4th and stayed above flood stage until the end of August.
A cold front sliding southeast across the region brought severe thunderstorms with large hail and damaging winds to parts of central and northeast South Dakota.

James River flooding, which began in early April, remained across Brown county throughout October as above normal precipitation occurred. The James River flooding from September at Ashton remained through October. In mid October, releases from the Jamestown Dam upstream in North Dakota caused the James River to rise anywhere from 3 to 5 feet at Stratford, Columbia, and Ashton from mid October through November. Agricultural land along with a few roads remained flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong jet dug into the southwestern U.S. on November 25 and ejected into the plains by early November 26, where a surface low pressure area began to organize in the southern High Plains.  The mid-level trough and surface cyclone intensified rapidly lifting toward the western Great Lakes by the morning of November 27. With sharp baroclinicity in place, a band of moderate to heavy snowfall from 4 to 9 inches blanketed far southeast South Dakota, northeast Nebraska, northwest Iowa, and much of southwest Minnesota. The snow was accompanied by northwest winds with gusts as high as 35 to 50 mph near and east of Interstate 29, which resulted in periodic visibility from one-quarter to one-half mile. Some heavier snowfall amounts across southeast South Dakota included 6.5 inches 6 miles southeast of Centerville, 6 inches at Yankton, 5.6 inches 2 miles north of Chester, 5.5 inches 6 miles northwest of Montrose and Menno, 5.3 inches 4 miles northwest of Renner, and 5.0 inches at Hurley.

Abundant low-level moisture, higher values of 0-3km CAPE and weak boundaries from overnight convection led to a couple of brief funnel clouds during initiation of spotty midday showers and thunderstorms.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several homes on Blue Dog Lake on the north side of Waubay became surrounded by water. Water was up against seven or eight homes. Much of the sandbags placed earlier were underwater.  Many roads were saturated or underwater in Day county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A continuation of heavy rainfall and necessary dam releases kept many locations above Gavins Point Dam above flood stage during July.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Much of the month (July 3-7, 12-31) was spent in moderate flood stage, and was in flood for the entire month other than a brief period from July 9-11. The Missouri River near Greenwood (gage site GRWS2) crested 1.75 feet above flood stage on July 28, the fifth highest crest on record. Impacts were mainly limited to inundation of agricultural land, as well as closing of numerous recreational boat ramps. The river remained at moderate flood stage at the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong jet dug into the southwestern U.S. on November 25 and ejected into the plains by early November 26, where a surface low pressure area began to organize in the southern High Plains.  The mid-level trough and surface cyclone intensified rapidly lifting toward the western Great Lakes by the morning of November 27. With sharp baroclinicity in place, a band of moderate to heavy snowfall from 4 to 9 inches blanketed far southeast South Dakota, northeast Nebraska, northwest Iowa, and much of southwest Minnesota. The snow was accompanied by northwest winds with gusts as high as 35 to 50 mph near and east of Interstate 29, which resulted in periodic visibility from one-quarter to one-half mile. Some heavier snowfall amounts across southeast South Dakota included 6.5 inches 6 miles southeast of Centerville, 6 inches at Yankton, 5.6 inches 2 miles north of Chester, 5.5 inches 6 miles northwest of Montrose and Menno, 5.3 inches 4 miles northwest of Renner, and 5.0 inches at Hurley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Poor road conditions led to a head-on collision around 1820CST near the intersection of 485th Avenue and SD Highway 42 which resulted in five injuries (indirect).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level wave lifted northeast from the Texas Panhandle early on March 9, reaching the western Great Lakes as an intense closed low.  This system included a leading surge of warm air, allowing precipitation to become rain or light freezing rain over a large area, with many locations receiving a few hundredths to two tenths of an inch ice accumulation before colder air wrapped in behind and transitioned precipitation to snowfall.  Brief periods of heavier snowfall due to marginally unstable air off the surface led to accumulations of 2 to 6 inches north of Interstate 90 along with travel disruptions, with generally a trace to 2 inches south; the exception being parts of northwest Iowa which received 2 to 4 inches.  As the surface low pressure moved across Iowa by later in the afternoon and evening of March 9, northwest winds increased to a gusty 30 to 45 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: After a brief period of freezing rain from 0400-0700CST which resulted in a light glaze of icing, snowfall developed with accumulations up to 3.0 inches 5 miles south of Chamberlain and 2.0 inches near Pukwana. Winds increased as the snow tapered off, gusting as high as 36 mph at Chamberlain.  Snow drifting across Interstate 90 created treacherous driving conditions and the roadway was closed from 1700CST March 9 until early the next morning.

A surface low pressure area and attached cold front triggered storms across north central South Dakota which became quickly severe. The severe storms became destructive as they moved southeast from the late afternoon through the evening hours. Extensive crop and property damage resulted from winds up to eighty mph along with large hail to baseball size or larger across portions of Corson, Dewey, Walworth, Potter, Sully, Hughes, and Lyman counties. Two rounds of severe storms about an hour apart affected areas roughly from Blunt to Lower Brule to Iona. Siding, windows, and vehicles were damaged along the path of the storms. Some big round hay bales were also rolled for quite a distance by the severe winds. ||The most destructive path of grassland and crop damage occurred from Little Eagle in Corson county southeast to near Onida in Sully county.  Several thousand acres of crops and grassland were destroyed along an estimated 70 mile path which varied from 2 to 6 miles wide. Satellite imagery showed the path of destruction.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Half dollar size hail caused some garden and crop damage.

Close on the heels of the last winter storm, an amplified upper-air pattern brought another impulse out of the southwestern U.S. early on Friday, November 29 and pushed a shield of light mixed precipitation northeastward across the region with a light accumulation of ice.  As the main trough and area of low pressure organized on Saturday, November 30 across Nebraska and deepened as it moved into southern Wisconsin by the morning of Sunday, December 1, additional snowfall developed and was accompanied by strong and gusty northwest winds of 30 to 50 mph by later Saturday night into Sunday morning.||Snowfall totals over the period generally ranged from 6 to 16 inches west of the James River,  to less than one inch from northeast Nebraska and far southeast South Dakota toward southern portions of northwest Iowa.  Another mesoscale band of heavy snowfall occurred on Saturday, November 30 near the Minnesota-Iowa border with between 6 and 9 inches accumulation.||This storm continued into December 1, although a majority of accumulating snowfall ended between 0000 and 0900CST.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mixed precipitation, with occasional light freezing rain or freezing drizzle, began late afternoon on November 29, and resulted in up to a tenth of an inch ice accumulation through the early morning of November 30. Snow developed again by early morning, with periods of moderate snowfall into early Sunday. Snowfall totals by early Sunday reached 4.0 inches 2 miles south southeast of Astoria and 1.5 inches at Brookings.  In addition, winds gusted to 36 mph at Brookings with patchy reduction in visibility below 1 mile in blowing snow.  An adult driver and two child passengers were injured (indirectly) when an SUV lost control on icy roads a mile-and-a-half south of White shortly before 1300CST November 30. The vehicle ramped off a ditch and went briefly airborne before coming to rest in a field.

As a strong upper level wave pushed into the western Dakotas during the evening hours,  isolated supercellular storms developed across central South Dakota and moved through areas west of the James River.  With 2000-2500 J/kg of MLCAPE and deep layer shear of 35 to 45 knots, the isolated storms produced hail up to golf ball size.  Soon, an increasing amount of DCAPE of 1000-1250 J/kg and increase in 0-3 km shear with development of a strong low-level jet aided in the transition to enhanced linear structures, which then developed a series of mesovortices as the line moved into far eastern South Dakota, southwest Minnesota and northwest Iowa.  Several brief tornadoes developed with mesovortices in southwest Minnesota and northwest Iowa, along with areas of enhanced wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several large limbs were broken off trees.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light snowfall developed on the afternoon of Thursday, November 28, then mixed with periods of light freezing drizzle and freezing rain by Friday midday, which resulted in a few hundredths of an inch ice accumulation before temperatures rose above freezing late Friday night. A little light snowfall closed the event later Saturday night into Sunday. Snowfall totals by Sunday morning were 2.7 inches at Marion and 1.6 inches at Hurley. Winds gusted to 37 mph at Freeman and 41 mph near Davis and resulted in several hours of visibility below 1 mile in snow and blowing snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level wave lifted northeast from the Texas Panhandle early on March 9, reaching the western Great Lakes as an intense closed low.  This system included a leading surge of warm air, allowing precipitation to become rain or light freezing rain over a large area, with many locations receiving a few hundredths to two tenths of an inch ice accumulation before colder air wrapped in behind and transitioned precipitation to snowfall.  Brief periods of heavier snowfall due to marginally unstable air off the surface led to accumulations of 2 to 6 inches north of Interstate 90 along with travel disruptions, with generally a trace to 2 inches south; the exception being parts of northwest Iowa which received 2 to 4 inches.  As the surface low pressure moved across Iowa by later in the afternoon and evening of March 9, northwest winds increased to a gusty 30 to 45 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: After a brief period of freezing rain from 0400-0700CST which resulted in a light glaze of icing, snowfall developed with accumulations up to 2.6 inches at Mitchell. Winds increased as the snow tapered off, gusting as high as 46 mph at Mitchell.  Snow drifting across Interstate 90 created treacherous driving conditions and the roadway was closed from 1700CST March 9 until early the next morning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two periods of heavy rainfall totaling 4 to nearly 8 inches occurred across Jones and Lyman counties at the end of May leading to widespread flooding. The first three-day period of heavy rainfall occurred as the result of a powerful low pressure system that slowly moved from the four corners region northward through the Plains.  The second period was the result of a series of disturbances associated with a persistent area of low pressure over the western half of the U.S. that produced several rounds of heavy rain. Both of these rainfalls, coupled with already saturated soils, resulted in widespread flooding across both counties particularly on Medicine Creek.||Numerous roads were flooded and damaged across the region with several culverts washed out. Many farms and homes were also flooded and damaged with many people unable to get out due to washed out roads. A home 10 miles west of Presho had 4 feet of water flow right through it. Presho and Kennebec received the brunt of the flooding on the Medicine Creek with a lot of property damage. Also, some cattle were lost in the flooding. ||Rainfall amounts from the two periods of rain were substantial ranging from 4 to nearly 8 inches. Some of the rainfall amounts include 4.40 inches 6 miles southwest of Oacoma; 5.67 inches 5 miles north of Vivian; 6.10 inches 14 miles northwest of Presho; 6.82 inches at Kennebec; 7.72 inches at Murdo; and 7.96 inches 3 miles northwest of Okaton.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado developed over Roy Lake and came onshore on the southeast side of the lake. The tornado flipped over a pontoon, broke several tree branches, and split a couple trees. It then caused minor damage to an outbuilding before producing extensive tree damage on the south side of a golf course. One piece of lawn furniture was found in a tree over 150 yards away from the original location. Large tree branches were also tossed over 60 yards across fairways on the golf course. In addition to the tornado damage, straight line winds tipped over a camper and caused tree damage in other locations in and around the golf course.

Periodic moderate to heavy rainfall allowed flooding to persist through the month along the James River. More than twice the normal rainfall occurred at Huron and Forestburg during August, with a widespread 6 to 8 inches of rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A continuation of flooding from July, the James River at Huron (gage site HURS2) crested 4.40 feet above flood stage on August 5. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mixed precipitation, with occasional light freezing rain or freezing drizzle, began late afternoon on November 29, and resulted in up to a tenth of an inch ice accumulation through the early morning of November 30. Snow developed again by early morning, becoming heavy at times during the afternoon. Snowfall totals by early Sunday reached 6.2 inches at Huron, 7.7 inches 1 miles south of Huron, and 11.0 inches 2 miles southeast of Wessington.  In addition, winds gusted to 40 mph at Wolsey and 44 mph at Huron with areas of blowing snow.  One passenger was killed (indirect) and another passenger injured (indirect) when the vehicle they were riding in lost control on icy roads and rolled into the ditch around 4 miles south of Cavour.

With moderate instability of 1000-2000 J/kg and deep layer shear from 45 to 55 knots, a few stronger storms produced hail of nickel to walnut size.
Continued high releases from Fort Randall Dam kept river levels at or above flood stage downstream through Lewis and Clark Lake.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A continuation of flooding from July, much of the month (August 1-13, 16-31) was spent in moderate flood stage. The Missouri River near Greenwood (gage site GRWS2) crested 1.71 feet above flood stage on August 31, the sixth highest crest on record. Impacts were mainly limited to inundation of agricultural land, as well as closing of numerous recreational boat ramps. The river remained at moderate flood stage at the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms produced damaging winds, large hail, along with a damaging tornado during the afternoon and early evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sixty mph winds were estimated.

Many rural roads near the river were inundated, with continued flooding of significant amounts of agricultural land. Moderate flooding continued at the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low pressure area and attached cold front triggered storms across north central South Dakota which became quickly severe. The severe storms became destructive as they moved southeast from the late afternoon through the evening hours. Extensive crop and property damage resulted from winds up to eighty mph along with large hail to baseball size or larger across portions of Corson, Dewey, Walworth, Potter, Sully, Hughes, and Lyman counties. Two rounds of severe storms about an hour apart affected areas roughly from Blunt to Lower Brule to Iona. Siding, windows, and vehicles were damaged along the path of the storms. Some big round hay bales were also rolled for quite a distance by the severe winds. ||The most destructive path of grassland and crop damage occurred from Little Eagle in Corson county southeast to near Onida in Sully county.  Several thousand acres of crops and grassland were destroyed along an estimated 70 mile path which varied from 2 to 6 miles wide. Satellite imagery showed the path of destruction.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Golf ball to baseball size hail along with severe winds brought a destructive path of crops from northwest of Java to Java during the evening hours. The path width was anywhere from 2 to 5 miles. The destruction was shown on satellite.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a strong upper level wave pushed into the western Dakotas during the evening hours,  isolated supercellular storms developed across central South Dakota and moved through areas west of the James River.  With 2000-2500 J/kg of MLCAPE and deep layer shear of 35 to 45 knots, the isolated storms produced hail up to golf ball size.  Soon, an increasing amount of DCAPE of 1000-1250 J/kg and increase in 0-3 km shear with development of a strong low-level jet aided in the transition to enhanced linear structures, which then developed a series of mesovortices as the line moved into far eastern South Dakota, southwest Minnesota and northwest Iowa.  Several brief tornadoes developed with mesovortices in southwest Minnesota and northwest Iowa, along with areas of enhanced wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail lasted for around 40 minutes, but was quarter size or larger for about 15 minutes of that time.

Close on the heels of the last winter storm, an amplified upper-air pattern brought another impulse out of the southwestern U.S. early on Friday, November 29 and pushed a shield of light mixed precipitation northeastward across the region with a light accumulation of ice.  As the main trough and area of low pressure organized on Saturday, November 30 across Nebraska and deepened as it moved into southern Wisconsin by the morning of Sunday, December 1, additional snowfall developed and was accompanied by strong and gusty northwest winds of 30 to 50 mph by later Saturday night into Sunday morning.||Snowfall totals over the period generally ranged from 6 to 16 inches west of the James River,  to less than one inch from northeast Nebraska and far southeast South Dakota toward southern portions of northwest Iowa.  Another mesoscale band of heavy snowfall occurred on Saturday, November 30 near the Minnesota-Iowa border with between 6 and 9 inches accumulation.||This storm continued into December 1, although a majority of accumulating snowfall ended between 0000 and 0900CST.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A period of light snow developed during the evening of Thursday, November 28 bringing a light accumulation before tapering off. Another surge of mixed-type precipitation developed the following afternoon before changing back to snow during the evening of November 30. A glaze of ice accumulation occurred through through November 30, followed by snowfall which totaled 3.5 inches at Bridgewater and 3.2 inches at Montrose by early Sunday. In addition, winds gusted to 43 mph near Montrose with brief reduction in visibility below 1 mile in blowing snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level wave lifted northeast from the Texas Panhandle early on March 9, reaching the western Great Lakes as an intense closed low.  This system included a leading surge of warm air, allowing precipitation to become rain or light freezing rain over a large area, with many locations receiving a few hundredths to two tenths of an inch ice accumulation before colder air wrapped in behind and transitioned precipitation to snowfall.  Brief periods of heavier snowfall due to marginally unstable air off the surface led to accumulations of 2 to 6 inches north of Interstate 90 along with travel disruptions, with generally a trace to 2 inches south; the exception being parts of northwest Iowa which received 2 to 4 inches.  As the surface low pressure moved across Iowa by later in the afternoon and evening of March 9, northwest winds increased to a gusty 30 to 45 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: After a brief period of freezing rain from 0400-0700CST which resulted in a light glaze of icing, snowfall developed with accumulations up to 3.5 inches near Gregory and 2.0 inches 5 miles southwest of Burke. Winds increased as the snow tapered off, gusting as high as 43 mph at Gregory and 38 mph near Fairfax.

A strong jet dug into the southwestern U.S. on November 25 and ejected into the plains by early November 26, where a surface low pressure area began to organize in the southern High Plains.  The mid-level trough and surface cyclone intensified rapidly lifting toward the western Great Lakes by the morning of November 27. With sharp baroclinicity in place, a band of moderate to heavy snowfall from 4 to 9 inches blanketed far southeast South Dakota, northeast Nebraska, northwest Iowa, and much of southwest Minnesota. The snow was accompanied by northwest winds with gusts as high as 35 to 50 mph near and east of Interstate 29, which resulted in periodic visibility from one-quarter to one-half mile. Some heavier snowfall amounts across southeast South Dakota included 6.5 inches 6 miles southeast of Centerville, 6 inches at Yankton, 5.6 inches 2 miles north of Chester, 5.5 inches 6 miles northwest of Montrose and Menno, 5.3 inches 4 miles northwest of Renner, and 5.0 inches at Hurley.

Above normal rainfall in August kept Lake Poinsett very high throughout the month. Many structures around the lake remained flooded along with some roads. The high lake levels remained into September.

A strong upper level wave lifted northeast from the Texas Panhandle early on March 9, reaching the western Great Lakes as an intense closed low.  This system included a leading surge of warm air, allowing precipitation to become rain or light freezing rain over a large area, with many locations receiving a few hundredths to two tenths of an inch ice accumulation before colder air wrapped in behind and transitioned precipitation to snowfall.  Brief periods of heavier snowfall due to marginally unstable air off the surface led to accumulations of 2 to 6 inches north of Interstate 90 along with travel disruptions, with generally a trace to 2 inches south; the exception being parts of northwest Iowa which received 2 to 4 inches.  As the surface low pressure moved across Iowa by later in the afternoon and evening of March 9, northwest winds increased to a gusty 30 to 45 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: After a brief period of freezing rain from 0400-0700CST which resulted in up to a tenth of an inch of icing, precipitation changed to rainfall early morning which was briefly heavy and produced some urban flooding. Cooler air changed rain to snowfall, with accumulations up to 2.8 inches near Garretson and from 2 to 3 inches around Sioux Falls. Winds increased as the snow tapered off, gusting as high as 43 mph at Sioux Falls.  A sheriff's deputy and driver of another car were indirectly injured when his vehicle was struck by another while stopped to aid at a separate non-injury accident around 0600CST along Interstate 90 near Sioux Falls. The accidents were a result of ice accumulation on roadways.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Close on the heels of the last winter storm, an amplified upper-air pattern brought another impulse out of the southwestern U.S. early on Friday, November 29 and pushed a shield of light mixed precipitation northeastward across the region with a light accumulation of ice.  As the main trough and area of low pressure organized on Saturday, November 30 across Nebraska and deepened as it moved into southern Wisconsin by the morning of Sunday, December 1, additional snowfall developed and was accompanied by strong and gusty northwest winds of 30 to 50 mph by later Saturday night into Sunday morning.||Snowfall totals over the period generally ranged from 6 to 16 inches west of the James River,  to less than one inch from northeast Nebraska and far southeast South Dakota toward southern portions of northwest Iowa.  Another mesoscale band of heavy snowfall occurred on Saturday, November 30 near the Minnesota-Iowa border with between 6 and 9 inches accumulation.||This storm continued into December 1, although a majority of accumulating snowfall ended between 0000 and 0900CST.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mixed precipitation, with occasional light freezing rain or freezing drizzle, began during the evening of Thursday, November 28, and resulted in a few hundredths of an inch ice accumulation through the early morning of Saturday, November 30. After temperatures warmed above freezing for most of Saturday, snow redeveloped by late Saturday evening and continued  with periods of snowfall into midday Sunday. Snowfall totals by Sunday morning were only 1.0 inch at Yankton, but winds gusted to 41 mph at Yankton and resulted in several hours of visibility below 1 mile in snow and blowing snow.  Travel was not recommended by late evening on Saturday, November 30.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mixed precipitation, with occasional light freezing rain or freezing drizzle, began early afternoon on November 29, and resulted in up to a tenth of an inch ice accumulation through the evening of November 29. In addition, winds gusted to 54 mph at Fort Randall Dam, with areas of blowing snow with visibility of a half mile or less through early Sunday morning. Travel was not recommended by late afternoon of November 30.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Periodic moderate to heavy rainfall returned the lower James River to flooding conditions mid to late month. More than twice the normal rainfall occurred at Huron and Forestburg during July.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The James River near Scotland (gage site SCOS2) crested at 3.24 feet above flood stage on July 19. Many rural roads near the river were inundated, with flooding of significant amounts of agricultural land.  Moderate flooding continued at the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex of thunderstorms developed during the evening across western South Dakota, moving east and becoming more organized as the low-level jet increased focus into south central and southeast South Dakota late at night.  Deep-layer effective shear reached 35 to 45 knots with MUCAPE to 2000J/kg. 0-3km shear was 25 to 35 knots, largely normal to the orientation of the convective line.  A large-scale bowing segment evolved quickly with a long track of damaging wind across southeast South Dakota into northwest Iowa.  Several mesovortices developed along the line and produced swaths of enhanced wind damage, many with winds from 80 to 90 mph, and as high as 115 mph.  Three people were injured as the strong winds went through a campground, tearing up trees and tents, and rolling campers.||A rare, but short-lived, late night tornado was spawned near one of the stronger mesovortices.  ||Areas of high rainfall rates resulted in spotty urban flash flooding.

A complex of thunderstorms developed during the evening across western South Dakota, moving east and becoming more organized as the low-level jet increased focus into south central and southeast South Dakota late at night.  Deep-layer effective shear reached 35 to 45 knots with MUCAPE to 2000J/kg. 0-3km shear was 25 to 35 knots, largely normal to the orientation of the convective line.  A large-scale bowing segment evolved quickly with a long track of damaging wind across southeast South Dakota into northwest Iowa.  Several mesovortices developed along the line and produced swaths of enhanced wind damage, many with winds from 80 to 90 mph, and as high as 115 mph.  Three people were injured as the strong winds went through a campground, tearing up trees and tents, and rolling campers.||A rare, but short-lived, late night tornado was spawned near one of the stronger mesovortices.  ||Areas of high rainfall rates resulted in spotty urban flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike to a single-family home caused a fire, resulting in total loss of the home. |Four residents were home at the time of the lightning strike, but there were no injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A colossal late winter storm began to develop early in the morning of March 13, with intense low pressure across southeast Colorado by 0600CST.  A surge of warm air ahead of this system resulted in bands of widespread liquid precipitation, along with scattered thunderstorms developing through the day on the 13th, and continuing as a stacked cyclone moved into western Iowa by 0600CST March 14.||Rain, with some areas of freezing rain resulting in around a tenth of an inch ice accumulation around Huron and Chamberlain, started to change to snow very late on March 13 into the early hours of the 14th.  As winds increased to 30 to 40 mph with gusts to 55 to 65 mph, whiteout conditions developed from the James River westward during the brief window of snowfall despite a limited accumulation of new snow (generally less than 2 inches) and widespread rainfall over the existing snow cover.  ||In addition, with the ground largely frozen to depths of 10 to 20 inches, antecedent snow cover with as much as 4 to 6 inches of liquid water equivalent, and the rapid warming of the air mass and resulting widespread rainfall from 1 to 3 inches, major flooding resulted across the region. ||Record precipitation on March 13 included, but was not limited to De Smet (1.90 inches), Vermillion (1.55 inches), Platte (1.86 inches), Gregory (1.26 inches), Mitchell (1.82 inches), Huron (1.43 inches), Primghar (1.98 inches), Menno (1.81 inches), Sioux City (1.63 inches), and Sioux Falls (1.91 inches).||Peak wind gusts of 58 mph or higher for the storm included: 67 mph at Mitchell, 65 mph 5 miles west of Geddes, 63 mph at Huron, 61 mph at Spencer, 60 mph at Sioux Falls, Jackson, and 2 miles west of Dovray, 59 mph at Gregory, Madison and Marshall, and 58 mph at Chamberlain, near Beaver Creek, and Sioux City.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: After an appreciable amount of rainfall and some light freezing rain resulting in up to a tenth of an inch ice accumulation, precipitation changed to snow after 1800CST March 13.  As northwest winds increased and frequent gusts topped 45 mph, periods of whiteout conditions developed during the morning hours on March 14 with travel not advised across the county.  Snowfall accumulated to 4.2 inches 8 miles south southwest of Chamberlain, 2.8 inches at Chamberlain and 2.5 inches near Pukwana.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A historic flooding event unfolded as heavy rainfall drenched the area on March 13-14, 2019.  With frozen and impervious ground, full and rapid runoff was maximized. In addition to the rainfall, between 1 and 3 inches of snow water equivalent remained in the snow pack south of Interstate 90, with 3 to 6 inches of snow water equivalent north of Interstate 90. Fifteen river gage sites measured record stages around the area.  Winds gusted to 50 to 70 mph at times, and colder air filtering into the system at the end of the storm produced a light  accumulation of ice west of the James River and from 1 to 4 inches of snowfall in parts of southeast South Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall (2.60 inches 8 miles west northwest of Springfield and 2.71 inches at Tyndall) and snow melt was the primary contributor to the flooding along Lewis and Clark Reservoir; however, the final level of crest had at least a partial contribution from an uncontrolled release with the failure of Spencer Dam along the Niobrara River in Nebraska during the evening of March 13. The highest crest on record resulted, as the Missouri River at Gavins Point Dam (gage site GPDN1) crested 2.02 feet above flood stage on March 15.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A historic flooding event unfolded as heavy rainfall drenched the area on March 13-14, 2019.  With frozen and impervious ground, full and rapid runoff was maximized. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow melt and heavy rainfall (measured 2.74 inches at Hartford, 2.25 inches at Colton and 2.49 inches at Sioux Falls) resulted in extensive flooding of agricultural land downstream of Sioux Falls. The Big Sioux River at Sioux Falls North Cliff Avenue (gage site SXFS2) reached a crest 4.97 feet above flood stage on March 15. The river would crest again later in the month due to water routed from upstream melting (separate entry).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A historic flooding event unfolded as heavy rainfall with a late winter storm drenched the area on March 13-14, 2019. With 1 to 3 inches of snow water equivalent still in the snow pack south of Interstate 90 and 3 to 6 inches of snow water equivalent north of Interstate 90, gradually warming temperatures, and the frozen impervious ground, area rivers crested a second time due to additional runoff about a week after the first rapid crests. Overall, fifteen river gage sites measured record stages around the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow melt and heavy rainfall (measured 1.74 inches 5 miles west of Bruce) resulted in extensive flooding of agricultural land. The Big Sioux River near Estelline (gage site BSES2) reached the highest crest on record of 2.90 feet above flood stage on March 28. The river remained at moderate flood stage at the end of the month.

With 1 to 3 inches of snow water equivalent still in the snow pack south of Interstate 90 and 3 to 6 inches of snow water equivalent north of Interstate 90, gradually warming temperatures, and the frozen impervious ground, area rivers crested a second time due to additional runoff about a week after the first rapid crests. Overall, fifteen river gage sites measured record stages around the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow melt and heavy rainfall (measured 1.69 inches at Bruce) resulted in extensive flooding of agricultural land and inundation of numerous county roads. The Big Sioux River near Bruce (gage site BRCS2) reached the second highest crest on record of 2.86 feet above flood stage on March 28. The river remained at moderate flood stage at the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Periodic moderate to heavy rainfall allowed flooding to persist through the month along the middle portions of the James River.  More than twice the normal rainfall occurred at Huron and Forestburg during July.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A continuation of flooding from June, the James River near Mitchell (gage site JRMS2) crested 4.74 feet above flood stage on July 12, with a secondary crest 3.17 feet above flood stage on July 30. Many rural roads near the river were inundated, with continued flooding of significant amounts of agricultural land. Minor flooding continued at the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a weak disturbance acted to focus persistent lower to mid-level warm advection, persistent showers with only a rogue flash of lightning or two blossomed in the high moisture content air mass. Rainfall from 3 to 6 inches was common across a small area of southern Charles Mix and southwest Bon Homme counties, especially between 0500-0900CST.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: After waters had receded earlier in the day, a plugged culvert resulted in extensive ponding of water along South Dakota Highway 46/50 on the west side of Wagner. Additional flooding was reported near 5th Street on the south side of Wagner, near the location of the plugged culvert.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex of thunderstorms developed during the evening across western South Dakota, moving east and becoming more organized as the low-level jet increased focus into south central and southeast South Dakota late at night.  Deep-layer effective shear reached 35 to 45 knots with MUCAPE to 2000J/kg. 0-3km shear was 25 to 35 knots, largely normal to the orientation of the convective line.  A large-scale bowing segment evolved quickly with a long track of damaging wind across southeast South Dakota into northwest Iowa.  Several mesovortices developed along the line and produced swaths of enhanced wind damage, many with winds from 80 to 90 mph, and as high as 115 mph.  Three people were injured as the strong winds went through a campground, tearing up trees and tents, and rolling campers.||A rare, but short-lived, late night tornado was spawned near one of the stronger mesovortices.  ||Areas of high rainfall rates resulted in spotty urban flash flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level wave lifted northeast from the Texas Panhandle early on March 9, reaching the western Great Lakes as an intense closed low.  This system included a leading surge of warm air, allowing precipitation to become rain or light freezing rain over a large area, with many locations receiving a few hundredths to two tenths of an inch ice accumulation before colder air wrapped in behind and transitioned precipitation to snowfall.  Brief periods of heavier snowfall due to marginally unstable air off the surface led to accumulations of 2 to 6 inches north of Interstate 90 along with travel disruptions, with generally a trace to 2 inches south; the exception being parts of northwest Iowa which received 2 to 4 inches.  As the surface low pressure moved across Iowa by later in the afternoon and evening of March 9, northwest winds increased to a gusty 30 to 45 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief period of freezing rain from 0400-0700CST brought up to two tenths of an inch ice accumulation.  Snowfall followed with visibility occasionally reduced to 1/4 to 1/2 mile between 1100-1500CST, with total accumulations of 3.2 inches 11 miles north of Colman and 2.7 inches at Flandreau.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very unstable atmosphere was in place across south central and southeast South Dakota with around 2000 J/kg MLCAPE.  As a strong upper-level wave dropped from Montana into North Dakota, storms initiated after 1400CST along a pre-frontal surface trough across north central South Dakota.  As storms moved into an environment with deep-layer effective shear of 40 to 50 knots, the southernmost cells produced very large hail as well as a brief tornado near Burke later in the evening. Two people were injured when the garage they were moving cars into at the time of the storm was destroyed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wooden power poles broken off by thunderstorm winds.  A large area of 80 to 100 mile per hour winds accompanied the tornadic development.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado spun up around two miles north of Tripp, touching down just east of a railroad line east of State Highway 37 and north of 285th Street. The tornado tracked to the southeast, where it impacted a farmstead, causing significant damage to two barns and destroying a small outbuilding. The tornado continued across 411th Ave. where it removed the second story of a poorly constructed residence.  Fortunately, the residents were not home at the time.  After a mile on the ground, a third farmstead was struck tearing metal roofing from a barn.  At this point, the tornado veered a bit more southward, finally lifting just after crossing U.S. Highway 18 near 412th Avenue.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms brought winds to over seventy mph, ping-pong ball size hail, isolated flooding, along with a tornado to parts of central South Dakota during the afternoon hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorm winds capsized an 18-foot catamaran on Lake Oahe about 8 miles north of Fort Pierre. The two men on the boat had to be rescued and were uninjured.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm briefly became severe over the central Black Hills, producing large hail in the Hill City area.

The continuation of snowmelt from a much above normal snowfall winter combined with a historic heavy snow/blizzard in mid-April resulted in widespread flooding across central and northeast South Dakota. Countless roads along with thousands of acres of cropland were flooded throughout April. Impacts include damaged roads, culverts, and bridges, and livestock, homes, and businesses were affected. Delayed planting resulted across all of the region as well. Cattle and calves were stressed by the cold and wet pattern, as the mud and cold caused some sickness with the livestock. Flooded roads made it difficult for many farmers or ranchers to get to their fields or livestock. There was a water rescue near Frederick on April 7th as someone drove into a washed out road. The wet pattern along with the flooding continued into May, further delaying planting across the region.||All counties declared emergencies/disasters in March and April due to the widespread flooding and March blizzard.  South Dakota's governor declared a disaster for the state in March. This declaration was followed by a disaster declaration by the President of the United States. As a result, 24 of the 26 counties across central and northeast South Dakota were able to have access to public property damage assistance. Overall, damage estimates from the blizzards and floods for the state were at 43 million dollars.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A colossal late winter storm began to develop early in the morning of March 13, with intense low pressure across southeast Colorado by 0600CST.  A surge of warm air ahead of this system resulted in bands of widespread liquid precipitation, along with scattered thunderstorms developing through the day on the 13th, and continuing as a stacked cyclone moved into western Iowa by 0600CST March 14.||Rain, with some areas of freezing rain resulting in around a tenth of an inch ice accumulation around Huron and Chamberlain, started to change to snow very late on March 13 into the early hours of the 14th.  As winds increased to 30 to 40 mph with gusts to 55 to 65 mph, whiteout conditions developed from the James River westward during the brief window of snowfall despite a limited accumulation of new snow (generally less than 2 inches) and widespread rainfall over the existing snow cover.  ||In addition, with the ground largely frozen to depths of 10 to 20 inches, antecedent snow cover with as much as 4 to 6 inches of liquid water equivalent, and the rapid warming of the air mass and resulting widespread rainfall from 1 to 3 inches, major flooding resulted across the region. ||Record precipitation on March 13 included, but was not limited to De Smet (1.90 inches), Vermillion (1.55 inches), Platte (1.86 inches), Gregory (1.26 inches), Mitchell (1.82 inches), Huron (1.43 inches), Primghar (1.98 inches), Menno (1.81 inches), Sioux City (1.63 inches), and Sioux Falls (1.91 inches).||Peak wind gusts of 58 mph or higher for the storm included: 67 mph at Mitchell, 65 mph 5 miles west of Geddes, 63 mph at Huron, 61 mph at Spencer, 60 mph at Sioux Falls, Jackson, and 2 miles west of Dovray, 59 mph at Gregory, Madison and Marshall, and 58 mph at Chamberlain, near Beaver Creek, and Sioux City.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: After an appreciable amount of rainfall and some light freezing rain resulting in up to a tenth of an inch ice accumulation, precipitation changed to snow after 0200CST March 14.  As northwest winds increased and frequent gusts topped 45 mph, periods of whiteout conditions developed during the morning hours on March 14 with travel not advised across the county.  Snowfall accumulated to 0.7 inches at Armour.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With frozen ground and most urban drainage plugged by ice and snow, a period of heavy rainfall during the morning in Sioux Falls produced urban flooding. Up to 1000 intersections were inundated with water as deep as 1 to 2 feet due to runoff from morning rain and plugged storm drains.  Numerous vehicles became stranded in standing water. The mayor of Sioux Falls joined in the effort to clear plugged drains.  Even by 1600CST, over 300 intersections remained inundated by standing water.  ||A record precipitation amount for the day occurred at the National Weather Service office in Sioux Falls (0.86 inches).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Diurnal heating produced extreme instability of 3000-4000 J/kg MLCAPE by late afternoon across southeast South Dakota.  An isolated supercell developed with an approaching weak mid-level wave from the northwest during the very late afternoon. Deep-layer shear was around 35 knots.  The storm traveled southeastward along the instability gradient through early evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Not much hail, but some was nickel to quarter size.

Several mesovortices developed along the line and produced swaths of enhanced wind damage, many with winds from 80 to 90 mph, and as high as 115 mph.  Three people were injured as the strong winds went through a campground, tearing up trees and tents, and rolling campers.||A rare, but short-lived, late night tornado was spawned near one of the stronger mesovortices.  ||Areas of high rainfall rates resulted in spotty urban flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lumber yard lost part of its roof, with most fences taken out.

A historic flooding event unfolded as heavy rainfall drenched the area on March 13-14, 2019.  With frozen and impervious ground, full and rapid runoff was maximized. In addition to the rainfall, between 1 and 3 inches of snow water equivalent remained in the snow pack south of Interstate 90, with 3 to 6 inches of snow water equivalent north of Interstate 90. Fifteen river gage sites measured record stages around the area.  Winds gusted to 50 to 70 mph at times, and colder air filtering into the system at the end of the storm produced a light  accumulation of ice west of the James River and from 1 to 4 inches of snowfall in parts of southeast South Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow melt and heavy rainfall (measured 2.46 inches 6 miles north northeast of Elk Point and 1.60 inches 2 miles northwest of Jefferson) resulted in extensive flooding of agricultural land in the flood plain. The Big Sioux River at Sioux City (gage site SXCI4) reached the second highest crest on record at 5.42 feet above flood stage on March 17.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusually strong and cold area of high pressure surged into the northern Plains in early March.  Winds were mainly a gusty 20 to 35 mph, and when coupled with temperatures from 10 below to 20 below zero, produced a long duration of wind chills colder than 20 below zero.  Coldest wind chills were recorded between 0400-0900 on March 3, reaching -51 near Oak Lake, -48 at Volga, -47 at Flandreau and White Lake, -46 at Colton and 5 miles south of Hendricks, -45 at Windom, Worthington and near Sioux Falls, and -44 at Huron, Brookings and Madison.  March 3 was one of the coldest March days on record for the area, with the coldest or second coldest March high temperatures on record at Huron, Sioux Falls, Mitchell and Sioux City.
A continuation of heavy rainfall and necessary dam releases kept many locations above Gavins Point Dam above flood stage during July.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding continued from June, with only a brief fall below flood stage from July 9-12. The Missouri River near Verdel (gage site MRVN1) crested 1.00 feet above flood stage on July 6, and again at 0.76 feet above flood stage on July 28. High water closed numerous recreational boat ramps. The river remained at minor flood stage at the end of the month.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: While there was some contribution from rainfall (2.60 inches 8 miles west northwest of Springfield and 2.71 inches at Tyndall) and snow melt, the crest severity along the Missouri River had at least a partial contribution from an uncontrolled release with the failure of Spencer Dam along the Niobrara River in Nebraska during the evening of March 13. The highest crest on record resulted, as the Missouri River at Springfield (gage site SPGS2) crested 7.67 feet above flood stage on March 14, inundating the golf course in Springfield as well as many river bottom roads and recreational areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A historic flooding event unfolded as heavy rainfall with a late winter storm drenched the area on March 13-14, 2019. With 1 to 3 inches of snow water equivalent still in the snow pack south of Interstate 90 and 3 to 6 inches of snow water equivalent north of Interstate 90, gradually warming temperatures, and the frozen impervious ground, area rivers crested a second time due to additional runoff about a week after the first rapid crests. Overall, fifteen river gage sites measured record stages around the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow melt and heavy rainfall (measured 2.02 in Brookings and 1.68 inches in Aurora) resulted in extensive flooding of agricultural land. Medary Creek near Medary (gage site MDYS2) reached the second highest crest on record of 1.71 feet above flood stage on March 21.

With 1 to 3 inches of snow water equivalent still in the snow pack south of Interstate 90 and 3 to 6 inches of snow water equivalent north of Interstate 90, gradually warming temperatures, and the frozen impervious ground, runoff into the controlled Missouri River resulted in crests late in the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow melt and heavy rainfall resulted in flooding of a few boat docks near Niobrara.  The Missouri River at Niobrara (gage site MRNN1) reached the fifth highest stage on record cresting 0.93 feet above flood stage on March 30.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very unstable atmosphere was in place across south central and southeast South Dakota with around 2000 J/kg MLCAPE.  As a strong upper-level wave dropped from Montana into North Dakota, storms initiated after 1400CST along a pre-frontal surface trough across north central South Dakota.  As storms moved into an environment with deep-layer effective shear of 40 to 50 knots, the southernmost cells produced very large hail as well as a brief tornado near Burke later in the evening. Two people were injured when the garage they were moving cars into at the time of the storm was destroyed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant crop damage caused by wind-driven hail.
