A colossal late winter storm began to develop early in the morning of March 13, with intense low pressure across southeast Colorado by 0600CST.  A surge of warm air ahead of this system resulted in bands of widespread liquid precipitation, along with scattered thunderstorms developing through the day on the 13th, and continuing as a stacked cyclone moved into western Iowa by 0600CST March 14.||Rain, with some areas of freezing rain resulting in around a tenth of an inch ice accumulation around Huron and Chamberlain, started to change to snow very late on March 13 into the early hours of the 14th.  As winds increased to 30 to 40 mph with gusts to 55 to 65 mph, whiteout conditions developed from the James River westward during the brief window of snowfall despite a limited accumulation of new snow (generally less than 2 inches) and widespread rainfall over the existing snow cover.  ||In addition, with the ground largely frozen to depths of 10 to 20 inches, antecedent snow cover with as much as 4 to 6 inches of liquid water equivalent, and the rapid warming of the air mass and resulting widespread rainfall from 1 to 3 inches, major flooding resulted across the region. ||Record precipitation on March 13 included, but was not limited to De Smet (1.90 inches), Vermillion (1.55 inches), Platte (1.86 inches), Gregory (1.26 inches), Mitchell (1.82 inches), Huron (1.43 inches), Primghar (1.98 inches), Menno (1.81 inches), Sioux City (1.63 inches), and Sioux Falls (1.91 inches).||Peak wind gusts of 58 mph or higher for the storm included: 67 mph at Mitchell, 65 mph 5 miles west of Geddes, 63 mph at Huron, 61 mph at Spencer, 60 mph at Sioux Falls, Jackson, and 2 miles west of Dovray, 59 mph at Gregory, Madison and Marshall, and 58 mph at Chamberlain, near Beaver Creek, and Sioux City.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: After an appreciable amount of rainfall and some light freezing rain resulting in up to a tenth of an inch ice accumulation, precipitation changed to snow after 1800CST March 13.  As northwest winds increased and frequent gusts topped 45 mph, periods of whiteout conditions developed during the morning hours on March 14 with travel not advised across the county.  Snowfall accumulated to 3.0 inches 9 miles south of Dallas and 2.0 inches at Gregory.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As an outflow boundary sagged through southeast South Dakota, an isolated elevated thunderstorm developed late morning northwest of Sioux Falls. Wind shear was weak, and as the storm underwent a split, both storms produced marginally large hail.  By mid afternoon, a second isolated storm developed with very slow movement south of Huron, SD, again featuring torrential rainfall and some severe hail.

Rainfall of one to three inches on frozen ground and into a snow pack with between 2 and 5 inches of liquid water equivalent resulted in considerable overland flooding.||One of the hardest hit areas was around Yankton, where a No Travel Advisory was issued for the city on March 13-14.  Businesses along north Highway 81 toward Yankton Mall had considerable ponding of water.  Water rescues were necessary as cars stalled in high water in low spots around Yankton.  Two people were also rescued from their inundated vehicle on Jim River Road east of Yankton. Water also got into power substations and caused spotty power outages and necessitated temporary evacuation of residents.||State government offices across the area were closed on March 14 due to the flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ponding of water over roadways at the intersections of 302nd St. and SD Highway 19, and SD Highway 46/19.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Diurnal heating produced extreme instability of 3000-4000 J/kg MLCAPE by late afternoon across southeast South Dakota.  An isolated supercell developed with an approaching weak mid-level wave from the northwest during the very late afternoon. Deep-layer shear was around 35 knots.  The storm traveled southeastward along the instability gradient through early evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several 6 to 12 inch diameter limbs removed from mature trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Diurnal heating produced extreme instability of 3000-4000 J/kg MLCAPE by late afternoon across southeast South Dakota.  An isolated supercell developed with an approaching weak mid-level wave from the northwest during the very late afternoon. Deep-layer shear was around 35 knots.  The storm traveled southeastward along the instability gradient through early evening.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level wave lifted northeast from the Texas Panhandle early on March 9, reaching the western Great Lakes as an intense closed low.  This system included a leading surge of warm air, allowing precipitation to become rain or light freezing rain over a large area, with many locations receiving a few hundredths to two tenths of an inch ice accumulation before colder air wrapped in behind and transitioned precipitation to snowfall.  Brief periods of heavier snowfall due to marginally unstable air off the surface led to accumulations of 2 to 6 inches north of Interstate 90 along with travel disruptions, with generally a trace to 2 inches south; the exception being parts of northwest Iowa which received 2 to 4 inches.  As the surface low pressure moved across Iowa by later in the afternoon and evening of March 9, northwest winds increased to a gusty 30 to 45 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: After a brief period of freezing rain from 0400-0700CST which resulted in a light glaze of icing, snowfall developed with accumulations up to 4.0 inches at Wessington Springs. Winds increased as the snow tapered off, gusting as high as 36 mph 10 miles south southwest of Wessington.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A historic flooding event unfolded as heavy rainfall drenched the area on March 13-14, 2019.  With frozen and impervious ground, full and rapid runoff was maximized. In addition to the rainfall, between 1 and 3 inches of snow water equivalent remained in the snow pack south of Interstate 90, with 3 to 6 inches of snow water equivalent north of Interstate 90. Fifteen river gage sites measured record stages around the area.  Winds gusted to 50 to 70 mph at times, and colder air filtering into the system at the end of the storm produced a light  accumulation of ice west of the James River and from 1 to 4 inches of snowfall in parts of southeast South Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: While there was some contribution from rainfall (2.90 inches near Wagner) and snow melt, the crest severity along the Missouri River had at least a partial contribution from backwater flow from an uncontrolled release with the failure of Spencer Dam along the Niobrara River in Nebraska during the evening of March 13.  A home south of Dante along River Bottom Road was surrounded by water.  Major flooding resulted, as the Missouri River at Verdel (gage site MRVN1) crested second highest on record at 2.6 feet above flood stage on March 14.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A historic flooding event unfolded as heavy rainfall drenched the area on March 13-14, 2019.  With frozen and impervious ground, full and rapid runoff was maximized. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow melt and heavy rainfall (measured 2.47 inches at Forestburg and 2.25 inches at Mitchell) resulted in significant flooding of agricultural lands and inundation of numerous county roads (including 257th St. near the gage site and 1st Avenue and 416th Avenue near Rosedale Colony). The James River at Mitchell (gage site JRMS2) reached major flood stage and the 5th highest crest on record to date, 6.6 feet above flood stage on March 25. Moderate flooding continued at the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A historic flooding event unfolded as heavy rainfall with a late winter storm drenched the area on March 13-14, 2019. With 1 to 3 inches of snow water equivalent still in the snow pack south of Interstate 90 and 3 to 6 inches of snow water equivalent north of Interstate 90, gradually warming temperatures, and the frozen impervious ground, runoff into the controlled Missouri River resulted in crests late in the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow melt and heavy rainfall resulted in flooding of a few boat docks and some camping areas near Chamberlain. The Missouri River at Chamberlain (gage site CBLS2) reached the fifth highest stage on record cresting 2.36 feet above flood stage on March 31.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A historic flooding event unfolded as heavy rainfall with a late winter storm drenched the area on March 13-14, 2019. With 1 to 3 inches of snow water equivalent still in the snow pack south of Interstate 90 and 3 to 6 inches of snow water equivalent north of Interstate 90, gradually warming temperatures, and the frozen impervious ground, area rivers crested a second time due to additional runoff about a week after the first rapid crests. Overall, fifteen river gage sites measured record stages around the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow melt and heavy rainfall (measured 2.47 inches at Forestburg and 1.60 inches at Huron) resulted in extensive flooding of agricultural land and numerous township and county roads. The James River near Forestburg (gage site FORS2) reached a crest of 4.18 feet above flood stage on March 25. The driver and passenger of a garbage truck were (directly) killed when their truck went off the road and into the flood waters at the base of the bridge near the gage site. The river remained at moderate flood stage at the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex of thunderstorms developed during the evening across western South Dakota, moving east and becoming more organized as the low-level jet increased focus into south central and southeast South Dakota late at night.  Deep-layer effective shear reached 35 to 45 knots with MUCAPE to 2000J/kg. 0-3km shear was 25 to 35 knots, largely normal to the orientation of the convective line.  A large-scale bowing segment evolved quickly with a long track of damaging wind across southeast South Dakota into northwest Iowa.  Several mesovortices developed along the line and produced swaths of enhanced wind damage, many with winds from 80 to 90 mph, and as high as 115 mph.  Three people were injured as the strong winds went through a campground, tearing up trees and tents, and rolling campers.||A rare, but short-lived, late night tornado was spawned near one of the stronger mesovortices.  ||Areas of high rainfall rates resulted in spotty urban flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant tree damage occurred at the Snake Creek Campground. Injuries occurred when tree branches fell on tents.  Several campers rolled by high winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusually strong and cold area of high pressure surged into the northern Plains in early March.  Winds were mainly a gusty 20 to 35 mph, and when coupled with temperatures from 10 below to 20 below zero, produced a long duration of wind chills colder than 20 below zero.  Coldest wind chills were recorded between 0400-0900 on March 3, reaching -51 near Oak Lake, -48 at Volga, -47 at Flandreau and White Lake, -46 at Colton and 5 miles south of Hendricks, -45 at Windom, Worthington and near Sioux Falls, and -44 at Huron, Brookings and Madison.  March 3 was one of the coldest March days on record for the area, with the coldest or second coldest March high temperatures on record at Huron, Sioux Falls, Mitchell and Sioux City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As an outflow boundary sagged through southeast South Dakota, an isolated elevated thunderstorm developed late morning northwest of Sioux Falls. Wind shear was weak, and as the storm underwent a split, both storms produced marginally large hail.  By mid afternoon, a second isolated storm developed with very slow movement south of Huron, SD, again featuring torrential rainfall and some severe hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Small creek flooding into field and ditches south of spotters home.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a strong upper level wave pushed into the western Dakotas during the evening hours,  isolated supercellular storms developed across central South Dakota and moved through areas west of the James River.  With 2000-2500 J/kg of MLCAPE and deep layer shear of 35 to 45 knots, the isolated storms produced hail up to golf ball size.  Soon, an increasing amount of DCAPE of 1000-1250 J/kg and increase in 0-3 km shear with development of a strong low-level jet aided in the transition to enhanced linear structures, which then developed a series of mesovortices as the line moved into far eastern South Dakota, southwest Minnesota and northwest Iowa.  Several brief tornadoes developed with mesovortices in southwest Minnesota and northwest Iowa, along with areas of enhanced wind damage.

A colossal late winter storm began to develop early in the morning of March 13, with intense low pressure across southeast Colorado by 0600CST.  A surge of warm air ahead of this system resulted in bands of widespread liquid precipitation, along with scattered thunderstorms developing through the day on the 13th, and continuing as a stacked cyclone moved into western Iowa by 0600CST March 14.||Rain, with some areas of freezing rain resulting in around a tenth of an inch ice accumulation around Huron and Chamberlain, started to change to snow very late on March 13 into the early hours of the 14th.  As winds increased to 30 to 40 mph with gusts to 55 to 65 mph, whiteout conditions developed from the James River westward during the brief window of snowfall despite a limited accumulation of new snow (generally less than 2 inches) and widespread rainfall over the existing snow cover.  ||In addition, with the ground largely frozen to depths of 10 to 20 inches, antecedent snow cover with as much as 4 to 6 inches of liquid water equivalent, and the rapid warming of the air mass and resulting widespread rainfall from 1 to 3 inches, major flooding resulted across the region. ||Record precipitation on March 13 included, but was not limited to De Smet (1.90 inches), Vermillion (1.55 inches), Platte (1.86 inches), Gregory (1.26 inches), Mitchell (1.82 inches), Huron (1.43 inches), Primghar (1.98 inches), Menno (1.81 inches), Sioux City (1.63 inches), and Sioux Falls (1.91 inches).||Peak wind gusts of 58 mph or higher for the storm included: 67 mph at Mitchell, 65 mph 5 miles west of Geddes, 63 mph at Huron, 61 mph at Spencer, 60 mph at Sioux Falls, Jackson, and 2 miles west of Dovray, 59 mph at Gregory, Madison and Marshall, and 58 mph at Chamberlain, near Beaver Creek, and Sioux City.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: After an appreciable amount of rainfall and some light freezing rain resulting in up to a tenth of an inch ice accumulation, precipitation changed to snow after 0000CST March 14.  As northwest winds increased and frequent gusts topped 45 mph, periods of whiteout conditions developed during the morning hours on March 14 with travel not advised across the county.  Snowfall accumulated to 0.9 inches at Geddes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rainfall from 2 to 3 inches fell across middle portions of the Big Sioux River Basin between August 15 and August 18.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Big Sioux River near Estelline (gage site BSES2) crested at 0.28 feet above flood stage on August 20. Significant amounts of agricultural land were flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Relentless rainfall during July 2019 brought basin averages from 6 to 10 inches north of Interstate 90 and 4 to 6 inches south of Interstate 90. As a result, flooding along the Big Sioux River either continued, or was renewed, during the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Big Sioux River at Trent (gage site TRES2) crested 1.83 feet above flood stage on July 16, flooding a significant amount of agricultural land and a few county roads near the gage.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The James River near Scotland (gage site SCOS2) crested at 3.24 feet above flood stage on July 19. Many rural roads near the river were inundated, with flooding of significant amounts of agricultural land.  Moderate flooding continued at the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A colossal late winter storm began to develop early in the morning of March 13, with intense low pressure across southeast Colorado by 0600CST.  A surge of warm air ahead of this system resulted in bands of widespread liquid precipitation, along with scattered thunderstorms developing through the day on the 13th, and continuing as a stacked cyclone moved into western Iowa by 0600CST March 14.||Rain, with some areas of freezing rain resulting in around a tenth of an inch ice accumulation around Huron and Chamberlain, started to change to snow very late on March 13 into the early hours of the 14th.  As winds increased to 30 to 40 mph with gusts to 55 to 65 mph, whiteout conditions developed from the James River westward during the brief window of snowfall despite a limited accumulation of new snow (generally less than 2 inches) and widespread rainfall over the existing snow cover.  ||In addition, with the ground largely frozen to depths of 10 to 20 inches, antecedent snow cover with as much as 4 to 6 inches of liquid water equivalent, and the rapid warming of the air mass and resulting widespread rainfall from 1 to 3 inches, major flooding resulted across the region. ||Record precipitation on March 13 included, but was not limited to De Smet (1.90 inches), Vermillion (1.55 inches), Platte (1.86 inches), Gregory (1.26 inches), Mitchell (1.82 inches), Huron (1.43 inches), Primghar (1.98 inches), Menno (1.81 inches), Sioux City (1.63 inches), and Sioux Falls (1.91 inches).||Peak wind gusts of 58 mph or higher for the storm included: 67 mph at Mitchell, 65 mph 5 miles west of Geddes, 63 mph at Huron, 61 mph at Spencer, 60 mph at Sioux Falls, Jackson, and 2 miles west of Dovray, 59 mph at Gregory, Madison and Marshall, and 58 mph at Chamberlain, near Beaver Creek, and Sioux City.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: After an appreciable amount of rainfall and some light freezing rain resulting in up to a tenth of an inch ice accumulation, precipitation changed to snow after 2200CST March 13.  As northwest winds increased and frequent gusts topped 45 mph, periods of whiteout conditions developed during the morning hours on March 14 with travel not advised across the county.  Snowfall accumulated to 2.5 inches 2 miles southeast of Wessington and 2.2 inches at Huron.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A historic flooding event unfolded as heavy rainfall drenched the area on March 13-14, 2019.  With frozen and impervious ground, full and rapid runoff was maximized. In addition to the rainfall, between 1 and 3 inches of snow water equivalent remained in the snow pack south of Interstate 90, with 3 to 6 inches of snow water equivalent north of Interstate 90. Fifteen river gage sites measured record stages around the area.  Winds gusted to 50 to 70 mph at times, and colder air filtering into the system at the end of the storm produced a light  accumulation of ice west of the James River and from 1 to 4 inches of snowfall in parts of southeast South Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow melt and heavy rainfall (measured 2.47 inches at Forestburg and 2.25 inches at Mitchell) resulted in significant flooding of agricultural lands and inundation of numerous county roads (including 257th St. near the gage site and 1st Avenue and 416th Avenue near Rosedale Colony). The James River at Mitchell (gage site JRMS2) reached major flood stage and the 5th highest crest on record to date, 6.6 feet above flood stage on March 25. Moderate flooding continued at the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Periodic moderate to heavy rainfall allowed flooding to persist through the month along the middle portions of the James River.  More than twice the normal rainfall occurred at Huron and Forestburg during July.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A continuation of flooding from June, the James River near Mitchell (gage site JRMS2) crested 4.74 feet above flood stage on July 12, with a secondary crest 3.17 feet above flood stage on July 30. Many rural roads near the river were inundated, with continued flooding of significant amounts of agricultural land. Minor flooding continued at the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rainfall from 2 to 3 inches fell across middle portions of the Big Sioux River Basin between August 15 and August 18.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Big Sioux River near Bruce (gage site BRCS2) crested 0.24 feet above flood stage with flooding of agricultural land.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rainfall from 2 to 3 inches fell across middle portions of the Big Sioux River Basin between August 15 and August 18.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Big Sioux River near Brookings (gage site BRKS2) crested at 0.21 feet above flood stage. Significant amounts of agricultural land remained flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rainfall from 2 to 3 inches fell across middle portions of the Big Sioux River Basin between August 15 and August 18.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Big Sioux River at Sioux Falls I-90 (gage site SFLS2) crested at 0.09 feet above flood stage on August 22. Significant amounts of agricultural land were flooded north of Sioux Falls.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The spotter estimated wind gusts at 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex of thunderstorms developed during the evening across western South Dakota, moving east and becoming more organized as the low-level jet increased focus into south central and southeast South Dakota late at night.  Deep-layer effective shear reached 35 to 45 knots with MUCAPE to 2000J/kg. 0-3km shear was 25 to 35 knots, largely normal to the orientation of the convective line.  A large-scale bowing segment evolved quickly with a long track of damaging wind across southeast South Dakota into northwest Iowa.  Several mesovortices developed along the line and produced swaths of enhanced wind damage, many with winds from 80 to 90 mph, and as high as 115 mph.  Three people were injured as the strong winds went through a campground, tearing up trees and tents, and rolling campers.||A rare, but short-lived, late night tornado was spawned near one of the stronger mesovortices.  ||Areas of high rainfall rates resulted in spotty urban flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree fell on a power line in Lennox, cutting off service to more than 660 customers for several hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Close on the heels of the last winter storm, an amplified upper-air pattern brought another impulse out of the southwestern U.S. early on Friday, November 29 and pushed a shield of light mixed precipitation northeastward across the region with a light accumulation of ice.  As the main trough and area of low pressure organized on Saturday, November 30 across Nebraska and deepened as it moved into southern Wisconsin by the morning of Sunday, December 1, additional snowfall developed and was accompanied by strong and gusty northwest winds of 30 to 50 mph by later Saturday night into Sunday morning.||Snowfall totals over the period generally ranged from 6 to 16 inches west of the James River,  to less than one inch from northeast Nebraska and far southeast South Dakota toward southern portions of northwest Iowa.  Another mesoscale band of heavy snowfall occurred on Saturday, November 30 near the Minnesota-Iowa border with between 6 and 9 inches accumulation.||This storm continued into December 1, although a majority of accumulating snowfall ended between 0000 and 0900CST.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mixed precipitation, with occasional light freezing rain or freezing drizzle, began late afternoon on November 29, and resulted in up to a tenth of an inch ice accumulation through the early morning of November 30. Snow developed again by early morning, becoming heavy at times during the afternoon. Snowfall totals by early Sunday reached 7.0 inches 4 miles southwest of Stickney with 4.8 inches at White Lake.  In addition, winds gusted to 48 mph near Plankinton with areas of blowing snow with visibility of a half mile or less through Sunday morning. Travel was not recommended by late afternoon of November 30.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rainfall of one to three inches on frozen ground and into a snow pack with between 2 and 5 inches of liquid water equivalent resulted in considerable overland flooding.||One of the hardest hit areas was around Yankton, where a No Travel Advisory was issued for the city on March 13-14.  Businesses along north Highway 81 toward Yankton Mall had considerable ponding of water.  Water rescues were necessary as cars stalled in high water in low spots around Yankton.  Two people were also rescued from their inundated vehicle on Jim River Road east of Yankton. Water also got into power substations and caused spotty power outages and necessitated temporary evacuation of residents.||State government offices across the area were closed on March 14 due to the flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding resulted in damage to public infrastructure including county and township roads and culverts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Elevated instability from 1000-2000 J/kg along with 40-50 kt deep-layer shear sustained organized convection pushing though southeast South Dakota, southwest Minnesota and northwest Iowa.  Winds averaged from 60 to 80 mph along the path of the large scale bow echo, producing significant damage to trees and power lines, and minor damage to buildings along the path.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree damage in Flandreau.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High water levels from April continued at the start of May 2019 for many locations along the Big Sioux, James, and Missouri Rivers.  For many locations, this started the third month of constant flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This is a continuation of flooding from April. Continued higher than average releases from Fort Randall dam kept downstream water levels high through the month.  During two periods (May 21-26 and May 27-31), the water crested at major flood levels, inundating some roadways near the river.  Missouri River levels crested 4.22 feet above flood stage at Niobrara (gage site MRNN1) on May 30.  Moderate flooding continued into June.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High water levels from April continued at the start of May 2019 for many locations along the Big Sioux, James, and Missouri Rivers.  For many locations, this started the third month of constant flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This is a continuation of flooding from April. Persistent higher than average releases from Fort Randall dam kept downstream water levels high through the month. By late month, additional rains helped to push the river into moderate flood levels, bringing water to some homes near Springfield Recreation Area. Missouri River levels crested 2.17 feet above flood stage at Springfield (gage site SPGS2) on May 31.  Minor flooding persisted into June.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread rainfall from 1 to 2 inches in the Vermillion River basin between May 7-9 led to a brief flooding episode toward the middle of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Vermillion River near Wakonda (gage site WKAS2) reached a crest of 2.53 feet above flood stage on May 13. Over 10000 acres were again flooded between Centerville and Vermillion, causing additional delays to spring planting activities in the flood plain. Several county and state roads were again inundated by flood waters, including Highway 19 near 299th Street.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Vermillion River near Davis (gage site DVSS2) reached a crest of 1.56 feet above flood stage on May 11. Water briefly flowed across a section of U.S. Highway 18 west of Davis and additional delays to spring planting occurred in the flood plain.

A severe thunderstorm developed northwest of Rapid City and moved into portions of Rapid City before weakening. Large hail was reported from Summerset to the Black Hawk area. One to two inches of rain in a short time caused flash flooding from northern Rapid City to Box Elder. Some roads were flooded north of Interstate 90, with three water rescues of people stuck in cars on flooded roads. Rises on Boxelder Creek flooded a home along the creek in Box Elder.
A thunderstorm briefly became severe north of Sturgis, producing quarter sized hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex of thunderstorms developed during the evening across western South Dakota, moving east and becoming more organized as the low-level jet increased focus into south central and southeast South Dakota late at night.  Deep-layer effective shear reached 35 to 45 knots with MUCAPE to 2000J/kg. 0-3km shear was 25 to 35 knots, largely normal to the orientation of the convective line.  A large-scale bowing segment evolved quickly with a long track of damaging wind across southeast South Dakota into northwest Iowa.  Several mesovortices developed along the line and produced swaths of enhanced wind damage, many with winds from 80 to 90 mph, and as high as 115 mph.  Three people were injured as the strong winds went through a campground, tearing up trees and tents, and rolling campers.||A rare, but short-lived, late night tornado was spawned near one of the stronger mesovortices.  ||Areas of high rainfall rates resulted in spotty urban flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind damage at farmstead to buildings and trees.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: This is a continuation of flooding from April. The Big Sioux River near Flandreau (gage site BSFS2) spent nearly the entire month in flood stage, with a week hovering just below flood stage from May 15-19.  An early month crest at 1.10 feet above flood stage occurred on May 12, with a second crest spurred by late month rains at 1.29 feet above flood stage on May 28. Significant agricultural flooding persisted, with no opportunity for spring planting throughout the flood plain. Near crest, water impacted the city park in Flandreau. The river remained at minor flood stage at the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm frontal boundary across Nebraska worked slowly northward into southeast South Dakota by the evening of May 17 in advance of low pressure which strengthened over the western High Plains.  A volatile air mass with around 2500 J/kg of elevated instability was in place, with deep layer shear as much as 50-60 knots.  As a result, numerous thunderstorms developed across southeast South Dakota during the early evening. One cell strengthened and briefly became severe producing large hail and damaging wind.  The repeated thunderstorm development and warm environment aloft resulted in heavy rainfall rates, which produced some flash flooding with the assistance of a more impervious urban environment.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water reached up to 2 feet in depth in many city intersections.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sixmile Creek above Brookings (gage site SMCS2) climbed above flood stage on two brief instances mid-month. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Close on the heels of the last winter storm, an amplified upper-air pattern brought another impulse out of the southwestern U.S. early on Friday, November 29 and pushed a shield of light mixed precipitation northeastward across the region with a light accumulation of ice.  As the main trough and area of low pressure organized on Saturday, November 30 across Nebraska and deepened as it moved into southern Wisconsin by the morning of Sunday, December 1, additional snowfall developed and was accompanied by strong and gusty northwest winds of 30 to 50 mph by later Saturday night into Sunday morning.||Snowfall totals over the period generally ranged from 6 to 16 inches west of the James River,  to less than one inch from northeast Nebraska and far southeast South Dakota toward southern portions of northwest Iowa.  Another mesoscale band of heavy snowfall occurred on Saturday, November 30 near the Minnesota-Iowa border with between 6 and 9 inches accumulation.||This storm continued into December 1, although a majority of accumulating snowfall ended between 0000 and 0900CST.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mixed precipitation, with occasional light freezing rain or freezing drizzle, began late afternoon on November 29, and resulted in up to a tenth of an inch ice accumulation through the early evening of November 29. Snow developed again overnight, becoming heavy at times Saturday. Snowfall totals by early Sunday reached 7.0 inches at Platte and 6.0 inches 2 miles northeast of Academy.  In addition, winds gusted to 50 mph 12 miles west of Platte and 39 mph at Wagner, with areas of blowing snow with visibility of a half mile or less through Sunday morning. Travel was not recommended by late afternoon of November 30.

A complex of thunderstorms developed during the evening across western South Dakota, moving east and becoming more organized as the low-level jet increased focus into south central and southeast South Dakota late at night.  Deep-layer effective shear reached 35 to 45 knots with MUCAPE to 2000J/kg. 0-3km shear was 25 to 35 knots, largely normal to the orientation of the convective line.  A large-scale bowing segment evolved quickly with a long track of damaging wind across southeast South Dakota into northwest Iowa.  Several mesovortices developed along the line and produced swaths of enhanced wind damage, many with winds from 80 to 90 mph, and as high as 115 mph.  Three people were injured as the strong winds went through a campground, tearing up trees and tents, and rolling campers.||A rare, but short-lived, late night tornado was spawned near one of the stronger mesovortices.  ||Areas of high rainfall rates resulted in spotty urban flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large tree limb broken off and tossed on top of car.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Elevated instability from 1000-2000 J/kg along with 40-50 kt deep-layer shear sustained organized convection pushing though southeast South Dakota, southwest Minnesota and northwest Iowa.  Winds averaged from 60 to 80 mph along the path of the large scale bow echo, producing significant damage to trees and power lines, and minor damage to buildings along the path.

High water levels from April continued at the start of May 2019 for many locations along the Big Sioux, James, and Missouri Rivers.  For many locations, this started the third month of constant flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This is a continuation of flooding from April. The Big Sioux River above Hawarden (gage site HWDI4) remained in flood through the month. Late month rains pushed the river to a crest at 10.33 feet above flood stage on May 29, the third highest stage on record. Significant agricultural flooding persisted, with no opportunity for spring planting throughout the flood plain. County road 13, Iowa Highway 10 and South Dakota Highway 46 were again subjected to closure at the end of the month. The river remained at major flood stage at the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High water levels from April continued at the start of May 2019 for many locations along the Big Sioux, James, and Missouri Rivers.  For many locations, this started the third month of constant flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This is a continuation of flooding from April. The Big Sioux River near Richland (gage site BSRS2) remained in flood through the month. Late month rains pushed the river to a crest at 5.21 feet above flood stage on May 30. Significant agricultural flooding persisted, with no opportunity for spring planting throughout the flood plain. A few farm levees were overtopped on the South Dakota side with flooding of several outbuildings. The river remained at major flood stage at the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High water levels from April continued at the start of May 2019 for many locations along the Big Sioux, James, and Missouri Rivers.  For many locations, this started the third month of constant flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This is a continuation of flooding from April. Split Rock Creek below Jasper (gage site JSPM5) saw flooding slowly end at the start of the month. Starting the month at 0.51 feet above flood stage, the river fell below flood stage on May 2. Low water crossings on 250th and 251st Streets on the Minnesota/South Dakota border were flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High water levels from April continued at the start of May 2019 for many locations along the Big Sioux, James, and Missouri Rivers.  For many locations, this started the third month of constant flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This is a continuation of flooding from April. The Big Sioux River near Estelline (gage site BSES2) spent the entire month in flood stage, with a pair of crests into moderate flood stage during the month. The first crest 1.83 feet above flood stage occurred on May 9, with a second crest spurred by late month rains at 1.70 feet above flood stage. Significant agricultural flooding persisted, with no opportunity for spring planting throughout the flood plain. The river remained at minor flood stage at the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A widespread rainfall of 2 to 4 inches between May 21 and May 27 led to significant rises on most rivers, worsening of flooding in existing flooded area, and expansion of flooding to more areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Increased releases from upstream dams along with heavy rainfall produced major flooding on the Missouri River at Chamberlain (gage site CBLS2).  Several recreational boat ramps were flooded along with the American Creek Recreational Area as the river reached the third highest crest on record (5.87 feet above flood stage) on May 31.  The river remained in major flood stage at the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A widespread rainfall of 2 to 4 inches between May 21 and May 27 led to significant rises on most rivers, worsening of flooding in existing flooded area, and expansion of flooding to more areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Increased releases from upstream Fort Randall Dam along with heavy rainfall over the preceding week produced minor flooding on the Missouri River near Greenwood (gage site GRWS2). Agricultural areas near the river were subjected to flooding as the river rose to 0.08 feet above flood stage on May 31. The river remained in minor flood stage at the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A widespread rainfall of 2 to 4 inches between May 21 and May 27 led to significant rises on most rivers, worsening of flooding in existing flooded area, and expansion of flooding to more areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Increased releases from upstream Fort Randall Dam along with heavy rainfall over the preceding week produced minor flooding on the Missouri River near Verdel (gage site MRVN1). A few recreational homes were subjected to flooding as the river rose to 0.75 feet above flood stage on May 30.  The river remained in minor flood stage at the end of the month.

Heavy rainfall brought both branches of Mud Creek near Rauville above flood stage for a short period of time from September 12th to the 13th. The rises were only from a half foot to a foot above flood stage.

A strong winter storm created numerous impacts across central and northeast South Dakota from late on December 27th until early on the 30th. Snowfall amounts ranged from 6 to as much of 20 inches. The heaviest precipitation came in two waves, with the first wave arriving during the late evening of December 27th and continuing into December 28th.  Behind this first wave of heavy snowfall, there was a period of light snow, drizzle, and freezing drizzle on Saturday the 28th for areas of northeast South Dakota.  The second wave of precipitation was all snow and arrived on the evening of December 28th and continued into December 30th. ||The winter storm evolved into a blizzard across much of the region. The combination of the snow and blowing snow severely impacted road conditions and travel across the region. No Travel Advised was designated for most all state and county roads and even the closure of I-90 and I-29 in parts of South Dakota. Some accidents occurred along with several vehicles sliding into the ditch. Many events were postponed or cancelled along with many state office openings delayed on Monday, December 30th. ||Some of the heaviest snowfall amounts include, 8 inches at Highmore, McLaughlin, and Onida; 9 inches at Castlewood, Doland, Sisseton, Mission Ridge, and Mobridge; 10 inches at Watertown, Ipswich, Aberdeen, and Eureka; 11 inches at Murdo and Milbank. Locations with a foot of more include, 12 inches at Roy Lake, Britton, Wilmot, and Clark; 13 inches at Summit; 14 inches at Bowdle; 15 inches at Seneca and Artas; 16 inches near Elm Lake; 17 inches at Roscoe; 18 inches near Herreid, and 20 inches at Clear Lake.  See the storm data entry for the blizzard.

Many events were postponed or cancelled along with many state office openings delayed on Monday, December 30th. ||Some of the heaviest snowfall amounts include, 8 inches at Highmore, McLaughlin, and Onida; 9 inches at Castlewood, Doland, Sisseton, Mission Ridge, and Mobridge; 10 inches at Watertown, Ipswich, Aberdeen, and Eureka; 11 inches at Murdo and Milbank. Locations with a foot of more include, 12 inches at Roy Lake, Britton, Wilmot, and Clark; 13 inches at Summit; 14 inches at Bowdle; 15 inches at Seneca and Artas; 16 inches near Elm Lake; 17 inches at Roscoe; 18 inches near Herreid, and 20 inches at Clear Lake.  See the storm data entry for the blizzard.

A winter storm turned into a blizzard for much of the region as strong winds developed as an intensifying area of low pressure moved into the southern half of Minnesota. This led to wind gusts of 50 to over 60 mph creating blizzard conditions from the Missouri River east to the James River Valley/western edge of the Sisseton Hills. The combination of the snow and blowing snow severely impacted road conditions and travel across the region. No Travel Advised was designated for most all state and county roads and even the closure of I-90 and I-29 in parts of South Dakota.||Some accidents occurred along with several vehicles sliding into the ditch. Many events were postponed or cancelled along with many state office openings delayed on Monday, December 30th.  The heavy snow and blizzard winds brought power outages to almost a thousand people in northeast South Dakota. Snowfall amounts ranged from 6 to as much as 20 inches as the blizzard subsided and the snow ended early on the 30th. See the storm data entry for the winter storm.

Close on the heels of the last winter storm, an amplified upper-air pattern brought another impulse out of the southwestern U.S. early on Friday, November 29 and pushed a shield of light mixed precipitation northeastward across the region with a light accumulation of ice. As the main trough and area of low pressure organized on Saturday, November 30 across Nebraska and deepened as it moved into southern Wisconsin by the morning of Sunday, December 1, additional snowfall developed and was accompanied by strong and gusty northwest winds of 30 to 50 mph by later Saturday night into Sunday morning.||Snowfall totals over the period generally ranged from 6 to 16 inches west of the James River, to less than one inch from northeast Nebraska and far southeast South Dakota toward southern portions of northwest Iowa. Another mesoscale band of heavy snowfall occurred on Saturday, November 30 near the Minnesota-Iowa border with between 6 and 9 inches accumulation.||This storm continued into December 1, although a majority of accumulating snowfall ended between 0000 and 0900CST. There were a total of 6 injuries (indirect) and one fatality (indirect) during the event. See November 2019 Storm Data episode for more information.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals by early December 1 reached 8.0 inches 5 miles west of Armour. In addition, winds gusted to 48 mph near Plankinton and 39 mph at Wagner with areas of blowing snow with visibility of a half mile or less at times through early morning. Travel was not recommended by late afternoon of November 30. See the 11/29 Winter Storm event at 1700CST for more details.

See the 11/29 Winter Storm event at 1700CST for more details.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep trough across the southwestern U.S. in the early morning of Saturday, December 28 pushed eastward through the day, allowing low pressure to organize near the Oklahoma and Texas Panhandles.  The storm intensified as it moved into eastern Nebraska and western Iowa by 0600CST December 29.  Strong warming aloft ahead of the system led to periods of light rain or light mixed precipitation, while heavy snow organized in the trowal over central and northeast South Dakota. ||Impacts from the storm became significant as the storm closed off in the mid-levels and slowed movement, only reaching southern Minnesota by 0600CST December 30.  Precipitation changed over to snow as colder air collapsed southward, and winds increased to a gusty 40 to 55 mph across southeast South Dakota and adjacent areas.  Snowfall totals ranged from 10 to 18 inches from the James River Valley westward, to 1 to 3 inches across much of northwest Iowa.  The storm resulted in record daily precipitation for Sioux Falls (0.40 inches on December 28 and 0.69 inches on December 29), as well as record daily precipitation (0.64 inches) and snowfall (7.0 inches) for Huron on December 28.||In total, South Dakota State Patrol reported 49 injury accidents and 44 slide-ins across southeast South Dakota during the episode.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light mixed precipitation resulted in a minor glaze of ice accumulation on December 28 before temperatures climbed above freezing. Snowfall developed early on December 29, and continued into December 30 producing hazardous travel. Snowfall totaled 3.8 inches at Canton and near 4 inches in southern Sioux Falls. Wind gusts reached as high as 46 mph near Davis on Monday, December 30, resulting in blowing and drifting snow.

The storm intensified as it moved into eastern Nebraska and western Iowa by 0600CST December 29.  Strong warming aloft ahead of the system led to periods of light rain or light mixed precipitation, while heavy snow organized in the trowal over central and northeast South Dakota. ||Impacts from the storm became significant as the storm closed off in the mid-levels and slowed movement, only reaching southern Minnesota by 0600CST December 30. EVENT_NARRATIVE: With surface temperatures hovering near freezing, light mixed precipitation resulted in a minor glaze of ice accumulation on December 28 and early December 29. Snowfall developed early on December 29, and continued heavy at times into December 30. Winds gusted as high as 49 mph at Mitchell after 1200CST December 29, resulting in white out conditions at times into early evening with travel not recommended. Interstate 90 was closed west of Mitchell from 1900CST December 28 until 1100CST December 30. Snowfall totaled 15.0 inches at Mitchell. Snow drifts to several feet were common. On December 30, the opening of state offices was delayed until 1300CST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow melt and some ice action along the upper portions of the Big Sioux river basin resulted in minor flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: With a period of snow melt and complication from minor ice jams, the Big Sioux River near Brookings (gage site BRKS2) climbed back into flood stage and crested at 0.70 feet above flood stage on December 14. A few county and township roads were flooded near the gage site, along with a considerable amount of agricultural land.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep low pressure system tracked across the Northern Plains in the days following Thanksgiving, and produced widespread heavy snow across most of central and northeast South Dakota. Areas of freezing drizzle occurred on Thanksgiving Day and on Friday the 29th, bringing some spotty icy conditions. Heavy, wet snow then tracked from south to north across the area from the late afternoon of the 29th persisting into the Saturday the 30th. The snow then ended during the morning hours of December 1st. See November 2019 storm data. ||Northwest winds gusting to 30 to 50 mph caused areas of blowing snow reducing visibilities, affecting holiday travel. Road conditions deteriorated quickly through the event, with many No Travel Advised statements as well as the closure of Interstate-90 from Chamberlain to the Wyoming border Friday night through Sunday morning, December 1st. Several accidents occurred across the region.  By the time the storm let up in the morning hours of December 1st, anywhere from 6 to 17 inches of snow occurred.||Some snowfall amounts include, 7 inches at Clear Lake, Milbank and Mobridge; 8 inches at Pierre and Sisseton; 9 inches at Miller; 10 inches at Roy Lake; 11 inches at Murdo, Faulkton, and Highmore; and 12 inches at Bowdle and Summit.  Locations with more than a foot include, 13 inches at Presho; 14 inches at Kennebec and Gettysburg; 15 inches at Roscoe and Ipswich; 16 inches at McLaughlin and Eureka; and 17 inches at Aberdeen. Aberdeen's 17 inches was 4 inches off the all-time three-day record of 21 inches from November 23rd to the 25th, 1993.

See the storm data entry for the blizzard.

The James River remained above flood stage throughout December across Brown and into  Spink county. The river was approaching or broke the record for the number of days continuously above flood stage at Columbia, Stratford, and Ashton. At the end of December, Columbia broke it's record by seven days being continuously above flood stage for 273 days. Ashton was just one day off the record by the end of December with 274 days above flood stage. Stratford was 24 days shy of the record with 272 days continuously above flood stage. The flooding continued into January 2020.

A winter storm turned into a blizzard for much of the region as strong winds developed as an intensifying area of low pressure moved into the southern half of Minnesota. This led to wind gusts of 50 to over 60 mph creating blizzard conditions from the Missouri River east to the James River Valley/western edge of the Sisseton Hills. The combination of the snow and blowing snow severely impacted road conditions and travel across the region. No Travel Advised was designated for most all state and county roads and even the closure of I-90 and I-29 in parts of South Dakota.||Some accidents occurred along with several vehicles sliding into the ditch. Many events were postponed or cancelled along with many state office openings delayed on Monday, December 30th.  The heavy snow and blizzard winds brought power outages to almost a thousand people in northeast South Dakota. Snowfall amounts ranged from 6 to as much as 20 inches as the blizzard subsided and the snow ended early on the 30th. See the storm data entry for the winter storm.

Close on the heels of the last winter storm, an amplified upper-air pattern brought another impulse out of the southwestern U.S. early on Friday, November 29 and pushed a shield of light mixed precipitation northeastward across the region with a light accumulation of ice. As the main trough and area of low pressure organized on Saturday, November 30 across Nebraska and deepened as it moved into southern Wisconsin by the morning of Sunday, December 1, additional snowfall developed and was accompanied by strong and gusty northwest winds of 30 to 50 mph by later Saturday night into Sunday morning.||Snowfall totals over the period generally ranged from 6 to 16 inches west of the James River, to less than one inch from northeast Nebraska and far southeast South Dakota toward southern portions of northwest Iowa. Another mesoscale band of heavy snowfall occurred on Saturday, November 30 near the Minnesota-Iowa border with between 6 and 9 inches accumulation.||This storm continued into December 1, although a majority of accumulating snowfall ended between 0000 and 0900CST. There were a total of 6 injuries (indirect) and one fatality (indirect) during the event. See November 2019 Storm Data episode for more information.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals by early December 1 reached 4.0 inches 2 miles south southeast of Astoria and 1.5 inches at Brookings. In addition, winds gusted to 36 mph at Brookings with patchy reduction in visibility below 1 mile in blowing snow. An adult driver and two child passengers were injured (indirectly) when an SUV lost control on icy roads a mile-and-a-half south of White shortly before 1300CST November 30. The vehicle ramped off a ditch and went briefly airborne before coming to rest in a field. See the 11/28 Winter Weather event at 2200CST for more details.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep trough across the southwestern U.S. in the early morning of Saturday, December 28 pushed eastward through the day, allowing low pressure to organize near the Oklahoma and Texas Panhandles.  The storm intensified as it moved into eastern Nebraska and western Iowa by 0600CST December 29.  Strong warming aloft ahead of the system led to periods of light rain or light mixed precipitation, while heavy snow organized in the trowal over central and northeast South Dakota. ||Impacts from the storm became significant as the storm closed off in the mid-levels and slowed movement, only reaching southern Minnesota by 0600CST December 30.  Precipitation changed over to snow as colder air collapsed southward, and winds increased to a gusty 40 to 55 mph across southeast South Dakota and adjacent areas.  Snowfall totals ranged from 10 to 18 inches from the James River Valley westward, to 1 to 3 inches across much of northwest Iowa.  The storm resulted in record daily precipitation for Sioux Falls (0.40 inches on December 28 and 0.69 inches on December 29), as well as record daily precipitation (0.64 inches) and snowfall (7.0 inches) for Huron on December 28.||In total, South Dakota State Patrol reported 49 injury accidents and 44 slide-ins across southeast South Dakota during the episode.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light mixed precipitation changed quickly to snow early on December 29, and continued into December 30 producing hazardous travel. Snowfall totaled 4.0 inches 6 miles southeast of Centerville and 1.5 inches at Vermillion. Wind gusts reached as high as 37 mph 6 miles southeast of Centerville by late Sunday, December 29, resulting in blowing and drifting snow.

Strong warming aloft ahead of the system led to periods of light rain or light mixed precipitation, while heavy snow organized in the trowal over central and northeast South Dakota. ||Impacts from the storm became significant as the storm closed off in the mid-levels and slowed movement, only reaching southern Minnesota by 0600CST December 30. EVENT_NARRATIVE: With surface temperatures hovering near freezing, light mixed precipitation resulted in a minor glaze of ice accumulation on December 28 and early December 29. Snowfall developed early on December 29, and continued heavy at times into December 30. Winds gusted as high as 47 mph near Freeman, 45 mph near Tripp and 37 mph at Parkston after 1200CST December 29, resulting in white out conditions at times into early evening with travel not recommended. Snowfall totaled 9.0 inches at Parkston and Menno, and 7.3 inches 11 miles south of Emery. On December 30, the opening of state offices was delayed until 1300CST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep low pressure system tracked across the Northern Plains in the days following Thanksgiving, and produced widespread heavy snow across most of central and northeast South Dakota. Areas of freezing drizzle occurred on Thanksgiving Day and on Friday the 29th, bringing some spotty icy conditions. Heavy, wet snow then tracked from south to north across the area from the late afternoon of the 29th persisting into the Saturday the 30th. The snow then ended during the morning hours of December 1st. See November 2019 storm data. ||Northwest winds gusting to 30 to 50 mph caused areas of blowing snow reducing visibilities, affecting holiday travel. Road conditions deteriorated quickly through the event, with many No Travel Advised statements as well as the closure of Interstate-90 from Chamberlain to the Wyoming border Friday night through Sunday morning, December 1st. Several accidents occurred across the region.  By the time the storm let up in the morning hours of December 1st, anywhere from 6 to 17 inches of snow occurred.||Some snowfall amounts include, 7 inches at Clear Lake, Milbank and Mobridge; 8 inches at Pierre and Sisseton; 9 inches at Miller; 10 inches at Roy Lake; 11 inches at Murdo, Faulkton, and Highmore; and 12 inches at Bowdle and Summit.  Locations with more than a foot include, 13 inches at Presho; 14 inches at Kennebec and Gettysburg; 15 inches at Roscoe and Ipswich; 16 inches at McLaughlin and Eureka; and 17 inches at Aberdeen. Aberdeen's 17 inches was 4 inches off the all-time three-day record of 21 inches from November 23rd to the 25th, 1993.

The James River remained above flood stage throughout December across Brown and into  Spink county. The river was approaching or broke the record for the number of days continuously above flood stage at Columbia, Stratford, and Ashton. At the end of December, Columbia broke it's record by seven days being continuously above flood stage for 273 days. Ashton was just one day off the record by the end of December with 274 days above flood stage. Stratford was 24 days shy of the record with 272 days continuously above flood stage. The flooding continued into January 2020.

Reduced flow through the Missouri River system allowed the river to fall below flood stage early in December.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A continuation of flooding from November, the Missouri River near Greenwood (gage site GRWS2) began the month in minor flood at 0.96 feet above flood stage, and fell below flood stage on December 3. Impacts were minor.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Flow from upstream continued at high levels through the month of December.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A continuation of flooding from November, the James River near Mitchell (gage site JRMS2) spent the entire month in minor flood stage, with a brief period in moderate flooding between December 10-16.  A crest at 3.37 feet above flood stage occurred on December 13. Significant amounts of agricultural land remained flooded. Minor flooding continued at the end of the month.

The combination of the snow and blowing snow severely impacted road conditions and travel across the region. No Travel Advised was designated for most all state and county roads and even the closure of I-90 and I-29 in parts of South Dakota. Some accidents occurred along with several vehicles sliding into the ditch. Many events were postponed or cancelled along with many state office openings delayed on Monday, December 30th. ||Some of the heaviest snowfall amounts include, 8 inches at Highmore, McLaughlin, and Onida; 9 inches at Castlewood, Doland, Sisseton, Mission Ridge, and Mobridge; 10 inches at Watertown, Ipswich, Aberdeen, and Eureka; 11 inches at Murdo and Milbank. Locations with a foot of more include, 12 inches at Roy Lake, Britton, Wilmot, and Clark; 13 inches at Summit; 14 inches at Bowdle; 15 inches at Seneca and Artas; 16 inches near Elm Lake; 17 inches at Roscoe; 18 inches near Herreid, and 20 inches at Clear Lake.  See the storm data entry for the blizzard.

A winter storm turned into a blizzard for much of the region as strong winds developed as an intensifying area of low pressure moved into the southern half of Minnesota. This led to wind gusts of 50 to over 60 mph creating blizzard conditions from the Missouri River east to the James River Valley/western edge of the Sisseton Hills. The combination of the snow and blowing snow severely impacted road conditions and travel across the region. No Travel Advised was designated for most all state and county roads and even the closure of I-90 and I-29 in parts of South Dakota.||Some accidents occurred along with several vehicles sliding into the ditch. Many events were postponed or cancelled along with many state office openings delayed on Monday, December 30th.  The heavy snow and blizzard winds brought power outages to almost a thousand people in northeast South Dakota. Snowfall amounts ranged from 6 to as much as 20 inches as the blizzard subsided and the snow ended early on the 30th. See the storm data entry for the winter storm.
Flow from upstream continued at high levels through the month of December.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A continuation of flooding from November, the James River near Mitchell (gage site JRMS2) spent the entire month in minor flood stage, with a brief period in moderate flooding between December 10-16.  A crest at 3.37 feet above flood stage occurred on December 13. Significant amounts of agricultural land remained flooded. Minor flooding continued at the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Flow from upstream continued at high levels through the month of December.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A continuation of flooding from November, the James River near Mitchell (gage site JRMS2) spent the entire month in minor flood stage, with a brief period in moderate flooding between December 10-16.  A crest at 3.37 feet above flood stage occurred on December 13. Significant amounts of agricultural land remained flooded. Minor flooding continued at the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow melt and some ice action along the upper portions of the Big Sioux river basin resulted in minor flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow melt in the upper portions of the Big Sioux river basin pushed water levels near Bruce (gage site BRCS2) into minor flood stage, with a crest of 1.21 feet above flood stage on December 14. The stage was affected by small ice jams, but impacts were minimal with only minor flooding of agricultural land.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep trough across the southwestern U.S. in the early morning of Saturday, December 28 pushed eastward through the day, allowing low pressure to organize near the Oklahoma and Texas Panhandles.  The storm intensified as it moved into eastern Nebraska and western Iowa by 0600CST December 29.  Strong warming aloft ahead of the system led to periods of light rain or light mixed precipitation, while heavy snow organized in the trowal over central and northeast South Dakota. ||Impacts from the storm became significant as the storm closed off in the mid-levels and slowed movement, only reaching southern Minnesota by 0600CST December 30.  Precipitation changed over to snow as colder air collapsed southward, and winds increased to a gusty 40 to 55 mph across southeast South Dakota and adjacent areas.  Snowfall totals ranged from 10 to 18 inches from the James River Valley westward, to 1 to 3 inches across much of northwest Iowa.  The storm resulted in record daily precipitation for Sioux Falls (0.40 inches on December 28 and 0.69 inches on December 29), as well as record daily precipitation (0.64 inches) and snowfall (7.0 inches) for Huron on December 28.||In total, South Dakota State Patrol reported 49 injury accidents and 44 slide-ins across southeast South Dakota during the episode.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: With surface temperatures hovering near freezing, light mixed precipitation resulted in a minor glaze of ice accumulation on December 28 and early December 29. Snowfall developed early on December 29, and continued heavy at times into December 30. Winds gusted as high as 46 mph at Cavour and 45 mph at Arlington after 1200CST December 29, resulting in white out conditions at times into early evening with travel not recommended. Snowfall totaled 13.0 inches at De Smet and 11.4 inches at Iroquois.  On December 30, the opening of state offices was delayed until 1300CST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep low pressure system tracked across the Northern Plains in the days following Thanksgiving, and produced widespread heavy snow across most of central and northeast South Dakota. Areas of freezing drizzle occurred on Thanksgiving Day and on Friday the 29th, bringing some spotty icy conditions. Heavy, wet snow then tracked from south to north across the area from the late afternoon of the 29th persisting into the Saturday the 30th. The snow then ended during the morning hours of December 1st. See November 2019 storm data. ||Northwest winds gusting to 30 to 50 mph caused areas of blowing snow reducing visibilities, affecting holiday travel. Road conditions deteriorated quickly through the event, with many No Travel Advised statements as well as the closure of Interstate-90 from Chamberlain to the Wyoming border Friday night through Sunday morning, December 1st. Several accidents occurred across the region.  By the time the storm let up in the morning hours of December 1st, anywhere from 6 to 17 inches of snow occurred.||Some snowfall amounts include, 7 inches at Clear Lake, Milbank and Mobridge; 8 inches at Pierre and Sisseton; 9 inches at Miller; 10 inches at Roy Lake; 11 inches at Murdo, Faulkton, and Highmore; and 12 inches at Bowdle and Summit.  Locations with more than a foot include, 13 inches at Presho; 14 inches at Kennebec and Gettysburg; 15 inches at Roscoe and Ipswich; 16 inches at McLaughlin and Eureka; and 17 inches at Aberdeen. Aberdeen's 17 inches was 4 inches off the all-time three-day record of 21 inches from November 23rd to the 25th, 1993.

Close on the heels of the last winter storm, an amplified upper-air pattern brought another impulse out of the southwestern U.S. early on Friday, November 29 and pushed a shield of light mixed precipitation northeastward across the region with a light accumulation of ice. As the main trough and area of low pressure organized on Saturday, November 30 across Nebraska and deepened as it moved into southern Wisconsin by the morning of Sunday, December 1, additional snowfall developed and was accompanied by strong and gusty northwest winds of 30 to 50 mph by later Saturday night into Sunday morning.||Snowfall totals over the period generally ranged from 6 to 16 inches west of the James River, to less than one inch from northeast Nebraska and far southeast South Dakota toward southern portions of northwest Iowa. Another mesoscale band of heavy snowfall occurred on Saturday, November 30 near the Minnesota-Iowa border with between 6 and 9 inches accumulation.||This storm continued into December 1, although a majority of accumulating snowfall ended between 0000 and 0900CST. There were a total of 6 injuries (indirect) and one fatality (indirect) during the event. See November 2019 Storm Data episode for more information.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals by early Sunday reached 6.2 inches at Huron, 7.7 inches 1 mile south of Huron, and 11.0 inches 2 miles southeast of Wessington. In addition, winds gusted to 40 mph at Wolsey and 44 mph at Huron with areas of blowing snow. See the 11/28 Winter Storm event at 2100CST for more details.

Heavy, wet snow then tracked from south to north across the area from the late afternoon of the 29th persisting into the Saturday the 30th. The snow then ended during the morning hours of December 1st. See November 2019 storm data. ||Northwest winds gusting to 30 to 50 mph caused areas of blowing snow reducing visibilities, affecting holiday travel. Road conditions deteriorated quickly through the event, with many No Travel Advised statements as well as the closure of Interstate-90 from Chamberlain to the Wyoming border Friday night through Sunday morning, December 1st. Several accidents occurred across the region.  By the time the storm let up in the morning hours of December 1st, anywhere from 6 to 17 inches of snow occurred.||Some snowfall amounts include, 7 inches at Clear Lake, Milbank and Mobridge; 8 inches at Pierre and Sisseton; 9 inches at Miller; 10 inches at Roy Lake; 11 inches at Murdo, Faulkton, and Highmore; and 12 inches at Bowdle and Summit.  Locations with more than a foot include, 13 inches at Presho; 14 inches at Kennebec and Gettysburg; 15 inches at Roscoe and Ipswich; 16 inches at McLaughlin and Eureka; and 17 inches at Aberdeen. Aberdeen's 17 inches was 4 inches off the all-time three-day record of 21 inches from November 23rd to the 25th, 1993.
Flow from upstream continued at high levels through the month of December.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A continuation of minor flooding from November, the James River at Huron (gage site HURS2) spent nearly the entire month at moderate flood levels, with a crest at 3.72 feet above flood stage on December 16. Significant amounts of agricultural land remained flooded, along with several county and township roads near the river. Moderate flooding persisted at the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Close on the heels of the last winter storm, an amplified upper-air pattern brought another impulse out of the southwestern U.S. early on Friday, November 29 and pushed a shield of light mixed precipitation northeastward across the region with a light accumulation of ice. As the main trough and area of low pressure organized on Saturday, November 30 across Nebraska and deepened as it moved into southern Wisconsin by the morning of Sunday, December 1, additional snowfall developed and was accompanied by strong and gusty northwest winds of 30 to 50 mph by later Saturday night into Sunday morning.||Snowfall totals over the period generally ranged from 6 to 16 inches west of the James River, to less than one inch from northeast Nebraska and far southeast South Dakota toward southern portions of northwest Iowa. Another mesoscale band of heavy snowfall occurred on Saturday, November 30 near the Minnesota-Iowa border with between 6 and 9 inches accumulation.||This storm continued into December 1, although a majority of accumulating snowfall ended between 0000 and 0900CST. There were a total of 6 injuries (indirect) and one fatality (indirect) during the event. See November 2019 Storm Data episode for more information.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals by early December 1 reached 5.0 inches at Howard. In addition, winds gusted to 37 mph at Arlington and 40 mph at Madison with patchy reduction in visibility below 1 mile in blowing snow.  See the 11/29 Winter Weather event at 1700CST for more details.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm turned into a blizzard for much of the region as strong winds developed as an intensifying area of low pressure moved into the southern half of Minnesota. This led to wind gusts of 50 to over 60 mph creating blizzard conditions from the Missouri River east to the James River Valley/western edge of the Sisseton Hills. The combination of the snow and blowing snow severely impacted road conditions and travel across the region. No Travel Advised was designated for most all state and county roads and even the closure of I-90 and I-29 in parts of South Dakota.||Some accidents occurred along with several vehicles sliding into the ditch. Many events were postponed or cancelled along with many state office openings delayed on Monday, December 30th.  The heavy snow and blizzard winds brought power outages to almost a thousand people in northeast South Dakota. Snowfall amounts ranged from 6 to as much as 20 inches as the blizzard subsided and the snow ended early on the 30th. See the storm data entry for the winter storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep trough across the southwestern U.S. in the early morning of Saturday, December 28 pushed eastward through the day, allowing low pressure to organize near the Oklahoma and Texas Panhandles.  The storm intensified as it moved into eastern Nebraska and western Iowa by 0600CST December 29.  Strong warming aloft ahead of the system led to periods of light rain or light mixed precipitation, while heavy snow organized in the trowal over central and northeast South Dakota. ||Impacts from the storm became significant as the storm closed off in the mid-levels and slowed movement, only reaching southern Minnesota by 0600CST December 30.  Precipitation changed over to snow as colder air collapsed southward, and winds increased to a gusty 40 to 55 mph across southeast South Dakota and adjacent areas.  Snowfall totals ranged from 10 to 18 inches from the James River Valley westward, to 1 to 3 inches across much of northwest Iowa.  The storm resulted in record daily precipitation for Sioux Falls (0.40 inches on December 28 and 0.69 inches on December 29), as well as record daily precipitation (0.64 inches) and snowfall (7.0 inches) for Huron on December 28.||In total, South Dakota State Patrol reported 49 injury accidents and 44 slide-ins across southeast South Dakota during the episode.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: With surface temperatures hovering near freezing, light mixed precipitation resulted in a minor glaze of ice accumulation on December 28 and early December 29. Snowfall developed early on December 29, and continued heavy at times into December 30. Travel became extremely hazardous by the morning of December 29 due to heavy snowfall. Snowfall totaled 8.0 inches at Bridgewater and 6.0 inches 6 miles northwest of Montrose. Wind gusts reached as high as 51 mph near Montrose early on Monday, December 30, resulting in considerable blowing and drifting snow. On December 30, the opening of state offices was delayed until 1300CST.

Some freezing rain fell across south central South Dakota before precipitation turned to snow. As the main trough and area of low pressure organized on Saturday, November 30 across Nebraska and deepened as it moved into southern Wisconsin by the morning of Sunday, December 1, additional snowfall developed and was accompanied by strong and gusty northwest winds of 30 to 50 mph by later Saturday night into Sunday morning.||Snowfall totals over the period generally ranged from 6 to 16 inches west of the James River, to less than one inch from northeast Nebraska and far southeast South Dakota toward southern portions of northwest Iowa. Another mesoscale band of heavy snowfall occurred on Saturday, November 30 near the Minnesota-Iowa border with between 6 and 9 inches accumulation.||This storm continued into December 1, although a majority of accumulating snowfall ended between 0000 and 0900CST. There were a total of 6 injuries (indirect) and one fatality (indirect) during the event. See November 2019 Storm Data episode for more information.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals by early December 1 reached 4.5 inches at Menno. In addition, winds gusted to 45 mph near Tripp, with spotty reduction in visibility below 1 mile in blowing snow. Travel was not recommended by late evening on Saturday, November 30. See the 11/29 Winter Weather event at 1800CST for more details.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep trough across the southwestern U.S. in the early morning of Saturday, December 28 pushed eastward through the day, allowing low pressure to organize near the Oklahoma and Texas Panhandles.  The storm intensified as it moved into eastern Nebraska and western Iowa by 0600CST December 29.  Strong warming aloft ahead of the system led to periods of light rain or light mixed precipitation, while heavy snow organized in the trowal over central and northeast South Dakota. ||Impacts from the storm became significant as the storm closed off in the mid-levels and slowed movement, only reaching southern Minnesota by 0600CST December 30.  Precipitation changed over to snow as colder air collapsed southward, and winds increased to a gusty 40 to 55 mph across southeast South Dakota and adjacent areas.  Snowfall totals ranged from 10 to 18 inches from the James River Valley westward, to 1 to 3 inches across much of northwest Iowa.  The storm resulted in record daily precipitation for Sioux Falls (0.40 inches on December 28 and 0.69 inches on December 29), as well as record daily precipitation (0.64 inches) and snowfall (7.0 inches) for Huron on December 28.||In total, South Dakota State Patrol reported 49 injury accidents and 44 slide-ins across southeast South Dakota during the episode.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light mixed precipitation resulted in a minor glaze of ice accumulation on December 28 before temperatures climbed above freezing. Snowfall developed early on December 29, and continued into December 30 producing hazardous travel. Snowfall totaled 7.5 inches at Marion 5.0 inches 7 miles west southwest of Parker and 3.8 inches at Hurley.  Wind gusts reached as high as 47 mph near Freeman and 46 mph near Davis on Monday, December 30, resulting in blowing and drifting snow.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: With surface temperatures hovering near freezing, light mixed precipitation resulted in a minor glaze of ice accumulation early afternoon on December 28. Snowfall developed mid-afternoon on December 28, and continued heavy at times into early December 30. Interstate 90 was closed west of Mitchell from 1900CST December 28 until 1100CST December 30. Snowfall totaled 8.4 inches at Chamberlain and 8.0 inches 5 miles south southwest of Chamberlain. Snow drifts to several feet were common. On December 30, the opening of state offices was delayed until 1300CST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system brought snow and gusty winds to south central and portions of western South Dakota. Some freezing rain fell across south central South Dakota before precipitation turned to snow. The heaviest snow fell across southwestern into central South Dakota, as well as portions of the northern Black Hills and foothills, where six to 12 inches and locally higher amounts were reported. Gusty winds produced blowing snow and greatly reduced visibility on the South Dakota plains.

A deep low pressure system tracked across the Northern Plains in the days following Thanksgiving, and produced widespread heavy snow across most of central and northeast South Dakota. Areas of freezing drizzle occurred on Thanksgiving Day and on Friday the 29th, bringing some spotty icy conditions. Heavy, wet snow then tracked from south to north across the area from the late afternoon of the 29th persisting into the Saturday the 30th. The snow then ended during the morning hours of December 1st. See November 2019 storm data. ||Northwest winds gusting to 30 to 50 mph caused areas of blowing snow reducing visibilities, affecting holiday travel. Road conditions deteriorated quickly through the event, with many No Travel Advised statements as well as the closure of Interstate-90 from Chamberlain to the Wyoming border Friday night through Sunday morning, December 1st. Several accidents occurred across the region.  By the time the storm let up in the morning hours of December 1st, anywhere from 6 to 17 inches of snow occurred.||Some snowfall amounts include, 7 inches at Clear Lake, Milbank and Mobridge; 8 inches at Pierre and Sisseton; 9 inches at Miller; 10 inches at Roy Lake; 11 inches at Murdo, Faulkton, and Highmore; and 12 inches at Bowdle and Summit.  Locations with more than a foot include, 13 inches at Presho; 14 inches at Kennebec and Gettysburg; 15 inches at Roscoe and Ipswich; 16 inches at McLaughlin and Eureka; and 17 inches at Aberdeen. Aberdeen's 17 inches was 4 inches off the all-time three-day record of 21 inches from November 23rd to the 25th, 1993.

Increased routing of snow melt water from upper portions of the James River Basin in North Dakota and northern South Dakota maintained and even increased river flooding during November.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A continuation of flooding from October, the James River near Mitchell (gage site JRMS2) spent the entire month at minor flood stage, and crested at 2.64 feet above flood stage on November 27. Significant amounts of agricultural land remained flooded. Minor flooding continued at the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Reduced flow through the Missouri River system allowed the river to fall below flood stage early in December.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A continuation of flooding from November, the Missouri River at Niobrara (gage site MRNN1) began the month in minor flood at 0.90 feet above flood stage, then fell below flood stage on December 4. Impacts were minor.
