Reduced flow through the Missouri River system allowed the river to fall below flood stage early in December.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A continuation of flooding from November, the Missouri River at Springfield (gage site SPGS2) began the month in minor flood at 1.14 feet above flood stage, then fell below flood stage on December 4.  Bottom Road near Springfield was flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Flow from upstream continued at high levels through the month of December.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A continuation of flooding from November, the James River near Forestburg (gage site FORS2) spent much of the month in moderate flood, with a brief period from December 10-13 in major flood. A crest of 4.88 feet above flood stage occurred on December 12. Most of the flood plain was inundated, with numerous county and township roads near the river under water for at least a half mile. Minor flooding continued at the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Flow from upstream continued at high levels through the month of December.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The James River near Scotland (gage site SCOS2) spent much of the month in flood, with brief drops below flood stage between December 1-5 and 9-13.  The river crested twice briefly in moderate flood (1.18 feet above flood stage on December 18 and again on December 31 at 1.15 feet above flood stage). Impacts were generally minor outside the growing season, with significant inundation of agricultural lands near the river.

Flooding continued in parts of far northeastern Rapid City, Box Elder, and along Box Elder Creek after heavy rain from a thunderstorm on the evening of May 31. Automated river gauges and public safety officials indicated flooding across most of the area north and northeast of Rapid City in Pennington and far southern Meade Counties. High water levels continued late night into the daytime hours on June 1, with flooding in Box Elder along Box Elder Creek.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: As the flash flooding ended during the evening of May 31, flooding continued on Box Elder Creek from north of Rapid City to Box Elder. Water flooded portions of County Road, North Elk Vale Road, Radar Hill Road, South Ellsworth Road south of Highway 14-16, and Line Road. Several mobile homes on Line Road were evacuated as water surrounded them.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Flooding continued in parts of far northeastern Rapid City, Box Elder, and along Box Elder Creek after heavy rain from a thunderstorm on the evening of May 31. Automated river gauges and public safety officials indicated flooding across most of the area north and northeast of Rapid City in Pennington and far southern Meade Counties. High water levels continued late night into the daytime hours on June 1, with flooding in Box Elder along Box Elder Creek.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: As the flash flooding ended during the evening of May 31, flooding continued on Box Elder Creek north of Rapid City. Water covered Peaceful Pines Road near Erickson Ranch Road, portions of Country Road, parts of Dyess Avenue, and north Elk Vale Road. Flash flooding earlier in the evening also washed out a culvert on Nike Road near Cobalt Road.

The Big Sioux River near Watertown which began the month above flood stage, slowly fell to below flood stage by the middle of the month.

Long term Lake Poinsett flooding which began in the spring remained through November just near to above flood stage. The lake then fell below flood stage in December. Some low lying areas remained flooded along the lake.
Decaying thunderstorms over Todd County produced wind gusts over 60 mph and heat burst conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A heat burst occurred with a decaying storm.

Minor flooding persisted into the beginning of June along parts of the White River, mainly from the Nebraska state line to the Badlands and near the confluence with the Missouri River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from heavy rain over northwestern Nebraska caused minor flooding of the White River from the Nebraska-South Dakota state line to the Badlands. Both river gauges at the state line and near Oglala crested a little over flood stage in late May.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding persisted into the beginning of June along parts of the White River, mainly from the Nebraska state line to the Badlands and near the confluence with the Missouri River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding of agricultural land persisted along the White River around the Highway 47 bridge south of Reliance. After the river crested at 19.0 feet and 30,600 cfs on May 29, the river fell below the flood stage of 15.0 feet early on June 1.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved through the area with an upper level shortwave following it. Snow developed from north to south across the area, with several inches falling across much of western South Dakota. Across portions of the Black Hills, especially the central Black Hills, a heavier band of snow produced over six inches of accumulation. Precipitation started off as a light freezing rain or freezing drizzle in some areas.

An intense winter storm brought heavy snow and strong winds to western and south central South Dakota. Snowfall amounts ranged from 5 to 10 inches across much of far southern South Dakota and the southern Black Hills to 10 to 20 inches across much of the northwest and west central South Dakota plains. Over the northern Black Hills, 15 to 30 inches of snow were recorded. Strong northwest winds over 50 mph produced significant blowing and drifting snow and blizzard conditions on the plains. Many highways were nearly impassible or closed, including Interstate 90.

Long term James River flooding remained throughout Brown and into Spink county throughout November. Although with releases upstream from the Jamestown Dam in North Dakota beginning in mid October, 3 to 5 foot rises occurred at Stratford, Columbia, and Ashton through the end of November. The river stage at Columbia remained steady or fell slightly by the end of November. In fact, at the end of November Stratford had reached its highest stage of just below 19 feet since the flooding began in the spring. Some roads and agricultural land remained flooded. South Dakota Highway 37 north of Hecla at the North Dakota border was closed temporarily in early November due to flooding.

Snow developed from north to south across the area, with several inches falling across much of western South Dakota. Across portions of the Black Hills, especially the central Black Hills, a heavier band of snow produced over six inches of accumulation. Precipitation started off as a light freezing rain or freezing drizzle in some areas.

An intense winter storm brought heavy snow and strong winds to western and south central South Dakota. Snowfall amounts ranged from 5 to 10 inches across much of far southern South Dakota and the southern Black Hills to 10 to 20 inches across much of the northwest and west central South Dakota plains. Over the northern Black Hills, 15 to 30 inches of snow were recorded. Strong northwest winds over 50 mph produced significant blowing and drifting snow and blizzard conditions on the plains. Many highways were nearly impassible or closed, including Interstate 90.

A deep low pressure system tracked across the Northern Plains in the days following Thanksgiving, and produced widespread heavy snow across most of central and northeast South Dakota. Areas of freezing drizzle occurred on Thanksgiving Day and on Friday the 29th, bringing some spotty icy conditions. Heavy, wet snow then tracked from south to north across the area from the late afternoon of the 29th persisting into the Saturday the 30th. The snow then ended during the morning hours of December 1st. See December 2019 storm data. ||Northwest winds gusting to 30 to 50 mph caused areas of blowing snow reducing visibilities, affecting holiday travel. Road conditions deteriorated quickly through the event, with many No Travel Advised statements as well as the closure of Interstate-90 from Chamberlain to the Wyoming border Friday night through Sunday morning. Several accidents occurred across the region.  By the time the storm let up Sunday morning, December 1st, anywhere from 6 to 17 inches of snow occurred.||Some snowfall amounts include, 7 inches at Clear Lake, Milbank and Mobridge; 8 inches at Pierre and Sisseton; 9 inches at Miller; 10 inches at Roy Lake; 11 inches at Murdo, Faulkton, and Highmore; and 12 inches at Bowdle and Summit.  Locations with more than a foot include, 13 inches at Presho; 14 inches at Kennebec and Gettysburg; 15 inches at Roscoe and Ipswich; 16 inches at McLaughlin and Eureka; and 17 inches at Aberdeen. Aberdeen's 17 inches was 4 inches off the all-time three-day record of 21 inches from November 23rd to the 25th, 1993.

A long-lived supercell thunderstorm developed northwest of Rapid City and moved south-southeast along the eastern foothills. The storm produced a long swath of large hail from western and southern portions of Rapid City to near Fairburn. The storm weakened as it moved off the Black Hills east of Buffalo Gap. The largest hail fell from southwest Rapid City to Hermosa and to west of Fairburn.

A severe thunderstorm brought damaging winds to central Tripp County.

A front stretching from west to east across the region moved back and forth as several surface low pressure areas moved along it from July 16th through the 20th. Several periods of severe weather occurred across central and northeast South Dakota during the evening, overnight, and morning hours causing mainly tree damage. ||During the the morning hours of July 19th, storms developed over north central South Dakota and tracked east across northeast South Dakota. The storms produced winds from 60 to 90 mph with reports of trees damaged or toppled, roof damage, along with structural damage in the storms path. North of Gettysburg, two grain bins were blown off their bases with two other bins receiving extensive damage. In Aberdeen, a localized area of intense winds occurred on the southwest side of town, where a store marquee sign was torn down and pieces tossed a block away, framing for new construction was flattened, and significant shingle damage occurred.  Eye witnesses noted some rotation as debris was lofted into the air which may have been from locally enhanced rotating winds from a gustnado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An estimated seventy mph wind downed several large tree branches in Miller along with some trees at the golf course.

The storms produced winds from 60 to 90 mph with reports of trees damaged or toppled, roof damage, along with structural damage in the storms path. North of Gettysburg, two grain bins were blown off their bases with two other bins receiving extensive damage. In Aberdeen, a localized area of intense winds occurred on the southwest side of town, where a store marquee sign was torn down and pieces tossed a block away, framing for new construction was flattened, and significant shingle damage occurred.  Eye witnesses noted some rotation as debris was lofted into the air which may have been from locally enhanced rotating winds from a gustnado.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front pushing through southeast South Dakota during the evening helped to spark a small complex of thunderstorms. These storms organized with deep unidirectional shear into a line, and the presence of dry air off the surface led to the production of a large outflow pool with damaging wind. One storm toward south central South Dakota on the southwest flank of the system briefly took on a supercell structure and produced a brief tornado.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front pushing through southeast South Dakota during the evening helped to spark a small complex of thunderstorms. These storms organized with deep unidirectional shear into a line, and the presence of dry air off the surface led to the production of a large outflow pool with damaging wind. One storm toward south central South Dakota on the southwest flank of the system briefly took on a supercell structure and produced a brief tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts from 60 to 63 mph over the three minute period.

Several periods of severe weather occurred across central and northeast South Dakota during the evening, overnight, and morning hours causing mainly tree damage. ||During the the morning hours of July 19th, storms developed over north central South Dakota and tracked east across northeast South Dakota. The storms produced winds from 60 to 90 mph with reports of trees damaged or toppled, roof damage, along with structural damage in the storms path. North of Gettysburg, two grain bins were blown off their bases with two other bins receiving extensive damage. In Aberdeen, a localized area of intense winds occurred on the southwest side of town, where a store marquee sign was torn down and pieces tossed a block away, framing for new construction was flattened, and significant shingle damage occurred.  Eye witnesses noted some rotation as debris was lofted into the air which may have been from locally enhanced rotating winds from a gustnado.

A strong cold front pushing through southeast South Dakota during the evening helped to spark a small complex of thunderstorms. These storms organized with deep unidirectional shear into a line, and the presence of dry air off the surface led to the production of a large outflow pool with damaging wind. One storm toward south central South Dakota on the southwest flank of the system briefly took on a supercell structure and produced a brief tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Branches down throughout town.

These storms organized with deep unidirectional shear into a line, and the presence of dry air off the surface led to the production of a large outflow pool with damaging wind. EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief tornado developed in an open field north of South Dakota Highway 50, about 5 miles west northwest of Lake Andes.  Witnesses said the tornado remained in open fields and caused no visible damage as it moved toward the east-southeast, lifting three minutes later after traveling just over one mile.

James River flooding, which began in early April, remained across Brown county throughout September as much above normal rainfall occurred. The James River into Spink county at Ashton went below flood stage on the 3rd only to go back above flood stage on September 12th and then remained above flood stage throughout the month and into October.  Several flooded roads along with agricultural land remained.

The ingredients necessary for severe weather including tornadoes came together ahead of a low pressure system in the afternoon and evening hours across northeastern South Dakota. The first tornadoes occurred in Hamlin and Codington counties, while several others followed in Day County a few hours later. While no injuries were reported with this activity, a couple farmsteads did sustain damage. Hail and strong straight-line winds also occurred on occasion with these storms, as did heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado touched down in a field west of 424th Ave, crossed the road and tracked along the northern edge of a farmstead 4 miles north of Bristol. Multiple broken tree branches were noted, with some tossed 50 to 80 yards from the original source. One fiberglass shed was destroyed and tossed about 70 yards. Another outbuilding was lifted off the foundation and set down about 30 yards away. The tornado continued east of the farmstead, crossing two small lakes. The track was clearly shown by cattails flattened in a convergent pattern. The tornado then struck another tree with limbs broken and tossed. The final evidence of the tornado was a downed deer stand.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: James River flooding, which began in early April, remained across Brown county throughout September as much above normal rainfall occurred. The James River into Spink county at Ashton went below flood stage on the 3rd only to go back above flood stage on September 12th and then remained above flood stage throughout the month and into October.  Several flooded roads along with agricultural land remained.

The ingredients necessary for severe weather including tornadoes came together ahead of a low pressure system in the afternoon and evening hours across northeastern South Dakota. The first tornadoes occurred in Hamlin and Codington counties, while several others followed in Day County a few hours later. While no injuries were reported with this activity, a couple farmsteads did sustain damage. Hail and strong straight-line winds also occurred on occasion with these storms, as did heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A witness observed a tornado touch down over a lake and then track onto a pasture before lifting.  No damage occurred.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado started south of Thomas and tracked northeast across farm fields. Trees and outdoor lawn items were damaged after the tornado crossed Highway 22 and before crossing Clear Lake.  The tornado moved off the lake, crossed a cornfield and proceeded to damage several metal beams on a quonset barn. Finally, several outbuildings and a silo were damaged on a farm before additional trees were uprooted in a shelter belt.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong and rare winter storm brought heavy wet snow along with an initial period of  heavy sleet to central and northeast South Dakota. Snowfall amounts ranged from 6 to 13 inches with sleet amounts of 1 to 2 inches. Strong northwest winds of 25 to 35 mph with gusts to 40 to 50 mph did bring some blowing snow creating lower visibility along with drifting snow. Travel was significantly disrupted or halted with a few accidents occurring. Many schools were closed and events were delayed or cancelled. The early heavy snow greatly affected harvest along with damaging some of the crops. ||Snowfall amounts include, 6 inches at Mobridge, Summit, and Britton; 7 inches at Andover, Onaka, Timber Lake, Mclaughlin, Highmore, Selby, and Fort Pierre; 8 inches near Claremont, Artas, north of Pierre, east of Pierpont, and Murdo; 9 inches at Lake City; 10 inches southeast of Herreid, Gettysburg, and Pollock, and 11 inches at Roscoe and Bowdle. Locations with a foot or more include, 12 inches at Eureka and northeast of Java, and 13 inches northwest of Onida.

Strong thunderstorms with heavy rainfall on already saturated ground caused flash flooding on the Pennington County plains from east of New Underwood to Badlands National Park.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding occurred on Sage Creek in Badlands National Park. Water inundated Sage Creek Road and flooded Sage Creek Campground. Flash flooding compromised a culvert under the railroad tracks about five miles east of New Underwood, which derailed a Rapid City, Pierre, & Eastern train. Two railroad employees in the partially submerged locomotive were injured.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm developed along an outflow-reinforced boundary during the late evening. This storm was located in an environment with increased shear compared to earlier evening storms, along with significant DCAPE.

Nearly stationary thunderstorms developed over and near Custer during the early afternoon. Radar estimated six to seven inches of rain fell west of Custer, with measured observations as much as 5.35 inches. Runoff moved downstream along French Creek, causing severe flooding in the town of Custer through the evening. Road closures and some evacuations were reported by the Custer County Sheriff's office before water began to recede.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant flooding occurred on French Creek from the Custer Limestone area downstream to Stockade Lake, including the City of Custer. Several smaller tributaries into French Creek, including Ruby Creek, also flooded. Flash flooding inundated Custer Limestone Road just east of the intersection of Forest Service 284 and Forest Service 285, French Creek Road at Hoover Court, Highway 16, Highway 16A, Highway 89, Custer Street, South 4th Street, French Creek Drive, Gordon Street, 7th Street, 8th Street, Washington Street, Sidney Park, Granite Valley Drive, and Iron Creek Road. Several homes and businesses were also flooded.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from two to three inches of rain caused flash flooding around Wewela. A few roads, including 328th Avenue, 331st Avenue, and 229th Street were covered with water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm stalled over east central Oglala Lakota County, producing locally large hail and very heavy rainfall. Runoff from three to five inches of rain caused flash flooding across parts of eastern Oglala Lakota County into western Bennett County.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain fell over Iron Cloud Canyon, causing flash flooding along Porcupine Creek. Several feet of fast moving water inundated BIA 27 between Evergreen and Goose Neck Road.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding occurred in Yellow Bear Canyon. Water inundated BIA 4 for a couple of hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm stalled over east central Oglala Lakota County, producing locally large hail and very heavy rainfall. Runoff from three to five inches of rain caused flash flooding across parts of eastern Oglala Lakota County into western Bennett County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff caused flooding along Porcupine Creek downstream to the White River. BIA 2 east of Sharps Corner was inundated by the morning of August 12. Flooding also occurred on Three Mile Creek and American Horse Creek; which stranded livestock, damaged corrals, and flooded several barns and outbuildings.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: March and April snowmelt and heavy rain flooding along with above normal May precipitation resulted in hundreds of thousands of acres of crops damaged or delayed/unplanted across central and northeast South Dakota. The widespread flooding continued into June with millions of dollars of loss.

March and April snowmelt and heavy rain flooding along with above normal May precipitation resulted in hundreds of thousands of acres of crops damaged or delayed/unplanted across central and northeast South Dakota. The widespread flooding continued into June with millions of dollars of loss.

A historic flooding event unfolded as heavy rainfall drenched the area on March 13-14, 2019.  With frozen and impervious ground, full and rapid runoff was maximized. In addition to the rainfall, between 1 and 3 inches of snow water equivalent remained in the snow pack south of Interstate 90, with 3 to 6 inches of snow water equivalent north of Interstate 90. Fifteen river gage sites measured record stages around the area.  Winds gusted to 50 to 70 mph at times, and colder air filtering into the system at the end of the storm produced a light  accumulation of ice west of the James River and from 1 to 4 inches of snowfall in parts of southeast South Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow melt and heavy rainfall (measured 2.97 inches 11 miles south of Emery and 2.79 inches at Menno) resulted in extensive flooding of agricultural lands and inundation of numerous roads (including state highway 44 south of Milltown).  A few farm buildings along the river stretch were also flooded.  The James River at Scotland (gage site SCOS2) reached major flood stage cresting 5.95 feet above flood stage on March 15.  A secondary crest of 5.51 feet above flood stage occurred on March 26. Major flooding continued at the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Spurred by a period of excessive precipitation from September 10-12 that resulted in 5 to 10 inches of rainfall in the middle to upper reaches of the Big Sioux River Basin and 2 to 5 inches from Sioux Falls downstream, extreme rises occurred mid-month, with many basin locations reaching record stages.  Recreational usage of the Big Sioux River, Split Rock Creek and Skunk Creek (in Moody, Minnehaha, Lincoln and Union counties) was banned for much of the remainder of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: After excessive two-day rainfall during September 10-12 (6.71 inches 11 miles north of Colman, 8.82 inches at Flandreau), the Big Sioux River above Flandreau (gage site BSFS2) reached the highest stage on record of 5.29 feet above flood stage on September 13. Water inundated Flandreau Park as well as numerous county and township roads north of Flandreau.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Continued high dam releases, along with another period of very heavy rainfall in the middle of the month, maintained river flooding along much of the Missouri River between Fort Randall and Gavins Point Dams.  Three to eight inches of rainfall was common between September 10 and 12 in basins above Gavins Point Dam.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A continuation of flooding from August, the start of September was spent in minor flood stage. The Missouri River near Verdel (gage site MRVN1) crested 0.58 feet above flood stage on September 11. The excessive precipitation of September 10-12 resulted in a decrease in releases from Fort Randall Dam, which rapidly brought the river below flood stage by September 15. Impacts were mainly limited to inundation of agricultural land, as well as closing of numerous recreational boat ramps.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Spurred by a period of excessive precipitation that saw from 4 to 9 inches of rainfall between September 10-12, the James River basin, which was in flood to start the month, saw extreme rises mid-month. Many lower basin locations reached record stages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A continuation of flooding from August; after falling briefly below flood stage on September 10, the James River near Scotland (gage site SCOS2) responded to excessive rainfall of 4 to 8 inches upstream September 10-12 (7.54 inches at Mitchell and 7.21 inches 7 miles south of Parkston) to crest at a record 9.24 feet above flood stage on September 13. Innumerable roads were inundated and closed for several days, including South Dakota Highway 44 near Milltown, Parkston and Freeman, Highway 18 near Olivet, Highway 81 north of Yankton, Highway 46 west of Irene and 431st Avenue in northern Yankton county - all major area roadways. Tschetter Hutterite Colony was inundated when water overtopped protective levies, causing extensive damage to buildings and displacing 10 families.  Similarly, water inundated Wolf Creek Colony when an earthen dam was breached displacing 20 families. Numerous turkeys and hogs were lost in the flooding.  Elsewhere, extensive amounts of agricultural land were inundated. Moderate flooding continued at the end of the month.

Innumerable roads were inundated and closed for several days, including South Dakota Highway 44 near Milltown, Parkston and Freeman, Highway 18 near Olivet, Highway 81 north of Yankton, Highway 46 west of Irene and 431st Avenue in northern Yankton county - all major area roadways. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Spurred by a period of excessive precipitation that saw from 4 to 9 inches of rainfall between September 10-12, the James River basin, which was in flood to start the month, saw extreme rises mid-month. Many lower basin locations reached record stages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A continuation of flooding from August, the James River near Mitchell (gage site JRMS2) responded sharply to 5 to 8 inches of rainfall between September 10-12 (7.54 inches at Mitchell) to reach the 4th highest crest on record at 8.05 feet above flood stage on September 13. Innumerable county and township roads near the river were inundated, with significant flooding of South Dakota Highway 37 east of Mitchell and 42 east of Ethan. Significant amounts of agricultural land remained flooded. Moderate flooding continued at the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Continued high dam releases, along with another period of very heavy rainfall in the middle of the month, maintained river flooding along much of the Missouri River between Fort Randall and Gavins Point Dams.  Three to eight inches of rainfall was common between September 10 and 12 in basins above Gavins Point Dam.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A continuation of flooding from August, the start of September was spent in minor flood stage. The Missouri River near Verdel (gage site MRVN1) crested 0.58 feet above flood stage on September 11. The excessive precipitation of September 10-12 resulted in a decrease in releases from Fort Randall Dam, which rapidly brought the river below flood stage by September 15. Impacts were mainly limited to inundation of agricultural land, as well as closing of numerous recreational boat ramps.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Spurred by a period of excessive precipitation that saw from 4 to 9 inches of rainfall between September 10-12, the James River basin, which was in flood to start the month, saw extreme rises mid-month. Many lower basin locations reached record stages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A continuation of flooding from August, the James River near Mitchell (gage site JRMS2) responded sharply to 5 to 8 inches of rainfall between September 10-12 (7.54 inches at Mitchell) to reach the 4th highest crest on record at 8.05 feet above flood stage on September 13. Innumerable county and township roads near the river were inundated, with significant flooding of South Dakota Highway 37 east of Mitchell and 42 east of Ethan. Significant amounts of agricultural land remained flooded. Moderate flooding continued at the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Spurred by a period of excessive precipitation from September 10-12 that resulted in 5 to 10 inches of rainfall in the middle to upper reaches of the Big Sioux River Basin and 2 to 5 inches from Sioux Falls downstream, extreme rises occurred mid-month, with many basin locations reaching record stages.  Recreational usage of the Big Sioux River, Split Rock Creek and Skunk Creek (in Moody, Minnehaha, Lincoln and Union counties) was banned for much of the remainder of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: After excessive two-day rainfall during September 10-12 (8.93 inches at Flandreau and 8.64 inches near Pipestone), Flandreau Creek near Flandreau (gage site FCRS2) reached the highest stage on record of 5.46 feet above flood stage on September 12. 487th Avenue at the gage site and numerous other township roads were inundated by water, as well as the Flandreau Golf Club.  Significant amounts of agricultural land were also flooded.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A continuation of flooding from August; after falling briefly below flood stage on September 10, the James River near Scotland (gage site SCOS2) responded to excessive rainfall of 4 to 8 inches upstream September 10-12 (7.54 inches at Mitchell and 7.21 inches 7 miles south of Parkston) to crest at a record 9.24 feet above flood stage on September 13. Innumerable roads were inundated and closed for several days, including South Dakota Highway 44 near Milltown, Parkston and Freeman, Highway 18 near Olivet, Highway 81 north of Yankton, Highway 46 west of Irene and 431st Avenue in northern Yankton county - all major area roadways. Extensive amounts of agricultural land were inundated. Moderate flooding continued at the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Spurred by a period of excessive precipitation that saw from 4 to 9 inches of rainfall between September 10-12, the James River basin, which was in flood to start the month, saw extreme rises mid-month. Many lower basin locations reached record stages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding continued from August, but the James River near Yankton (YNNS2) soon fell below flood stage on September 4.  However, excessive rainfall of 4 to 8 inches upstream September 10-12 (7.54 inches at Mitchell and 7.21 inches 7 miles south of Parkston) led to a rapid rise and a record crest 15.1 feet above flood stage on September 14.  Five of the six bridges crossing the James River in Yankton county were closed. Innumerable roads were inundated and closed for several days, including major roadways of Highway 81 north of Yankton and Highway 46 west of Irene. Extensive amounts of agricultural land were inundated.  Fifty-five homes were impacted by flood waters in Yankton county, with residents forced to take temporary shelter until floodwaters receded. Minor flooding continued at the end of the month.

The Missouri River at Niobrara (gage site MRNN1) crested 5.34 feet above flood stage on September 22, which was the second highest crest on record. High water closed numerous recreational boat ramps. The river remained at major flood stage at the end of the month.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A continuation of flooding from August, the Missouri River at Springfield (gage site SPGS2) crested at 2.41 feet above flood stage on September 21. High water closed numerous recreational boat ramps, and some camping areas at Springfield Recreational Area. The river remained at moderate flood stage at the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A nearly stationary boundary again focused widespread heavy rainfall during the late evening on September 11 and early morning hours of September 12.  Precipitable water was at or exceeding the record values for the date at nearby locations.  As the low-level jet increased during the evening over the outflow pool in the wake of earlier severe convection, training of convection initiated across areas which had received several inches of rainfall during the previous night.  Even short duration intense rainfall produced areas of flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous homes and businesses were inundated by flood water in Mitchell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Continued high dam releases, along with another period of very heavy rainfall in the middle of the month, pushed a small section of the lower Missouri River into flood late month. Three to eight inches of rainfall was common  through the James and Vermillion River Basins between September 10 and 12, which flowed into the unrestricted Missouri River below Gavins Point Dam. Areas of the Missouri River below Ponca State Park were placed in a no-wake zone through the end of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from heavy rainfall (4.59 inches at Hurley, 4.24 inches at Menno, and 8.14 inches at Clayton) brought the Missouri River at Ponca (gage site MRPN1) to a crest 3.06 feet above flood stage on September 15.  The river remained in minor flood stage at the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A couple of post-frontal thunderstorms developed as a frontal boundary stalled just south of the Nebraska border, and an upper-level wave approached from the west.  Elevated instability of 1000-1500 J/kg was available in a lesser-sheared environment, enough to allow for isolated stronger updrafts and small to marginally large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail, mostly dime-sized, covered roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Spurred by a period of excessive precipitation from September 10-12 that resulted in 5 to 10 inches of rainfall in the upper reaches of the Big Sioux River Basin and 2 to 5 inches from Sioux Falls southward, rises occurred mid-month that routed downstream to cause minor flooding in the lowest sections of the basin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: After excessive two-day rainfall during September 10-12 (generally 3 to 4 inches around Sioux Falls, 4.72 inches at Canton, 2.51 inches at Hawarden), the Big Sioux River at Sioux City (gage site SXCI4) reached the 3rd highest stage on record of 0.81 feet above flood stage on September 19. Impacts were mainly limited to inundation of agricultural land.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Despite a dry period in late September with less than a quarter inch on average across the Missouri River Basin, flooding returned to areas below Fort Randall Dam late in the month with increased releases.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: As releases from Fort Randall Dam increased following the mid-month rainfall, the Missouri River near Greenwood (gage site GRWS2) rose sharply into major flood stage and crested 2.47 feet above flood stage on September 30. Impacts were mainly limited to inundation of agricultural land, as well as the closing of numerous recreational boat ramps. The river remained in major flood at the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Spurred by a period of excessive precipitation from September 10-12 that resulted in 5 to 10 inches of rainfall in the middle to upper reaches of the Big Sioux River Basin and 2 to 5 inches from Sioux Falls downstream, extreme rises occurred mid-month, with many basin locations reaching record stages.  Recreational usage of the Big Sioux River, Split Rock Creek and Skunk Creek (in Moody, Minnehaha, Lincoln and Union counties) was banned for much of the remainder of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: After excessive two-day rainfall during September 10-12 (8.74 inches at Flandreau, 7.81 inches at Dell Rapids), the Big Sioux River above Dell Rapids (gage site DBRS2) reached the highest stage on record of 9.00 feet above flood stage on September 13. Extensive flooding of homes occurred in Dell Rapids, some as far as three city blocks from the river. A dozen homes were deemed to have substantial damage, and removed after being flooded.  Jasper Road east of Dell Rapids was closed due to water over the roadway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal boundary provided focus for convective development across south central South Dakota during the very late afternoon and early evening as a strong impulse moved in the southwest flow aloft.  Initial discrete cells produced spotty hail and damaging wind, with MLCAPE values around 1500-2000 J/kg and 45-55 knots of deep-layer shear.  As cells spread eastward and developed into a line, the increase in low-level jet during the evening quickly increased the 0-3 km shear to 35-40 knots.  Three QLCS tornadoes occurred within the city limits of Sioux Falls.  In addition to the tornadoes, widespread wind damage to trees and spottier damage to residences were reported across much of the city.  A total of nine people were injured in the storms around Sioux Falls.  Repeated cells resulted in flash flooding by later evening and into the overnight hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees down, with one onto a residence causing damage to roof and siding.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: As releases from Fort Randall Dam increased following the mid-month rainfall, the Missouri River near Verdel (gage site MRVN1) rose sharply into moderate flood stage and crested 1.17 feet above flood stage on September 21. High water levels led to the closing of many recreational boat ramps. The river remained in moderate flood at the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A nearly stationary boundary again focused widespread heavy rainfall during the late evening on September 11 and early morning hours of September 12.  Precipitable water was at or exceeding the record values for the date at nearby locations.  As the low-level jet increased during the evening over the outflow pool in the wake of earlier severe convection, training of convection initiated across areas which had received several inches of rainfall during the previous night.  Even short duration intense rainfall produced areas of flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: State Highway 34 was under a foot or two of flowing water both west and east of Madison. Several homes were evacuated due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal boundary provided focus for convective development across south central South Dakota during the very late afternoon and early evening as a strong impulse moved in the southwest flow aloft.  Initial discrete cells produced spotty hail and damaging wind, with MLCAPE values around 1500-2000 J/kg and 45-55 knots of deep-layer shear.  As cells spread eastward and developed into a line, the increase in low-level jet during the evening quickly increased the 0-3 km shear to 35-40 knots.  Three QLCS tornadoes occurred within the city limits of Sioux Falls.  In addition to the tornadoes, widespread wind damage to trees and spottier damage to residences were reported across much of the city.  A total of nine people were injured in the storms around Sioux Falls.  Repeated cells resulted in flash flooding by later evening and into the overnight hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced hail to quarter size in the Sturgis area. One to two inches of heavy rain over saturated ground caused flooding across parts of eastern Lawrence County into the Sturgis area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding continued across the area from heavy rainfall. Runoff from additional rainfall during the afternoon and evening of July 5 kept creek levels high into the morning hours on July 6.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extensive flooding occurred along Bear Butte Creek in Boulder Canyon through Sturgis to Fort Meade. Highway 34 was covered with water at the S-curve between Sturgis and Fort Meade, the Sturgis city park was completely underwater, and the 7th Street bridge at Bear Butte Creek was inundated. The Sly Street crossing, West Woodland Drive, and Blanche Street were damaged, as well as almost all the other crossings over Bear Butte Creek in Sturgis.

A frontal boundary provided focus for convective development across south central South Dakota during the very late afternoon and early evening as a strong impulse moved in the southwest flow aloft.  Initial discrete cells produced spotty hail and damaging wind, with MLCAPE values around 1500-2000 J/kg and 45-55 knots of deep-layer shear.  As cells spread eastward and developed into a line, the increase in low-level jet during the evening quickly increased the 0-3 km shear to 35-40 knots.  Three QLCS tornadoes occurred within the city limits of Sioux Falls.  In addition to the tornadoes, widespread wind damage to trees and spottier damage to residences were reported across much of the city.  A total of nine people were injured in the storms around Sioux Falls.  Repeated cells resulted in flash flooding by later evening and into the overnight hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Medium size ash tree broken off by thunderstorm winds.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from heavy rain destroyed several camping sites at the Boxelder Forks Campground and washed out Forest Service Road 140, Butcher Gulch Road/Forest Service Road 172, and Boxelder Creek Road in multiple locations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Runoff from heavy rainfall caused flooding across parts of southern Fall River County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from heavy rainfall caused flooding along Hat Creek and its smaller tributaries, Duck Creek, and Plains Creek. The flooding compromised a culvert on Highway 71 about 4 miles north of Ardmore and a county road bridge just east of Ardmore was washed away.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Runoff from heavy rainfall over the last few days caused Boxelder Creek to flood from around Black Hawk to northwest Rapid City.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding occurred along Boxelder Creek from near Black Hawk to northwest Rapid City.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter observed the rapid spin up of a tornado to the west of his location 5 miles south of Avon, just north of 307th Street and west of 405th Avenue. The tornado tracked northeast, and moved into a farmstead as it crossed 306th Street. Roof panels were blown off a farm outbuilding, with doors on the building bowed inward. Several trees on the farmstead suffered damage to large branches.  The tornado continued to track northeast for around another half mile before lifting.  Average path width was 20 yards.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Dense fog contributed to a fatal two vehicle accident which occurred four miles west of Aberdeen around 8 am on Highway 12. A teenage boy was pulling onto Highway 12 in dense fog when his car was struck by a truck traveling east. The teenager died at the hospital from the injuries he sustained. The other vehicles' driver sustained serious non-life threatening injuries.

A large, long-lived supercell thunderstorm developed over Oglala Lakota County and tracked slowly eastward across southern South Dakota through the late afternoon and early evening. The storm produced a large swath of large hail and strong winds, eventually producing mostly strong winds as it moved across far south central South Dakota. Brief tornado touchdowns were reported over Bennett County, but did not cause damage. Heavy rain also produced flooding in some areas of Oglala Lakota and Bennett Counties.

With liquid equivalent precipitation of 2 to 3 inches over much of southeast South Dakota during the first two weeks of April, many rivers and creeks experienced a rise back into flood stage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from occasional precipitation in early April returned flooding to city parks near the Big Sioux River in Sioux Falls. The Big Sioux River at Sioux Falls Western Avenue (gage site WAVS2) crested at 0.30 feet above flood stage on April 16.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High water levels persisted through April, with continued damage to road and drainage infrastructure.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Continued precipitation with high water levels damaged county and township roadways during April.  260 miles of gravel roads were reported completely unusable at times during the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Runoff from significant early spring precipitation during the first couple of weeks of April led to flooding along portions of the Vermillion River.  Much of the basin experienced as much as 3 to 4 inches of liquid equivalent precipitation through April 14.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from occasional precipitation in late March and early April produced considerable flooding of agricultural lands along the river between Davis and Vermillion, along with some brief flooding of South Dakota Highway 19 south of Centerville as well as several other county and township roads. The Vermillion River near Wakonda (gage site WKAS2) crested at 3.10 feet above flood stage on April 19.

A second crest in moderate flood stage occurred on April 17 at 3.79 feet above flood stage.  Minor flooding continued at the end of the month.

There was significant structural damage to the roof.  The organ soundboard, computer server, and video system were disabled by the lightning strike.

A direct fatality to a 22 year-old male resulted on April 3 when his vehicle entered flood waters nearly 7 feet deep in a field near the James River bridge on 250th Street northeast of Mitchell.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from occasional precipitation in late March and April maintained major flooding of county and township roads near the James River throughout Beadle county. The James River at Huron (gage site HURS2) crested at 6.44 feet above flood stage on April 25, the sixth highest crest on record. Major flooding continued at the end of the month.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from occasional precipitation in late March and April maintained extensive flooding throughout the flood plain. Highway 44 south of Milltown was flooded, along with numerous county and township roads. The James River near Scotland (gage site SCOS2) crested at 5.79 feet above flood stage on April 18, the ninth highest crest on record. Major flooding continued at the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Runoff from significant early spring precipitation during the first couple of weeks of April maintained flooding along portions of the James River. Much of the basin experienced as much as 1 to 3 inches of liquid equivalent precipitation through April 14.  A direct fatality to a 22 year-old male resulted on April 3 when his vehicle entered flood waters nearly 7 feet deep in a field near the James River bridge on 250th Street northeast of Mitchell.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from occasional precipitation in late March and April maintained considerable flooding throughout the flood plain across Davison county. 416th Avenue near Rosedale Colony and 257th Street near the gage site were impacted by water. The James River at Mitchell (gage site JRMS2) crested at 6.30 feet above flood stage on April 22, the sixth highest crest on record. Major flooding continued at the end of the month.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from occasional precipitation in late March and April maintained major flooding of agricultural land as well as county and township roads near the James River throughout Yankton county. The James River near Yankton (gage site YNNS2) crested at 8.43 feet above flood stage on April 18, the eighth highest crest on record. Major flooding continued at the end of the month.
